banks_financial_institutions: Trinidad and Tobago Mortgage Finance Company Limited (TTMF)
social_headline: Judge strikes out TTMF foreclosure, calling the mortgage deed 'invalid... perhaps fraudulent'
social_post: A foreclosure action by the Trinidad and Tobago Mortgage Finance Company has been thrown out, with the High Court branding the underlying mortgage deed invalid and 'perhaps fraudulent'. TTMF sued Annette Young for over $1.2 million and possession of her Arima home at The Crossings, Santa Rosa West. But the 2017 deed of mortgage had been created by attaching the old 2010 signature pages to an 'updated' deed, and TTMF's senior counsel ultimately conceded it was invalid. When TTMF tried to expand its claim with new causes of action without amending the claim form, Justice Carol Gobin struck the whole action out as an abuse of process and ordered TTMF to pay the borrower's costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A claim form, not the statement of case, defines the reliefs sought; a statement of case cannot smuggle in new causes of action without a proper amendment
The overriding objective does not let a court ignore plain rules of procedure
Prosecuting a claim on a deed known to be invalid or improperly produced can itself be an abuse of process
outcome: The mortgagee action struck out as an abuse of process. The expanded reliefs (enforceability of the 2009 loan agreement, equitable mortgage, restitution, the larger $1,363,534.84 sum) struck out; the deed of mortgage held invalid/fraudulent. Claimant to pay the defendant's costs on the prescribed scale up to trial.
osint_notes: Foreclosure/mortgage dispute naming a major state-linked lender (TTMF) and a borrower, with an identified Arima property and large loan figures. The court found the mortgage deed invalid/fraudulent (no charges). Lending/property data of OSINT value.

individuals.notes: English barrister; entered a November 2017 cooperation agreement with the T&T Government to give a notarised statement about a corrupt scheme; later pleaded guilty and was fined

individuals.name: Faris Al Rawi SC
individuals.notes: Signed the Agreement for the Government; alleged to have breached the undertaking to recommend non-prosecution

individuals.name: Stuart Young
individuals.role: then Minister in the Ministry of the Attorney General
individuals.notes: Alleged to have breached confidentiality by disclosing the Notarised Statement to the UK National Crime Agency

criminal_individuals.name: Vincent Nelson KC
offences: corruption-related offences (paying kickbacks out of government legal fees)
criminal_individuals.notes: Pleaded guilty in 2019 before Holdip J; fined TTD$2.25 million on 2 March 2020 for participating in an arrangement paying a portion of government legal fees as kickbacks to Gerald Ramdeen for Anand Ramlogan's benefit

criminal_individuals.name: Anand Ramlogan SC
criminal_individuals.notes: Former Attorney General; alleged beneficiary of the kickback scheme disclosed in Nelson's notarised statement; subject of related criminal proceedings

criminal_individuals.name: Gerald Ramdeen
criminal_individuals.notes: Attorney-at-law alleged to have received kickbacks from government legal fees for Ramlogan's benefit; subject of related criminal proceedings

financial_figures.what_it_is: Criminal fine imposed on Vincent Nelson KC in March 2020 (claim to recover it later withdrawn)

related_litigation: Criminal proceedings against Anand Ramlogan and Gerald Ramdeen arising from the disclosed scheme
Nelson's 2019 guilty plea before Holdip J
social_headline: State whistle-blower barrister loses bid to make T&T pay for his corruption fallout
social_post: The High Court has dismissed a damages claim by English barrister Vincent Nelson KC against the Trinidad and Tobago Government. In 2017 Nelson struck a deal with then-AG Faris Al Rawi to give a notarised statement exposing a legal-fee kickback scheme allegedly benefiting former Attorney General Anand Ramlogan and attorney Gerald Ramdeen. Nelson later pleaded guilty himself and was fined $2.25 million. He sued, claiming the State breached confidentiality (by sharing his statement with the UK's National Crime Agency) and failed to recommend he not be prosecuted. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC found one breach but held his losses flowed from his own conviction and reputation, not the breach, and fell outside the agreement's indemnity. Claim dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Damages for breach of contract must be caused by the breach, not by independent factors such as the claimant's own conviction
An indemnity is construed strictly; it covers only the losses within its scope
Courts are reluctant to void freely-made agreements on public-policy grounds, especially where the State contracts to assist prosecuting crime
outcome: Claim dismissed. Although the Government breached the confidentiality clause, the claimant's losses (loss of practice, earnings, insurance discount) flowed from his own prosecution, guilty plea and conviction rather than the breach, and fell outside the indemnity in clause 5.
osint_notes: High-profile public-corruption matter naming a former Attorney General (Anand Ramlogan), an attorney (Gerald Ramdeen), a sitting/former minister (Stuart Young), ex-AG Faris Al Rawi and a convicted English KC (Vincent Nelson). Concerns a government legal-fee kickback scheme; strong crime-intelligence and political-network value.

property_lease.address_or_description: 15-acre parcel in the Wards of Tamana and Pointe-a-Pierre (Certificate of Title Vol 2671 Folio 477) and a 10-acre parcel in the Ward of Turure (Certificate of Title Vol 4020 Folio 123)

property_lease.outcome: Challenge to the 2000 will and consequential transfers dismissed; the disputed devise stands

related_litigation: 1987 proceedings by Dowling Thomas and Juanita Thomas against Veronica De Gale (compromised by 1996 consent order)
social_headline: Daughter's 18-year-late challenge to a 'stranger's' will over 25 acres fails
social_post: A daughter's bid to overturn her late mother's will and recover 25 acres of land has failed because she waited too long with too little evidence. Veronica De Gale died in 2001 leaving a 1983 will favouring her children and a disputed 2000 will that instead left her 15-acre and 10-acre parcels to two men she allegedly didn't know. Her daughter Elizabeth Pirtam claimed the 2000 will was a forgery and the signature wasn't her mother's. But Justice Joan Charles found the handwriting expert relied on just four sample signatures - too few to prove forgery - and Pirtam had sat on her rights for 18 years while executors and witnesses died. The claim was dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Allegations of fraud or forgery in a will must be distinctly pleaded and proved with cogent evidence
Handwriting comparisons based on too few sample signatures are unlikely to establish forgery on the balance of probabilities
Long delay in challenging a probated will weakens a claim, especially once witnesses and executors have died
outcome: Claim dismissed. The court declined to revoke the 2000 will or set aside the consequential land transfers; forgery and lack of testamentary capacity were not proven and the 18-year delay counted against the claimant. Claimant to pay the defendants' costs.
osint_notes: Probate/land dispute over two identified parcels (25 acres in Tamana, Pointe-a-Pierre and Turure) with certificate-of-title references and a long litigation history. Property/estate data of OSINT value; alleged forgery not proven, no charges.

property_lease.lease_terms: 25-year commercial lease to Kenneth and Lloyd Ramcharan from 1 June 1993, expired 31 May 2018; thereafter a month-to-month tenancy at $6,667; negotiations for a new 25-year lease at $190,000/year failed over the commencement date
property_lease.outcome: Tenancy validly terminated by Notice to Quit; possession ordered by 9 July 2026; mesne profits and costs awarded; tenant's lease/estoppel counterclaim dismissed

related_litigation: Anglican Church Diocese v Jimison Sinanan (TT 2024 HC 304) - holding-over principles applied
social_headline: Anglican Diocese evicts long-time Arima tenant after lease talks stalled on a back-dated rent
social_post: The Anglican Church has won possession of its commercial property at No. 6 Farfan Street, Arima, which the Ramcharan family had occupied under lease since 1947. After the 25-year lease expired in 2018, the parties negotiated a new lease at $190,000 a year but never agreed when the higher rent would start - the tenants refused to backdate it to 2018. Meanwhile they kept paying the old $6,667 rent, which the Diocese returned. Justice Westmin James held there was only a month-to-month tenancy, validly ended by a Notice to Quit, and that tendering rejected rent cannot force a tenancy on a landlord. The estate must vacate by July 2026 and pay $320,888 in mesne profits plus costs; its counterclaim for a new lease was dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A binding agreement for a lease requires unqualified agreement on all essential terms, including the commencement date
A tenant cannot revive or impose a tenancy by paying rent the landlord actively rejects and returns
After a valid Notice to Quit, a holding-over occupier becomes a trespasser liable for mesne profits at market rate
outcome: Judgment for the claimant. Month-to-month tenancy validly terminated by Notice to Quit; defendant to deliver up possession by 9 July 2026 and pay mesne profits of $320,888.84 plus interest and $54,266.93 costs. Counterclaim for a new 25-year lease/estoppel dismissed with $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Commercial landlord-tenant dispute naming the Anglican Diocese (a major institutional landowner) and an identified Arima property, with detailed rent and mesne-profit figures. Property/corporate data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

social_headline: Insurer COLFIRE shares blame after seven-month delay sending an adjuster to a crash
social_post: A Tobago motor-accident case has spread the blame three ways. Keith Louis was overtaking a stopped car on Milford Road when Melissa Louis pulled out of a minor street and hit him. Justice Joan Charles found both drivers careless, apportioning 70% of the fault to Melissa Louis and 30% to Keith. But the judge also faulted insurer COLFIRE, which took until August 2020 - some seven months after the January crash - to send an adjuster, leaving the claimant's 1997 Lexus stuck at the repair shop racking up storage and rental costs. He was awarded about $90,000 in total: reduced repair costs, storage fees and three months' car rental. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Overtaking near a T-junction without slowing, and emerging from a minor road without stopping, can both amount to negligence (here split 70/30)
Damages for an older vehicle are limited; courts may discount the cost of new parts and apply the liability split
An insurer that delays inspecting a vehicle can be liable for the storage and loss-of-use costs that accrue
outcome: Liability apportioned 70% to the first defendant and 30% to the claimant. Defendants ordered to pay $90,410.70 total special damages (repairs $50,248.20, storage $21,262.50, three months' rental $18,900), plus prescribed costs and 3% interest.
osint_notes: Motor-accident negligence claim naming a major insurer (COLFIRE), a repair shop and an adjuster firm, with detailed damages figures. Commercial/insurer data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

social_headline: Soca star Patrice Roberts must pay ex-manager ~US$25k after fee dispute - but wins escrow back
social_post: A management-fee fight between Soca artiste Patrice Roberts and her former Canadian management company, Soca Bookings Incorporated, has been settled in the High Court. The company - which had secured her master rights and booked international tours from 2015 until she terminated the deal in 2017 - sued for over US$42,000 in unpaid fees and advances. Justice Robin Mohammed awarded it US$22,535 in fees and US$8,200 in advances, but also upheld Roberts' counterclaim for US$9,000 in digital-sales money the company held in escrow. After a recommended set-off, Roberts owes about US$25,104 plus costs. Roberts had previously been managed by Machel Montano's HD Productions. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Where an oral management contract's terms are uncertain, fees may be recovered on a quantum meruit (reasonable value) basis
Money held in escrow on an artiste's behalf (e.g. digital-sales proceeds) remains the artiste's and can be recovered on counterclaim
Cross-judgments for both parties can be netted by set-off into a single balance
outcome: Judgment for both sides. Claimant awarded US$22,535 fees (quantum meruit) and US$8,200 advances plus interest and TT$45,021.82 costs; defendant awarded US$9,000 escrowed digital-sales proceeds plus interest and TT$18,038.11 costs. After set-off, the defendant pays the claimant about US$25,104.12 and TT$26,983.71.
osint_notes: Music-industry commercial dispute naming a Canadian artiste-management company (Soca Bookings Inc, with directors), a well-known Soca artiste (Patrice Roberts) and her prior management (Machel Montano/HD Productions), with detailed USD/TTD figures. Strong commercial-network and financial OSINT value.

title: Hearn v Her Worship Magistrate Ms. Rehana Ali
court: Court of Appeal, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: Her Worship Magistrate Ms. Rehana Ali (Respondent)

individuals.name: Magistrate Rehana Ali
individuals.notes: Granted the prosecution an adjournment in Hearn's DUI trial; the Court held magistrates should not be named as parties to judicial review of their decisions

criminal_individuals.name: Stefan Hearn
offences: driving with breath-alcohol exceeding the prescribed limit (s.70A(1) Motor Vehicle and Road Traffic Act)
criminal_individuals.notes: Charged 24 July 2020; sought judicial review of a magistrate's decision to adjourn his trial when prosecution witnesses failed to appear. Underlying DUI trial remains to be heard.

related_litigation: High Court CV2023-03724 (Seepersad J dismissed the judicial review)
Summary DUI prosecution before the Magistrates' Court
social_headline: Court of Appeal: stop naming magistrates as parties when their rulings are challenged
social_post: A drunk-driving defendant who tried to overturn a magistrate's decision to adjourn his trial has lost his appeal - but the case produced an important reform. Stefan Hearn argued Magistrate Rehana Ali wrongly adjourned his DUI trial when the prosecution's witnesses didn't show up. The Court of Appeal, led by Justice Bereaux, held she had properly weighed the factors, including the seriousness of drunk driving and the public interest, and judicial review is not an appeal on the merits. The court also ruled that the long-standing practice of naming magistrates as parties and cross-examining them on affidavits is wrong and must stop, to protect judicial impartiality. Such challenges should be defended by the prosecution, not the judge. Appeal dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Judicial review examines the decision-making process, not the merits; an appellate court interferes only where the lower court was plainly wrong
A magistrate has broad discretion to adjourn, weighing the history of the case, seriousness of the offence and the public interest
Magistrates (and tribunals) should not be named as parties or actively defend their decisions in judicial review - they must remain neutral
outcome: Appeal dismissed; the magistrate's decision to adjourn upheld as a proper exercise of discretion. The Court ruled that the practice of joining magistrates as parties to judicial review of their decisions should be discontinued. Parties to be heard on appeal costs.
osint_notes: Judicial-review/criminal-procedure ruling with significant precedent on magistrates as parties. No commercial entities, property or financial content. Crime tracking: underlying drink-driving charge.

title: The Attorney General v Ramjattan
court: Court of Appeal, Trinidad and Tobago
claimants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago (Appellant)

individuals.name: Dervanand Ramjattan
individuals.notes: Charged in 2014 with Liquor Licences Act offences; won malicious-prosecution damages at trial, reversed on appeal

individuals.name: Inspector Subash Ramkelawan
individuals.notes: Investigated for about six months and laid the four liquor-licence charges on his superior's instructions; held to have had reasonable cause and no malice

criminal_individuals.name: Dervanand Ramjattan
offences: breaches of the Liquor Licences Act ss.48(1), 51(1), 51(3), 54(1)
criminal_individuals.notes: Charged 2 May 2014 over alleged liquor-licence breaches (missing cases of alcohol/vodka). The civil case was his malicious-prosecution claim; the Court of Appeal found the charges were brought with reasonable cause and without malice.

related_litigation: High Court CV2016-01175 (malicious-prosecution award, set aside on appeal)
social_headline: Appeal Court overturns malicious-prosecution win over liquor-licence charges
social_post: The Court of Appeal has reversed a malicious-prosecution award in a liquor-licence case. Dervanand Ramjattan had won at the High Court after being charged in 2014 with four breaches of the Liquor Licences Act, with the trial judge finding the police lacked reasonable cause and acted maliciously. But the Appeal Court, led by Justices Bereaux and Mohammed, found the trial judge made material errors and ignored the defence's evidence. The charging officer, Inspector Ramkelawan, had investigated for nearly six months and acted on his superior's instructions, so even if some charges lacked reasonable cause, there was no malice. The appeal was allowed and the award set aside. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Malicious prosecution requires both absence of reasonable and probable cause AND malice - sloppiness or recklessness alone is not malice
Appellate courts defer to trial judges' findings but may intervene where the judge ignored a party's case or made material errors
Acting on a superior's instructions after a thorough investigation supports an honest belief in a proper basis to charge
outcome: Appeal allowed; the trial judge's findings of no reasonable cause and malice set aside. The charging officer held to have had reasonable cause and to have acted without malice. Parties to be heard on costs.
osint_notes: Malicious-prosecution appeal arising from liquor-licence charges. No commercial entities, property or financial figures. Crime tracking: underlying regulatory (Liquor Licences Act) charges.

social_headline: Handshake deal between sand and concrete firms unravels in $5.3M court reckoning
social_post: Two construction-materials companies whose decade-long trading ran on trust have ended up in court. Ashmead Ali Contracting Services sued Kaizen Trading for over $9.2 million on a running account that exchanged some $69 million in concrete, sand and aggregate from 2010 to 2019, plus disputed loans. Both sides kept poor records and tried to walk back a 2019 letter to First Citizens Bank admitting Kaizen owed $4.23 million. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC found the claim inflated and rejected a suspicious $2 million 'loan' meant to double to $4 million in months, but held Kaizen still owed a net $5,322,257.22. Kaizen's $1.6 million counterclaim was dismissed as unsupported. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Informal trading on trust without proper records leaves both sides struggling to prove their figures
An unqualified written admission of debt (e.g. a letter to the bank) is strong evidence parties cannot easily resile from
A loan that doubles the principal in months invites scrutiny and may be disbelieved absent a cogent explanation
outcome: Judgment for the claimant: Kaizen to pay $5,322,257.22 as damages for breach of contract plus $237,222.57 costs. The claimant's claimed $4 million 'loan' and $1 million truck-loss claim were rejected; the defendant's $1,645,511.67 counterclaim dismissed with $141,275.58 costs.
osint_notes: Large commercial running-account dispute naming two construction companies and their MDs, plus suppliers (Readymix) and a third-party debtor (KJS Enterprises), with multi-million-dollar figures and a bank (First Citizens). Strong commercial-network and financial OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: Matthews v The State
court: Court of Appeal, Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Roslyn Lucas
individuals.notes: Four-year-old child murdered in August 1978

criminal_individuals.name: Peter Matthews
criminal_individuals.notes: Convicted in 1984 of the 1978 murder of four-year-old Roslyn Lucas; sentenced to death, commuted to life imprisonment in 1994 (Pratt and Morgan). In custody about 46 years. Resentenced to 50 years in May 2025; on appeal that resentence was quashed and remitted for resentencing before another judge.

related_litigation: Original conviction (1984), appeals dismissed (CoA 1985; Privy Council 1986); constitutional motions on execution/stay; 2024 constitutional motion leading to resentencing
social_headline: Appeal Court quashes 50-year resentence for 1978 child murder after 46 years in custody
social_post: The Court of Appeal has quashed the resentencing of Peter Matthews, convicted in 1984 of the 1978 murder of four-year-old Roslyn Lucas. Matthews was sentenced to death, but his sentence was commuted to life imprisonment in 1994 under the Pratt and Morgan principle, and he has been in custody for some 46 years. A resentencing judge gave him 50 years in May 2025 with rehabilitation programs and probation conditions. The Appeal Court, led by Justice Maria Wilson, held that exercise was flawed and effectively imposed an original sentence that would deny both sides a proper right of appeal, so it remitted the matter to another High Court judge for fresh resentencing. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Resentencing must be conducted so as to preserve the parties' right of appeal
Constitutional relief is not normally a substitute for an appeal against conviction or sentence
Long pre-sentence custody and rehabilitation must be properly factored into a fresh sentence
outcome: Appeal allowed; the 26 May 2025 resentence (50 years) quashed and the matter remitted to the High Court for resentencing before another judge, with prison reports on the appellant's rehabilitation programs to be provided.
osint_notes: Long-running murder/resentencing appeal (1978 child murder). No commercial entities, property or financial content. Tracked for crime intelligence (historic murder, death-penalty commutation, resentencing).

title: The Minister of Works and Transport v Neranjen
court: Court of Appeal, Trinidad and Tobago
claimants: The Honourable Minister of Works and Transport (Appellant)

individuals.name: Navin Neranjen
individuals.notes: Owner of red-band maxi-taxi HCW 6474 and holder of the seized Priority Bus Route permit; failed to respond to the Ministry's inquiry

individuals.name: Doodnath Neranjen
individuals.notes: Father of Navin; was driving the maxi-taxi on the PBR when stopped; the court found his evidence untruthful

related_litigation: High Court CV (Seepersad J granted judicial review, reversed on appeal)
social_headline: Maxi-taxi owners lose PBR permit fight - and the court says the wound was self-inflicted
social_post: The Court of Appeal has overturned a judicial-review win for two maxi-taxi operators over a seized Priority Bus Route permit. Doodnath Neranjen was stopped on the PBR near Macoya driving his son Navin's red-band maxi (HCW 6474) and was issued a fixed-penalty notice; a transit officer seized the PBR permit. Father and son sued the Minister of Works and Transport over its retention. The majority, with Justice Bereaux writing, found the claim 'completely without merit', held the operators had not been candid with the court, and noted Navin ignored the Ministry's request to explain. Chief Justice Boodoosingh dissented. The claim was dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Natural justice is concerned with substance, not technicalities; a self-inflicted situation may not attract relief
Applicants for judicial review owe a duty of full and frank disclosure (candour) to the court
Failing to respond to a public authority's request to explain can defeat a later complaint of being denied a hearing
outcome: Appeal allowed (Bereaux and Mohammed JJA; Boodoosingh CJ dissenting). The High Court's judicial-review judgment set aside and the claim dismissed; respondents to pay the Ministry's costs in both courts.
osint_notes: Judicial-review dispute over a maxi-taxi Priority Bus Route permit. No commercial entities, property or financial figures; a fixed-penalty notice but no conviction.

title: Ramkissoon and Ali-Ramkissoon v Guerra (trading as DG Readymix)
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago

companies.name: DG Readymix
companies.ownership_notes: Supplied concrete and a pump for casting the claimants' suspended floor slab in Toco

companies.name: CARIRI (Caribbean Industrial Research Institute)
companies.ownership_notes: Took core samples of the concrete; its report author (Lisa Ramoutar) was not called as a witness

companies.name: Etercnoc Limited Concrete Solutions
companies.ownership_notes: Produced a report (struck out) relying on the CARIRI findings

individuals.name: Regan Ramkissoon
individuals.notes: Homeowner building a multi-storey house in Toco

individuals.name: Wilson Maloney
individuals.notes: Accepted delivery of the concrete with 10 extra cement bags added; testified decking can fail for many reasons

individuals.name: Daniel Guerra
individuals.notes: Proprietor of DG Readymix; made a no-case submission and called no evidence

financial_figures.what_it_is: Price paid for 28 metres of concrete and pump rental
Labour cost claimed as part of special damages
Claim value on which prescribed costs were awarded against the claimants

social_headline: Homeowners' cracked-slab claim against concrete supplier crumbles without expert proof
social_post: A Toco couple who blamed defective ready-mix concrete for their cracking, sagging floor slab have lost their case against supplier DG Readymix. Regan Ramkissoon and Shamiza Ali-Ramkissoon said loads kept too long in mixer trucks - one of which got stuck on the access road - produced weak concrete, pointing to a CARIRI core-sample report. But Justice Joan Charles struck out their technologist's report for not complying with the expert-evidence rules, and the CARIRI author who actually tested the cores was never called. With their own contractor admitting decking can fail for many reasons, the couple couldn't prove the concrete caused the damage. The claim was dismissed and they must pay costs on a $359,934 claim value. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A claimant alleging defective goods must prove causation, usually with admissible expert evidence
Expert reports must comply with CPR Part 33; a report relying on another's untested findings can be struck out
Res ipsa loquitur will not fill an evidential gap where the damage could have many causes
outcome: Claim dismissed. Without admissible expert evidence the claimants failed to prove the concrete was defective or breached the Sale of Goods Act. Claimants ordered to pay prescribed costs on a claim value of $359,934.69.
osint_notes: Construction/Sale-of-Goods dispute naming a ready-mix supplier (DG Readymix), a testing body (CARIRI) and a consultancy (Etercnoc Ltd), with project and damages figures. Commercial data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: National Security Council (Rowley et al) v Griffith
court: Court of Appeal, Trinidad and Tobago
claimants: The National Security Council (Dr Keith Rowley; Faris Al-Rawi; Fitzgerald Hinds; Colm Imbert; Stuart Young; Marvin Gonzales); the Prime Minister; the Minister of National Security (Appellants)
defendants: The Audit Committee (Wellington Virgil, Raymond Craig, Lennard Charles, Brian Pierre) (Second Respondent)

companies.name: National Security Council (NSC)
companies.ownership_notes: Cabinet-level body that appointed an audit committee to investigate the issuance of firearms users' licences by the TTPS Firearms Department

companies.name: The Audit Committee
companies.ownership_notes: Committee appointed by the NSC to audit the TTPS Firearms Department under successive Commissioners of Police, including Gary Griffith

individuals.name: Gary Griffith
individuals.notes: Former Commissioner of Police; challenged the audit committee's report and its potential release as a breach of natural justice and the duty of candour

individuals.name: Dr Keith Rowley
individuals.notes: NSC chairman; said the report should be laid before Parliament and considered by Cabinet

individuals.name: Fitzgerald Hinds
individuals.notes: Member of the NSC

related_litigation: High Court CV2022-03915 (trial judgment 15 March 2023 and costs ruling 25 May 2023)
Earlier interim injunction proceedings and 21 Dec 2022 Court of Appeal variation
social_headline: Top court weighs whether Rowley's NSC can table its firearms-licence audit on ex-COP Griffith
social_post: A high-stakes constitutional battle reached the Court of Appeal over whether the National Security Council - chaired by then-PM Keith Rowley and including ministers Al-Rawi, Hinds, Imbert, Young and Gonzales - can release an audit committee's report into how firearms users' licences were issued by the police, including during Gary Griffith's time as Commissioner. A trial judge had injuncted the PM from laying the report or an executive summary before Parliament, while ruling the committee was lawfully constituted. The NSC appealed the gag; Griffith cross-appealed, alleging breaches of natural justice and the duty of candour and seeking the committee members' names. The judgment turns heavily on the duty of full and frank disclosure owed by the State in public-law cases. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: In public-law cases the State owes a high duty of candour - full and frank disclosure of relevant facts to the court
An audit body can be lawfully constituted even where the affected person has not yet been given a hearing, if no adverse decision has been made
Courts will not condone 'fishing expeditions' for disclosure where a claimant has no positive case
outcome: Appeal and counter-appeal concerning the NSC's audit committee report into firearms-licence issuance and an injunction barring its release to Parliament. The trial judge had held the committee lawfully constituted and the report not null and void, while restraining the report's publication. The final Court of Appeal disposition is contained in the full 97-page judgment (not fully captured in the extracted text).
osint_notes: High-profile constitutional/firearms-policy matter naming the National Security Council and its members (PM Rowley and five ministers), former Police Commissioner Gary Griffith and four named audit-committee members. Strong political-network and firearms-governance OSINT value; full disposition spans a 97-page judgment only partly captured here.

title: Bissoon v Absolute Transport Ltd
court: Court of Appeal, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: Absolute Transport Ltd (formerly K&C Logistics Ltd) (Respondent)

companies.name: Absolute Transport Ltd (formerly K&C Logistics Ltd)
companies.ownership_notes: Transport/logistics company; employed the appellant as a driver/salesman; found liable at trial for an unsafe system of work

individuals.name: Rennie Bissoon
individuals.notes: Driver/salesman who fell off a truck in July 2014 and suffered back injuries; also a weekend market vendor; appealed the quantum of damages

financial_figures.what_it_is: General damages awarded at trial
Loss of future earnings as a driver/salesman (less Workmen's Compensation)
Appellant's average monthly salary
Prescribed costs awarded at trial

related_litigation: High Court CV2016-03211 (trial award; respondent's counter-appeal discontinued)
social_headline: Injured truck driver's bid for bigger payout fails on appeal over unproven market earnings
social_post: A driver/salesman who fell off a truck while making deliveries has lost his appeal for higher damages. Rennie Bissoon was injured in 2014 and won a negligence claim against his employer, Absolute Transport (formerly K&C Logistics), receiving $100,000 general damages plus future-earnings and care awards. He appealed, arguing the judge over-discounted his income, ignored his weekend market-vending profits and wrongly refused past earnings and extra medical costs. The Court of Appeal, with Justice Dean-Armorer writing, held an appellate court only interferes where a damages award is wrong in principle or wildly off - and the trial judge wasn't. His market profits and past losses weren't properly proved or pleaded. Appeal dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: An appeal court will not disturb a damages award unless it is wrong in principle or a wholly erroneous estimate
Past loss of earnings and quantifiable medical costs are special damages that must be specifically pleaded and proved
Uncorroborated oral evidence of income (e.g. market profits) may be rejected, especially where corroborating witnesses or documents were available but not produced
outcome: Appeal dismissed; the trial judge's assessment of damages upheld. The 25% income discount, multiplier of 5, refusal of unproven market-vendor profits, unpleaded past earnings and unpleaded extra medical costs were all within the judge's proper discretion.
osint_notes: Personal-injury damages appeal naming an employer company (Absolute Transport / formerly K&C Logistics) with salary and damages figures. Modest commercial data; no criminal content.

title: Charles and others v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Jerome Charles
individuals.notes: One of 17 former Defence Force members claiming pension/retirement benefits

financial_figures.what_it_is: Estimated cumulative retirement and pension benefits the 17 claimants sought

social_headline: Court rejects 17 ex-soldiers' $55M pension claim - can't rewrite the Defence Act
social_post: Seventeen former Defence Force members have lost a constitutional claim seeking about $55 million in retirement and pension benefits. They argued the State breached their rights by never making regulations under the Defence Act that would let them earn a monthly pension, leaving them worse off than others. Justice Frank Seepersad dismissed the claim, holding that the soldiers enlisted knowing the statutory framework - including compulsory retirement at 45, which could fall before the qualifying period for a pension. Extending pension entitlement and assuming new financial liabilities, he said, is for Parliament and the Executive, not the courts. The claim was dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts will not retrospectively rewrite a statutory pension framework to create entitlements Parliament did not authorise
Decisions on extending pension liability and allocating finite public resources fall to the Legislature and Executive
Entering service with knowledge of the governing rules undercuts a later claim of unfair deprivation
outcome: Claim dismissed in its entirety. The court held it could not expand State pension liability beyond the statutory framework; that is a matter for Parliament. Claimants to pay the defendant's costs.
osint_notes: Constitutional/pension claim by 17 former Defence Force members against the State. A single large aggregate figure ($55M) but no commercial entities, property or criminal content.

social_headline: Ousted MD wins ~$940k from Diego Martin land firms and their chairman for sacking and slander
social_post: A construction and property professional has won a substantial judgment against two land-development companies and their chairman. Michael Khan, former Managing Director of Capildeo Lands Limited and Prism Trust and Finance Company - both controlled by Carlton Reis and holding vast acreage in Diego Martin - sued for constructive dismissal, breach of contract and defamation. Justice Robin Mohammed found in his favour on all issues, awarding $805,744.30 in damages including exemplary damages, plus $135,817.57 interest and over $104,000 in costs - close to $940,000 in value. The companies' counterclaim was dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: An employer's wrongful/constructive dismissal combined with defamatory statements can attract aggravated and exemplary damages
Company directors can be personally liable for slander they utter
Exemplary damages and interest are included in the 'value' of a claim when quantifying prescribed costs
outcome: Judgment for the claimant on constructive dismissal, breach of contract and defamation. Defendants to pay $805,744.30 damages (including exemplary), $135,817.57 interest and $104,617.14 costs; the defendants' re-amended counterclaim dismissed with $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Employment/defamation dispute naming two Diego Martin land-development companies (Capildeo Lands, Prism Trust and Finance), their chairman/owner Carlton Reis, and the claimant's own construction firm (KM Electrical), with registered addresses and large damages figures. social_headline: Neighbours split the win in D'Abadie boundary fight - each takes a slice of Lot 2A
social_post: A boundary dispute over Lot 2A on Beharry Trace, Red Hill, D'Abadie has ended in a split decision. Jason and Nakisha Maule sued neighbours Nicholas and Shirley John for trespassing on the northern edge of their land and blocking a private access way. Justice Robin Mohammed agreed the Johns had trespassed on the northern boundary, granting the Maules possession there and $15,000 in damages. But the Johns succeeded on their counterclaim for adverse possession of a north-eastern strip, extinguishing the Maules' title to that piece. With each side winning, the costs orders cancelled out. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Boundary disputes can produce split outcomes, with each party succeeding on different portions of the land
Long, exclusive occupation of a strip of a neighbour's land can extinguish title by adverse possession
Where both claim and counterclaim succeed in part, costs awards may offset each other
outcome: Mixed result: claimants granted possession of and an injunction over the northern boundary of Lot 2A plus $5,000 special and $10,000 nominal damages; defendants succeed on adverse possession of the north-eastern portion, extinguishing the claimants' title there. Costs (65% each way, $9,100) cancel out.
osint_notes: Real-property boundary/adverse-possession dispute with an identified parcel (Lot 2A, Red Hill, D'Abadie; 634.4 sq m). Property data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

Compensation awarded for injurious affection (acquisition of ~5.11 acres)

social_headline: Penal contractor wins $26M for land taken for the Point Fortin Highway
social_post: A Penal company has been awarded over $26 million for land the State took to build the San Fernando to Point Fortin Highway. Jokhan General Contractors, now in receivership, had about 5.11 acres at Jokhan Trace compulsorily acquired by NIDCO and claimed it was owed $48.6 million under an accepted 2017 offer. NIDCO disputed any binding deal, but a consent order in 2023 settled liability, leaving the court to assess the figure. Justice Westmin James, noting weak before-and-after valuation evidence, awarded $4.4 million for injurious affection and $21.8 million for disturbance to the firm's operations and tenancies - $26,220,593.74 in total, plus 5% interest from 2014. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Compensation for compulsory acquisition aims at fairness, not punishment or a windfall, and must be tied to losses proved to flow from the acquisition
Claimants should adduce valuation/accounting evidence prepared specifically for acquisition, with before-and-after analysis and clear parcel identification
Even where precision is impossible, the quality of evidence shapes the size and reliability of the award
outcome: Compensation assessed for the compulsory acquisition: $4,433,356.01 for injurious affection and $21,787,237.73 for disturbance (total $26,220,593.74), plus 5% interest from 17 Oct 2014 and prescribed costs. The claimed $48.6 million was not fully supported by the evidence.
osint_notes: State compulsory-acquisition compensation matter naming a contractor (Jokhan General Contractors, in receivership) and the state infrastructure company NIDCO, with a major highway project, an identified Penal property and multi-million-dollar figures. Strong commercial/public-works OSINT value.

title: Thomas v The Chief of Defence Staff and The Attorney General
court: High Court of Justice, San Fernando, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Junior Thomas
individuals.notes: Former Staff Sergeant of the Trinidad and Tobago Regiment; completed the Warrant Officers Course but reached compulsory retirement before any promotion to Warrant Officer Class II

related_litigation: Russell Reyes v The Chief of Defence Staff CV2022-04043 (the 'Reyes Injunction' staying Staff Sergeant promotions)
social_headline: Soldier who 'aced' his warrant officer course loses promotion fight - blame the court order
social_post: A former Defence Force Staff Sergeant has lost his bid to be promoted to Warrant Officer Class II. Junior Thomas was sent on the Warrant Officers Course in late 2022 on the strength of his skills and passed in January 2023. But a separate court order in the Russell Reyes case had frozen all Staff Sergeant promotions, and Thomas hit compulsory retirement age in February 2023 before that freeze lifted in June. He sued, arguing the Chief of Defence Staff wrongly refused to receive a promotion recommendation. Justice Lambert Peterson held the whole promotion process was lawfully stayed to comply with the injunction, that he had signed an acknowledgement that passing the course guaranteed nothing, and that his rights weren't breached. Claim dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Receiving and acting on a promotion recommendation is part of the promotion process, which can be lawfully frozen by a court injunction
Signing an acknowledgement that a course does not guarantee promotion undercuts any later legitimate-expectation claim
Compliance with a binding court order is a legal necessity, not an irrational or arbitrary exercise of power
outcome: Claim dismissed. The Chief of Defence Staff's refusal to receive a promotion recommendation was lawful compliance with the Reyes Injunction, not an error of law, irrationality or breach of legitimate expectation or constitutional rights. Costs to be decided on written submissions.
osint_notes: Military promotion/judicial-review and constitutional claim against the Defence Force. No commercial entities, property or financial figures; no criminal content.

title: Walker v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Marsha Walker
individuals.notes: Stopped by police during a one-person protest outside Parliament on 9 July 2021; won a declaration that police conduct breached her constitutional rights

social_headline: Police acted unconstitutionally shutting down a lone protester outside Parliament, court rules
social_post: A protester has partly won her constitutional claim against the State over how police handled her demonstration. Marsha Walker said officers stopped her one-person protest outside Parliament on 9 July 2021, telling her she needed permission. Justice Rahim held the police instructions were unlawful and unconstitutional, breaching her rights to protection of the law, freedom of expression and freedom of assembly - and that no permission is required for such a public meeting, only notification. She was awarded damages including a vindicatory sum, to be assessed. A separate complaint about events on 23 November 2021 was dismissed, as the court found she was not then engaged in a protest. The State must pay 50% of her costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Under the Summary Offences Act, a public meeting or march requires notification, not police permission
Police directions that unlawfully suppress a lawful protest can breach the rights to expression, assembly and protection of the law
Such breaches can attract vindicatory damages in addition to compensatory damages
outcome: Partial success. Declared that police conduct on 9 July 2021 breached the claimant's rights under sections 4(b), 4(i) and 4(j) of the Constitution; damages including vindicatory damages to be assessed by a Master. The claim regarding 23 November 2021 dismissed. State to pay 50% of costs (fit for senior, junior and instructing counsel).
osint_notes: Constitutional/protest-rights matter against the State (police suppression of a one-person protest). No commercial entities, property or quantified figures; no criminal content. Modest public-interest value.

social_headline: Elderly woman's claim to a Cumuto right of way fails despite a fence trapping her access
social_post: A claim to a longstanding right of way over a Cumuto property has failed on the law, despite sympathetic facts. Elizabeth Wilson-Hogan sued on behalf of her ailing mother Chandai Wilson, saying the family had used a 40-foot strip to reach the Cumuto Main Road since 1994 until neighbour Sheila Mahabir fenced it off in 2024, leaving the elderly woman without vehicular access. Justice Westmin James dismissed the claim: the claimant hadn't shown the fee simple interest needed for a prescriptive easement, the pleaded case was confined to prescription, and the route's continuous use, dimensions and necessity weren't sufficiently proved. The judge stressed sympathy can't substitute for legal proof. Costs of $14,000 were awarded against her. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A prescriptive easement requires the claimant to hold a fee simple interest in the dominant land
A right-of-way claim must be pleaded on the correct basis (prescription vs necessity vs implied grant) and proved with certainty as to route and dimensions
Continuous use 'as of right' for the statutory period must be clearly established by evidence
outcome: Claim dismissed. The claimant failed to establish the fee simple interest, continuous user, certainty of the route or strict necessity required for a right of way. Claimant to pay $14,000 prescribed costs; 28-day stay.
osint_notes: Real-property/easement dispute over an identified Cumuto property and access strip, involving a sale via the Agricultural Development Bank. Property data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

social_headline: 'Sick building' claim against UDeCOTT fails - worker couldn't link illness to office air
social_post: A UDeCOTT employee who blamed poor air quality at the state company's Port of Spain head office for making him ill has lost his negligence claim. Daryl Geawan said 2014-15 refurbishment works filled the building with dust, leaving staff with breathing problems, and pointed to CARIRI air-quality reports. But Justice Margaret Mohammed found he failed to prove UDeCOTT breached its duty or caused his illness: his sick records showed similar symptoms from 2011, his family fell ill without visiting the office, his medical reports lacked dates and test results, and he repeatedly refused company medical exams. The claim was dismissed and he was ordered to pay $48,800 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: In a workplace-illness claim the employee must prove both breach of the duty to provide a safe system of work and causation
Vague, undated medical reports without test results or prescriptions carry little weight
Refusing the employer's medical examinations and selectively visiting doctors can undermine a personal-injury claim
outcome: Claim dismissed. The claimant failed to prove UDeCOTT breached its duty of care or that the building's air quality caused his illness. Claimant ordered to pay $48,800 prescribed costs.
osint_notes: Workplace personal-injury claim against the state development company UDeCOTT, naming its Port of Spain buildings and CARIRI, with damages figures. Corporate/public-entity data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: Modeste v Reid, Cornwall and Bankers Insurance Company of Trinidad and Tobago Limited
court: High Court of Justice, Sub-Registry San Fernando, Trinidad and Tobago (Master's assessment)

companies.ownership_notes: Local equivalent of the Ogden Tables; accepted by the court as a scientific basis for the future-loss multiplier

individuals.notes: Contractor/manual labourer; passenger seriously injured in a Nov 2017 collision; 30% permanent partial disability plus PTSD and depression

individuals.name: Natasha Trudy Cornwall
individuals.notes: Driver of the vehicle in which the claimant was a passenger

individuals.notes: Co-creator of The Smart Tables; gave expert evidence on the multiplier

General damages for pain, suffering and loss of amenities
Future loss of earnings (multiplicand $90,000 x multiplier 10, less discounts)

banks_financial_institutions: Bankers Insurance Company of Trinidad and Tobago Limited
social_headline: Injured contractor wins ~$1.87M, with 'Smart Tables' setting the future-earnings multiplier
social_post: A contractor seriously hurt as a passenger in a 2017 crash has been awarded about $1.87 million. Steve Modeste suffered a liver laceration, an open ankle fracture, PTSD and depression, with a 30% permanent partial disability, and can no longer do manual work. Master Rishma Ramrattan assessed $280,000 general damages plus large future-loss and special-damages awards against the driver, owner and insurer Bankers Insurance. Notably, instead of the traditional 'rough scale' multiplier, the court accepted The Smart Tables - a Trinidad-specific actuarial tool likened to the UK's Ogden Tables - as a more scientific basis, settling on a multiplier of 10 for future earnings. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts may use locally-developed actuarial tools (The Smart Tables) to set the multiplier for future loss, as a starting point rather than a slave to the figure
Inconsistent or undocumented self-employed earnings can lead the court to discount claimed daily rates and days worked
Future surgery/medication costs may be reduced where adequate treatment is available in the public health system
outcome: Damages assessed at about $1,865,112.55 total: $308,482.30 general (with interest), $400,711.50 special (with interest, after a $300,000 interim payment), $1,155,918.75 future loss, plus expert and prescribed costs. Multiplier derived from The Smart Tables (set at 10).
osint_notes: Motor-accident personal-injury assessment naming an insurer (Bankers Insurance), an actuarial tool (Smart Tables) and large damages figures, and establishing local use of actuarial multipliers. Commercial/legal-precedent data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

social_headline: Credit union CEO sacked over a 'sham' MasterCard audit wins $688k for wrongful dismissal
social_post: Eastern Credit Union's former Acting Group CEO has won a wrongful-dismissal case after the court branded the audit behind her firing a facade. Sherry Ann McDonald-Joseph, a chartered accountant with about 26 years at ECU, was sent on surprise leave, then summarily dismissed in March 2022 on seven grounds tied to the credit union's MasterCard chip-card project. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC found ECU proved none of them: the MasterCard decision pre-dated her, the costing was MARSIDS' job, and the audit had no terms of reference and never even spoke to her. She was awarded $612,900 contract damages plus $75,000 exemplary damages and costs - and the court found ECU's conduct egregious and high-handed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: To justify summary dismissal, the employer must prove just cause; the burden is on the employer
An audit without terms of reference, documentary support or any engagement with the employee will not support dismissal
Exemplary damages may be awarded where an employer's manner of dismissal is outrageous and high-handed
outcome: Judgment for the claimant. ECU failed to prove just cause for summary dismissal on any of the seven grounds. Awarded $612,900 breach-of-contract damages, $75,000 exemplary damages, 2.5% interest and $90,992.04 costs.
osint_notes: Employment dispute naming a major credit union (ECU) and its executives, a consultancy (MARSIDS), the claimant's firm (Harcon Harrington) and a MasterCard/EMV project, with detailed remuneration and damages figures and Central Bank regulatory context. Strong corporate-governance OSINT value.

related_litigation: Previous Proceedings CV2012-04599, CV2012-04598, CV2006-01349 (debenture found valid; appeal dismissed 2021)
social_headline: Court hands San Juan highway land to Seasons' receiver on a resulting trust
social_post: A long-running fight over a 0.4-hectare parcel on the Churchill Roosevelt Highway in San Juan has gone to the receiver of Seasons Limited. The land was held by Signature Properties - a non-trading company owned 50/50 by Dev Debideen and Om Prakash Maraj - but bought with money advanced by Maraj Gold Limited and recorded as Seasons' debt. Debideen claimed he'd paid US$100,000 cash toward it, but Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC rejected that as a recent invention, found the land was held on a resulting trust for Seasons, and rejected his bid to reopen the legality of the underlying $13M debenture as res judicata. Signature Properties must transfer the land to Seasons within 28 days. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Where one party provides the purchase money and title goes to another, a resulting trust is presumed in favour of the payer
Using a company to hold title for an operational reason does not by itself transfer beneficial ownership
Issues already decided between parties in privity (here a 50% shareholder and his company) cannot be re-litigated - res judicata
outcome: Claim succeeds. The Signature Land (Churchill Roosevelt Highway, San Juan) declared held on trust for Seasons Limited absolutely; Signature Properties ordered to convey it within 28 days. Challenge to the debenture's legality rejected as res judicata; limitation defence rejected. No order as to costs.
osint_notes: Corporate trust/property dispute mapping a network of related companies (Seasons, Signature Properties, Maraj Gold, Rajish, Resham) and individuals (Debideen, the Marajs), with a moneylender debenture and multi-million-dollar figures over an identified San Juan parcel. Strong corporate-network and asset-tracing OSINT value.

social_headline: 'Playgirl' tabloid libel costs Jack Warner's Sunshine Today $300k
social_post: Businesswoman Shirlanne Sacha Singh has won $300,000 in a libel case against the Sunshine Today newspaper, published by Jack Warner's New Media Sunshine Today Company. Two 2020 articles headlined 'Playgirl Singh' and 'Playgirl to Face US Court' accused her of fraudulently getting a $500,000 bank loan, stealing from a sick man, and facing US theft and fraud proceedings. Warner admitted in court he had no proof and was just 'providing the documentation', and the source - businessman Jorge Quintero - was never called. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC found the articles plainly defamatory, rejected the unparticularised justification defence, and granted damages plus a permanent injunction against republishing. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A defence of justification must be properly pleaded with particulars of truth (CPR 73.3) and supported by evidence
Saying you merely published a source's documents is not a defence; the publisher must prove the defamatory sting is substantially true
Republishing after being told a story is false, and refusing to apologise, are aggravating features increasing damages
outcome: Judgment for the claimant. The two articles held defamatory (imputing theft, fraud, dishonesty and immorality); the justification defence failed for want of pleading and evidence. Awarded $300,000 general damages (with aggravated uplift) plus interest, a permanent injunction against republication, and $55,695.89 costs. Special and exemplary damages refused.
osint_notes: Defamation case naming a media company and its publisher (Jack Austin Warner), a businesswoman and her US companies (Eagles Private Chartered Jet Services), and a US source (Jorge Quintero), with cross-border litigation and damages figures. Strong media/reputation and corporate-network OSINT value.

social_headline: Oropouche family land row: cousin ordered off the property she claimed by adverse possession
social_post: A family land dispute in Oropouche has gone to the Bhagwansingh family. They sued their cousin Tarawatie Balkissoon for trespassing on their Siparia Road land - about 1,266 sq m held under a 1956 deed - where she had put up a concrete building and galvanised sheds. Balkissoon counterclaimed that she owned the spot as a statutory tenant or by adverse possession. Justice Robin Mohammed rejected her counterclaim, declared the Bhagwansinghs the owners, and ordered her to deliver up the land within 60 days, demolish the structures and pay $45,000 in nominal damages for trespass and nuisance plus costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Documentary title under a registered deed prevails over a relative's claim of statutory tenancy or adverse possession unless that claim is clearly proved
Adverse possession requires clear, exclusive possession with intention to exclude the true owner for the statutory period
A failed counterclaim carries its own costs
outcome: Judgment for the claimants. Declared owners of the Siparia Road land; defendant to deliver up possession within 60 days; her counterclaim dismissed. Defendant to pay $45,000 nominal damages (trespass and nuisance) plus interest and costs of $14,000 (claim) and $9,100 (counterclaim).
osint_notes: Family real-property/adverse-possession dispute with an identified Oropouche parcel (Deed 6888 of 1956) and damages figures. Property data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: The Trinidad and Tobago Cricket Board of Control v Ramnarine
court: Court of Appeal, Trinidad and Tobago
claimants: The Trinidad and Tobago Cricket Board of Control (TTCB) (Appellant)

companies.name: The Trinidad and Tobago Cricket Board of Control (TTCB)
companies.ownership_notes: Governs cricket in T&T; incorporated by Act 34 of 1989, making its decisions amenable to judicial review

companies.name: National Gas Company (NGC)
companies.role: TTCB sponsor whose audit report was at issue
companies.ownership_notes: Sponsor of the TTCB; its internal audit report on TTCB was leaked and central to the orders under appeal

individuals.name: Dinanath Ramnarine
individuals.notes: TTCB member who brought judicial review challenging the 2019 board election and was tied to the leaked NGC audit report

related_litigation: High Court CV2019-01537 (Seepersad J; judgment 27 Jan 2020 and consequential orders 13 Feb 2020)
social_headline: Appeal Court trims judge's orders in TTCB election and NGC audit fight
social_post: The Court of Appeal has partly reined in a High Court ruling arising from a Trinidad and Tobago Cricket Board election dispute. TTCB member Dinanath Ramnarine had challenged the 2019 executive election as non-compliant with the board's constitution. The trial judge rejected that challenge but went further, ordering the TTCB to set up a detailed five-member committee to investigate a leaked National Gas Company audit report of the board. The Appeal Court, with Justice Dean-Armorer writing, held the judge overstepped by prescribing the committee's make-up and granting relief not sought: it kept only a bare declaration that the TTCB should appoint a committee to investigate the NGC report, struck out the rest, and dismissed Ramnarine's cross-appeal on the election. He must pay 60% of the appeal costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: In judicial review a judge should generally not grant relief that was not sought by the parties
A court can declare that a body should investigate, but should not micro-manage how (e.g. dictating committee membership)
Whether a constitutional/rule provision is mandatory or directory determines if non-compliance nullifies an election
outcome: Appeal allowed in part: the trial judge's detailed orders for a five-member investigative committee and the 13 Feb 2020 consequential orders struck out, leaving only a declaration that the TTCB should appoint a committee to investigate the NGC report. Ramnarine's cross-appeal (election non-compliance) dismissed; he pays 60% of the appeal costs and all cross-appeal costs.
osint_notes: Sports-governance/judicial-review matter naming the TTCB (statutory cricket body), its sponsor NGC and a known figure (Dinanath Ramnarine), involving a leaked corporate audit report. Institutional/governance OSINT value; no criminal content or financial figures disclosed.

social_headline: Dog owner ordered to pay ~$40k after his pet bit a mason at a family gathering
social_post: A San Fernando mason who was bitten by a neighbour's dog has been awarded damages. Anil Seales was walking to his car at a family gathering in January 2025 when a large mixed-breed dog bit his lower left leg. The owner, Dexter Francis, apologised and gave him travelling money for the health centre, but Seales sued under the scienter (dangerous-animal) doctrine and negligence. Francis didn't defend and a default judgment was entered. Master Rishma Ramrattan assessed the puncture-wound injury against comparable dog-bite cases and awarded $30,520 general damages plus special damages for travel, the medical report and six lost workdays, and costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A dog owner can be liable for bites under the scienter doctrine or in negligence for failing to secure the animal
General damages for dog-bite injuries are guided by comparable cases and the severity/duration of the injury
Informal expenses like taxi fares may be allowed without receipts where it is reasonable that none exist
outcome: Damages assessed after default judgment: general damages $30,520.70 and special damages $3,494.71 (both with interest), plus $6,002.31 costs, payable by the defendant dog owner.
osint_notes: Minor personal-injury (dog-bite) assessment naming the claimant's employer (Omnicorp Construction). Modest financial figures; no commercial intelligence or criminal content.

social_headline: Arima widow wins half a family lot after husband's will left her without provision
social_post: An Arima estate dispute has given a widow a half-share of family land even though her late husband's will stood. Eileen Davis, through her daughter and attorney, challenged Leon Davis's 2007 will and the 2018 grant of probate, alleging incapacity and undue influence by their son. Justice Karen Reid upheld the will as valid but found the deceased had failed to make reasonable financial provision for his widow. She declared Eileen entitled to a 50% interest in a portion of the Rock Road, Torrecilla Gardens property, ordered the personal representative to convey it within 30 days, and confirmed a separate lot to another son. Each side bears its own costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A valid will can still be adjusted where it fails to make reasonable financial provision for a spouse
Challenges to capacity, knowledge/approval and undue influence are distinct from a family-provision claim
Courts can order a personal representative to convey specific shares of estate land to satisfy provision
outcome: The 2007 will of Leon Davis declared valid, but the court found it failed to make reasonable financial provision for his widow. The widow awarded a 50% interest in a portion (Lot 3C) of the Rock Road, Arima land, with conveyances to her and to the second defendant ordered within 30 days. Each party bears own costs.

social_headline: Cocorite gunman convicted of shooting one victim, cleared of firing on police
social_post: A man known as 'Mataran' has been convicted over a 2006 shooting in Cocorite. Hayden Ryan faced five counts arising from an August 2006 incident in which gunmen shot at Avalon Strachan and two police officers. Justice Trevor Jones found Ryan guilty of shooting Avalon Strachan with intent to cause grievous bodily harm, and of possessing a 12-gauge shotgun, a 9mm pistol and ammunition without a licence. But the judge acquitted him on the counts of shooting at the two officers, being sure only that two other men - Obi Cummings and Steven De Mills - had fired at police. Sentencing follows. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: For joint liability for a shooting, the court must be sure the accused intentionally assisted or encouraged the specific act
Possession of a firearm and ammunition is established where there is no evidence of a valid Firearm User's Licence
An accused can be convicted on some counts of a multi-count indictment and acquitted on others on the same facts
outcome: Guilty of shooting Avalon Strachan with intent (Count 1) and of possession of a firearm (Count 4) and ammunition (Count 5); Not Guilty of shooting at PC Omari Danglade (Count 2) and PC Gary Moore (Count 3).
osint_notes: Firearms/shooting-with-intent conviction arising from a 2006 Cocorite incident. No commercial entities, property or financial content. Tracked for crime intelligence (firearms, shooting, named co-shooters).

social_headline: Roofing subcontractor's pay claim collapses - he ends up owing the contractor $87k
social_post: A roofing subcontractor who sued for unpaid work has ended up owing money instead. Shaun Bowman of A Class Roofing claimed Life Time Roofing owed him over $200,000 for cladding and roofing on the Massy Energy (MEES) building at Point Lisas. But Justice Karen Reid found the agreement was the written schedule Bowman refused to sign, that his work was defective - leaks, poorly cut windows, misaligned panels - and that his evidence, contradicted by his own WhatsApp messages, was untruthful. The defects caused main contractor Weldfab to withhold $115,023.76 from Life Time Roofing. Bowman's claim was dismissed, the counterclaim succeeded, and after set-off he must pay Life Time Roofing $87,428.76 plus costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A subcontractor's work carries an implied term that it be done in a good and workmanlike manner; defective work entitles the employer to an abatement
Contemporaneous records (e.g. WhatsApp messages) can fatally contradict a party's later inflated claims
Beginning time-sensitive works signals acceptance of the written contract terms even if the document is unsigned
outcome: Claim dismissed; counterclaim succeeds. Bowman's defective work caused Weldfab to withhold $115,023.76 from Life Time Roofing; after set-off against the small balance owed, Bowman ordered to pay Life Time Roofing $87,428.76 in damages plus costs.
osint_notes: Construction subcontract dispute mapping a contractor chain (A Class Roofing, Life Time Roofing, Weldfab, MEES/Massy) with named directors and detailed contract/withholding figures. Strong commercial-network OSINT value; no criminal content.
