social_headline: Ex-son-in-law must leave Rio Claro family land - his stay was only a revocable licence
social_post: A Rio Claro family has won back a lot of its land from a former in-law. In 2008 the Yearwood/King family let their granddaughter Afi and her then-fiance Vernon Phipps build a home on a parcel of the family's Dades Trace land for their marriage. The couple married in 2009, but the marriage broke down in 2017 and Afi moved out - while Phipps stayed put and ignored a notice to quit. Phipps filed no defence and led no evidence, only arguing in submissions that his licence was meant to be for life. Justice Karen Reid held the arrangement was a revocable, rent-free licence that ended on the licensor's death and with the notice to quit, and ordered him to give up possession within 30 days and pay $14,000 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A rent-free permission to occupy family land is usually a revocable licence that creates no interest in the land
A licence generally ends on the licensor's death and can be revoked by notice to quit
Arguments of an irrevocable licence or constructive trust need a pleaded case and evidence - they cannot be raised in submissions alone
outcome: Judgment for the claimants. The defendant held only a revocable licence (terminated on the licensor's death and by the 2018 notice to quit) and must deliver vacant possession of the Dades Trace lot within 30 days and pay $14,000 costs. No trespass damages awarded.
osint_notes: Family-land possession dispute with an identified Rio Claro parcel (Crown Grant Vol 235 Folio 49). Property data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: Stoute v University of Trinidad and Tobago
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago

companies.name: University of Trinidad and Tobago (UTT)
companies.ownership_notes: State university; employed the claimant as Professor in Practice and Head of the Environmental Studies Department

individuals.name: Valerie Stoute
individuals.notes: Professor in Practice and Head of Environmental Studies at UTT; placed on administrative leave during a misconduct investigation that was dropped; later retired

financial_figures.what_it_is: Breach-of-contract damages the claimant sought
Claimant's monthly base salary (2013-2019)
Ex gratia emolument, benefits and salary received on retirement
Nominal damages awarded for breach of the implied term of trust and confidence

related_litigation: Roger Carrington v University of Trinidad and Tobago CV2016-03460 (cited)
social_headline: UTT professor wins only $15k after botched misconduct probe before her retirement
social_post: A University of Trinidad and Tobago professor has largely lost her near-$1 million claim against the institution. Valerie Stoute, who headed UTT's Environmental Studies Department until the programme was discontinued, sued for breach of contract and fiduciary duty after being placed on administrative leave in 2018 over misconduct allegations that were eventually dropped. Justice Lambert Peterson held an employer owes no general fiduciary duty, and that UTT did not breach the contract by failing to renew it or raise her pay. But the way it ran the investigation - leaving her in limbo for months, ignoring its own legal advice - breached the implied term of trust and confidence. As she suffered no provable loss (she kept full pay and retired normally), she was awarded just $15,000 in nominal damages. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: An employer does not generally owe an employee a fiduciary duty; fiduciary duties arise only from specific undertakings
Every employment contract carries an implied term of mutual trust and confidence, which a heavy-handed investigation can breach
Where a breach causes no provable loss, only nominal damages (at the court's discretion) are awarded
outcome: Partial success. No fiduciary duty found; no breach in failing to renew the contract, raise pay or apply the pre-retirement policy. UTT breached the implied term of trust and confidence by the manner of its misconduct investigation, but the claimant proved no loss. Nominal damages of $15,000 awarded; costs to be decided on submissions.
osint_notes: Employment dispute naming a state university (UTT) with academic-salary and retirement figures. Institutional/employment data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: Chickoree v Scott
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Ramnarine Chickoree
individuals.notes: Claimed possessory title to a 37-perch lot at Souria Trace based on long occupation; claim dismissed

individuals.name: Andrew Scott
individuals.notes: Held the superior possessory claim; succeeded on his counterclaim

individuals.name: Clyde Hernandez
individuals.notes: Occupied the land as a permissive licensee, not adversely, so no possessory title passed to his estate

financial_figures.what_it_is: Costs of the claim awarded to the defendant (a further $14,000 awarded on the counterclaim)

property_lease.address_or_description: Lot 19, Souria Trace, Brazil Village, Talparo, Ward of San Raphael; 37 perches (linked to Royal Grant Vol 13 Folio 563, LP210)
property_lease.lease_terms: Claim for possessory title/adverse possession vs the defendant's superior possessory claim
property_lease.outcome: Claim dismissed; occupation by the claimant's predecessor was permissive. Property removed from Clyde Hernandez's estate; 2017 Deed of Assent set aside; claimant restrained from entering or dealing with the land

related_litigation: Estate of Clyde Hernandez L 465 of 2016 (inventory to be amended)
social_headline: Talparo land claim fails - decades of occupation 'with permission' can't ripen into ownership
social_post: A bid to claim title to a small lot at Souria Trace, Talparo, by long occupation has failed. Ramnarine Chickoree said he and his predecessors had held the 37-perch lot for over 30 years, and sued Andrew Scott for interfering. But Justice Westmin James found the key predecessor, the late Clyde Hernandez, occupied the land only as a permissive licensee - and permission, no matter how long, does not mature into adverse possession. The claim was dismissed, the defendant's counterclaim upheld, the property removed from Hernandez's estate inventory, and a 2017 deed set aside. Chickoree was restrained from entering or dealing with the land and ordered to pay $28,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Occupation with the owner's permission, however long, cannot become adverse possession
A permissive licensee acquires no transmissible possessory title for his estate to pass on
Where a property was wrongly included in an estate, the court can amend the inventory and set aside the resulting deed
outcome: Claim dismissed. The claimant's predecessor occupied the land as a permissive licensee, so no possessory title passed; the defendant's superior claim prevailed. The lot removed from Clyde Hernandez's estate inventory, the 2017 Deed of Assent set aside, and the claimant restrained and ordered to pay $14,000 costs on the claim and $14,000 on the counterclaim.
osint_notes: Adverse-possession/estate dispute over an identified Talparo lot (Royal Grant Vol 13 Folio 563) with a set-aside deed. Property data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: Phillip v Service Commissions Department
court: High Court of Justice, Sub-Registry San Fernando, Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Andre Phillip
individuals.notes: Made a Freedom of Information request about Research Officer promotions/seniority; the department failed to respond within 30 days

related_litigation: Vijay Singh v Public Service Commission [2019] UKPC 18 (cited)
social_headline: State body broke FOI law by missing the 30-day deadline - even though it later handed over the records
social_post: A public servant has won a declaration that the Service Commissions Department broke the Freedom of Information Act. Andre Phillip asked for documents about Research Officer promotions and seniority in February 2023, but the department blew past the mandatory 30-day deadline, only producing the records in stages months later - after he had filed judicial review. Justice Robin Mohammed held that belated compliance doesn't erase the breach: 'access delayed is access denied'. The orders to compel disclosure were academic since the records were handed over, but the breach declaration stood, and the State was ordered to pay his costs - though the judge trimmed the bill, refusing to certify Senior Counsel for what was a well-settled point. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A public authority has an absolute duty to decide a FOI request within 30 days; the breach occurs on the 31st day
Later voluntary disclosure does not cure or moot the statutory breach - a declaration still vindicates the applicant
Costs against the State for FOI delay must be proportionate; Senior Counsel may not be certified where the point is well-settled
outcome: Declaration that the Service Commissions Department breached section 15 of the FOIA by missing the 30-day deadline. Mandamus and remaining declarations dismissed as academic (records since provided). Costs awarded to the claimant, certified for one Band D advocate and one instructing attorney only - no Senior Counsel.
osint_notes: Freedom-of-Information/administrative-law matter against a state body. No commercial entities, property or financial figures; no criminal content. Notable as a precedent on FOI delay and proportionate costs.

title: Schullere and others v James (Estate of Eurina Schullere)
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Gregory Harold Schullere
individuals.notes: Beneficiary/representative of the estate of Eurina Schullere; sought possession of the family land

individuals.name: Gerard James
individuals.notes: Grandson of the deceased's daughter Ruby Blackman; claimed adverse possession (rejected as occupation was permissive)

individuals.name: Eurina Schullere
individuals.notes: Died 8 August 2008; owned the four-acre Charuma parcel

financial_figures.what_it_is: Mesne profits ($500/month from the notice to quit to judgment)
Prescribed costs on the claim (a further $14,000 on the counterclaim)

property_lease.address_or_description: Approximately 1 acre of a 4-acre registered parcel in the Ward of Charuma (Crown Grant Vol 196 Folio 427; Certificate of Title Vol 931 Folio 145), estate of Eurina Schullere
property_lease.lease_terms: Permissive family occupation vs counterclaim for adverse possession
property_lease.outcome: Adverse-possession counterclaim rejected; defendant to deliver up possession within 3 months and pay mesne profits

social_headline: Charuma family land stays with the estate as relative's adverse-possession claim fails
social_post: A family fight over an acre of land in Charuma has gone to the estate of the late Eurina Schullere. Her beneficiaries sued relative Gerard James, whose grandmother (the deceased's daughter) had been allowed to occupy the land. James claimed he'd held it long enough to own it by adverse possession. Justice Westmin James found the occupation was permissive and never became adverse, so no possessory title arose. James was ordered to deliver up vacant possession within three months, restrained from harassing the claimants, and ordered to pay mesne profits of $500 a month (about $22,700 to date and continuing) plus $28,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Occupation that starts with family permission does not ripen into adverse possession without a clear change to adverse intention
An adverse possessor must show exclusive, undisturbed possession with intent to exclude the true owner for the limitation period
A successful owner can recover mesne profits for the period of wrongful occupation after a notice to quit
outcome: Judgment for the claimants. The defendant's adverse-possession counterclaim failed (occupation was permissive). He must deliver up vacant possession within 3 months, is restrained from interfering with or harassing the claimants, and must pay mesne profits ($22,709.68 plus $500/month ongoing) and costs of $14,000 on the claim and $14,000 on the counterclaim.
osint_notes: Family estate/adverse-possession dispute over an identified Charuma parcel (Crown Grant Vol 196 Folio 427) with mesne-profit and costs figures. Property data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: Skeete v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Curtis Skeete
individuals.notes: Retired Police Sergeant (regimental #12055); 35 years' service; unpaid pension and gratuity for over five years

related_litigation: Chandresh Sharma v AG Civil Appeal No. 52 of 2005 (binding - salary/benefits are property under s.4(a))
Samuel Friday v AG CV2021-02843 (materially similar facts)
social_headline: Retired cop's pension unpaid for 5 years - court lets his constitutional claim proceed
social_post: A retired police sergeant who served 35 years has been allowed to pursue a constitutional claim against the State for never paying his pension and gratuity. Curtis Skeete retired in September 2020, but despite about 20 phone calls and 10 visits to the Police Administration Building - on one of which he had to help find his own misplaced increment certificate - nothing was paid. The State tried to strike the claim out as a mere employment matter dressed up as a constitutional one. Justice Robin Mohammed refused: binding authority holds that earned pension and gratuity are 'property' protected by the Constitution, and the prolonged, unexplained delay (now over five years) was exceptional. The strike-out was dismissed, the State ordered to pay costs and to file its defence within 28 days. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Earned pension and gratuity are property protected by section 4(a) of the Constitution, not merely contractual entitlements
Constitutional redress is reserved for exceptional cases, but prolonged, unexplained State delay can qualify
Where no adequate parallel remedy genuinely exists, the constitutional jurisdiction may be properly invoked
outcome: The State's application to strike out dismissed. The court held the constitutional jurisdiction was properly invoked (pension/gratuity are property under s.4(a); no adequate alternative remedy; the years-long unexplained delay was exceptional). State to pay the costs of the application and file its evidence in answer within 28 days; CMC fixed for 25 June 2026.
osint_notes: Constitutional/pension claim by a retired police officer against the State. No commercial entities, property or quantified figures; no criminal content. Part of a pattern of police-pension-delay litigation.

title: Camacho Teran v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Hober Luis Camacho Teran
individuals.notes: Venezuelan/Colombian national and UNHCR asylum seeker; detained June 2019 after entering by boat; challenged a 2022 deportation order

financial_figures.what_it_is: Amount the claimant said he paid a boat captain for passage to Trinidad

related_litigation: Jesus Alexander Rodriguez Martinez v The Chief Immigration Officer [2022] UKPC 29 (cited)
social_headline: Venezuelan asylum seeker's deportation challenge fails - UNHCR card is not law here
social_post: A Venezuelan and Colombian national has lost his constitutional challenge to his deportation from Trinidad and Tobago. Hober Luis Camacho Teran entered illegally by boat in June 2019 amid a surge of Venezuelan arrivals, was briefly detained, and later served with a 2022 deportation order. He argued his arrest was unlawful and that the State should honour the 1951 Refugee Convention, a 2014 draft national refugee policy and his pending UNHCR application. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC dismissed the claim: his entry was unlawful, the Minister had a broad statutory power to deport, the Refugee Convention is not part of T&T law, the draft policy was never implemented, and a UNHCR card confers no protection from the Immigration Act. The claim was dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: International treaties like the 1951 Refugee Convention create no enforceable rights unless incorporated into domestic law
A UNHCR asylum-seeker card does not confer legal status or protect against deportation under the Immigration Act
A draft, unimplemented government policy and general political statements do not create an enforceable legitimate expectation
outcome: Claim dismissed. The detention was lawful under the Immigration Act and the deportation order a valid exercise of ministerial power; no enforceable legitimate expectation or treaty right protected the claimant, and the failure to inform him of his right to counsel caused no material prejudice. Claimant ordered to pay the State's costs.
osint_notes: Immigration/asylum constitutional challenge by a Venezuelan/Colombian national, touching on the 2014 refugee policy, UNHCR and Venezuelan migration figures. No commercial entities or property; sensitive migration topic handled as public-record fact. Limited OSINT value.

title: Chang Kit v The National Insurance Board of Trinidad and Tobago
court: High Court of Justice, Sub-Registry San Fernando, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The National Insurance Board of Trinidad and Tobago (NIB)

companies.name: The National Insurance Board of Trinidad and Tobago (NIB)
companies.ownership_notes: Administers the National Insurance Scheme; determined the claimant was not an employee of Gift & Co.

companies.name: Gift & Co.
companies.ownership_notes: Bacolet Street, Scarborough, Tobago law firm; retained the claimant as an IT consultant on a $1,250/month retainer

individuals.name: Richard Chang Kit
individuals.notes: Retired engineer who worked as an IT Systems Maintenance Manager/consultant for Gift & Co.; sought to count those contributions toward a Retirement Pension

individuals.name: Jocelyn Gift
individuals.notes: Confirmed in interview that the claimant was a consultant free to work for others, with no employee benefits

financial_figures.what_it_is: Retirement Grant the claimant was found entitled to (instead of a pension)
Monthly retainer paid by Gift & Co. to the claimant

related_litigation: National Insurance Appeals Tribunal decision of 3 August 2023 (held it had no jurisdiction - issue was one of law)
banks_financial_institutions: The National Insurance Board of Trinidad and Tobago (NIB)
social_headline: Retired IT man loses NIS pension fight - he was a consultant, not an employee
social_post: A retired engineer has lost his appeal to have years of work counted toward a National Insurance retirement pension. Richard Chang Kit did IT work for the Tobago law firm Gift & Co. on a $1,250 monthly retainer, and the firm paid NIS for him. But when he applied for a pension, the NIB found his role was a 'contract for service' - an independent consultant, not an employee. Justice Avason Quinlan-Williams, rehearing the appeal, agreed: he used his own equipment, worked only a few days a month, was free to work for others to 'sustain himself', and got no vacation or sick leave. So he qualifies only for a $63,445 Retirement Grant, not a pension. The judge urged the NIB to give detailed reasons earlier in such cases. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Whether someone is an 'employee' under a contract of service is decided by weighing many factors, not one control test
Working for multiple clients, using your own tools and receiving no benefits point toward independent-contractor status
Registering for and paying NIS creates only a presumption of employment, not an entitlement to benefits
outcome: Appeal dismissed. On a rehearing, the court found the claimant worked under a contract for service (as an IT consultant), not a contract of service, so the disputed contributions could not count toward a pension. He is entitled only to a Retirement Grant of $63,445.41. The court recommended the NIB give detailed reasons at the preliminary-determination stage.
osint_notes: National Insurance/employment-status appeal naming the NIB and a Tobago law firm (Gift & Co., partner Jocelyn Gift), with contribution and grant figures. Useful on employee-vs-contractor classification; no criminal content.

title: Sookoo v The Chief Personnel Officer
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Marsha Sookoo
individuals.notes: Forester I in the Forestry Division, Ministry of Agriculture; sought FOI documents about why her University of Idaho degree was held not equivalent to a Forestry degree for promotion to Assistant Conservator of Forests

related_litigation: Vijay Singh v Ministry of Agriculture / PSC / AG CV2016-02327 and CA S090/2017 (a colleague's similar challenge, partly successful)
social_headline: Forester wins FOI fight to see why her US degree was rejected for promotion
social_post: A government forester has largely won a Freedom of Information battle against the Chief Personnel Officer. Marsha Sookoo earned a Resource, Recreation and Tourism degree from the University of Idaho on a scholarship, but was repeatedly told it wasn't equivalent to a Forestry degree needed to become Assistant Conservator of Forests. She used FOI to seek the comparative analysis, the evaluators' details and the equivalency guidelines, and was refused. Justice Avason Quinlan-Williams held the refusals breached the FOIA: she's entitled to the assessment report (with officers' identities redacted), the job titles of the evaluators (but not their names), and the equivalency guidelines, which weren't truly exempt. The CPO was ordered to hand them over within 14 days and pay costs fit for senior counsel. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The FOIA has a strong bias toward disclosure; a public authority claiming an exemption must justify it with evidence
Deliberative internal documents can still be disclosed with personal identities redacted under s.16(2)
Job titles of officials may be disclosable in the public interest even where their names are not
outcome: Judgment largely for the claimant. The CPO's refusals under the FOIA held illegal. She is entitled to the comparative-analysis Assessment Report (redacted to protect officers' identities), the job titles/designations (not names) of the evaluators, and the equivalency guidelines. Mandamus to produce them within 14 days; costs to the claimant fit for senior and junior counsel.
osint_notes: Freedom-of-Information/public-service-promotion matter naming the Chief Personnel Officer and the Forestry Division of the Ministry of Agriculture, concerning a University of Idaho degree-equivalency dispute. No commercial entities, property or figures; no criminal content.

title: The State v Lewis, Christopher
court: High Court of Justice, Criminal Division, Port of Spain

individuals.name: Jeffrey Bonaparte
individuals.notes: Shot multiple times on 29 Dec 2023; identified the accused but the recognition was held unreliable under Turnbull

individuals.name: Dane Baker
individuals.notes: Recognition witness; admitted fabricating part of his evidence and that he did not see the accused's face during the shooting

individuals.name: Michael Baggan
individuals.notes: Recognition witness; uncertainty about his position and the quality of his observation

criminal_individuals.name: Christopher Lewis
offences: shooting with intent to do grievous bodily harm
criminal_individuals.notes: Aka 'Rockback'. Charged over the 29 Dec 2023 shooting of Jeffrey Bonaparte. Found Not Guilty on all four counts because the recognition evidence of three witnesses was held insufficiently reliable under R v Turnbull, with no firearm, forensic or CCTV evidence to support it.

related_litigation: A matter involving Tristan De Silva, in which the accused was a State witness (alleged motive for fabrication)
social_headline: 'Rockback' acquitted of attempted murder as judge finds split-second IDs unreliable
social_post: A man known as 'Rockback' has been acquitted of attempting to murder Jeffrey Bonaparte, who was shot multiple times in Port of Spain in December 2023. Three witnesses who all knew Christopher Lewis said he was the gunman, but Justice Nalini Singh applied the Turnbull caution on identification and found their evidence too weak to be sure. Bonaparte's view lasted only 'a couple of seconds' at night while he was falling under gunfire; one witness admitted fabricating part of his account and conceded he never saw the shooter's face; the third gave conflicting evidence about where he even was. With no gun recovered, no forensics and no CCTV, the judge was left with reasonable doubt. Lewis was found Not Guilty on all four counts. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Even recognition of a known person requires the Turnbull caution; honest witnesses can still be mistaken
A brief, stressful observation during gunfire is a serious weakness in identification evidence
Multiple weak identifications do not become proof beyond reasonable doubt merely by accumulation, especially without forensic or CCTV support
outcome: Not Guilty on all four counts (attempted murder, shooting with intent, possession of firearm and ammunition). The recognition evidence of the three witnesses was held unreliable under Turnbull and unsupported by any firearm, forensic or CCTV evidence; identity not proved to the criminal standard.
osint_notes: Firearms/attempted-murder prosecution ending in acquittal on identification grounds. No commercial entities, property or financial content. Tracked for crime intelligence (firearms, shooting, acquittal).

title: Massy Machinery Ltd v Jusamco Pavers Limited
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago

companies.name: Massy Machinery Ltd
directors_officers: Anjeli Narine (Vice President, Legal and General Counsel)
companies.ownership_notes: Part of the Massy group; sold equipment and maintenance services to the defendant 2017-2021

companies.name: Jusamco Pavers Limited
companies.ownership_notes: Part of the Junior Sammy Group of Companies; failed to pay invoices for equipment and services

companies.name: Junior Sammy Group of Companies
companies.ownership_notes: Group of companies whose indebtedness to Massy Machinery was discussed at a Nov 2023 meeting

individuals.name: Anjeli Narine
individuals.notes: VP Legal and General Counsel of Massy Machinery; swore the affidavits supporting summary judgment

financial_figures.what_it_is: Debt found due and owing from the defendant to the claimant
Sum demanded in the pre-action letter, partly acknowledged by the defendant
Prescribed costs of the action awarded to the claimant
Interest at 1.25% from July 2024 to judgment

social_headline: Massy wins $947k debt judgment as Jusamco's 'can't admit or deny' defence is struck out
social_post: Massy Machinery has won a near-million-dollar debt judgment against Jusamco Pavers, part of the Junior Sammy Group. Massy sold the company equipment and maintenance services between 2017 and 2021 and said it was owed $946,962.68. Jusamco's defence mostly said it could neither admit nor deny the invoices and put Massy to proof. Justice Margaret Mohammed held that, having acknowledged most of the debt in earlier correspondence and offered to pay it off in instalments, the company's 'can't admit or deny' stance was a bare denial that breached the pleading rules. She struck out the defence (except for older, statute-barred 2015-2016 invoices Massy dropped) and entered judgment for $946,962.68 plus interest and over $73,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A defence of 'can neither admit nor deny' is a bare denial that breaches CPR 10.5 unless reasons and search efforts are given
Acknowledging a debt and proposing instalment payments undermines a later attempt to dispute it
Without-prejudice settlement correspondence can be admitted to explain alleged delay in pursuing a debt
outcome: Defence struck out (save for statute-barred 2015-2016 invoices the claimant abandoned). Judgment for the claimant for $946,962.68 plus $21,394.89 interest, costs of the application, and $73,515.54 prescribed costs.
osint_notes: Commercial debt claim naming two corporate groups (Massy Machinery and the Junior Sammy Group's Jusamco Pavers) with a named in-house counsel and large financial figures, plus acknowledged inter-company indebtedness. Strong corporate-network and financial OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: The Attorney General v Boodram
court: Court of Appeal, Trinidad and Tobago
claimants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago (Appellant)

individuals.name: Daisy Boodram
individuals.notes: Won declarations at first instance about accumulation of leave under reg 78 CSR and reg 97 PSCR; partly upheld on appeal

related_litigation: High Court CV2022-04537 (Rampersad J, order 9 Jan 2024)
social_headline: Appeal Court trims public officer's win over accumulated-leave entitlements
social_post: The Court of Appeal has partly overturned a ruling on how public officers accumulate leave. Daisy Boodram had won declarations from Justice Rampersad about the combined effect of regulation 78 of the Civil Service Regulations and regulation 97 of the Public Service Commission Regulations, which govern how much annual leave an officer can build up. The Attorney General appealed. The Appeal Court, led by Chief Justice Boodoosingh with Justices Lucky and Rahim, allowed the appeal in part - setting aside three of the declarations and affirming the other three. Costs are to be argued separately. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Annual-leave accumulation for public officers is governed by reg 78 CSR and reg 97 PSCR, read together
An appeal court may affirm some declarations and set others aside where each turns on a distinct point
outcome: Appeal allowed in part: declarations 1.1, 1.2 and 1.5 of the trial judge's order set aside; declarations 1.3, 1.4 and 1.6 affirmed. Parties to be heard on costs.
osint_notes: Public-service/constitutional appeal on leave-accumulation regulations. No commercial entities, property or financial figures; no criminal content.

title: DCM Acute Stroke and Vascular Intervention Centre Limited v Howida Limited
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago
claimants: DCM Acute Stroke and Vascular Intervention Centre Limited

companies.name: DCM Acute Stroke and Vascular Intervention Centre Limited
companies.ownership_notes: Medical (stroke/vascular) centre company; relieved from a deemed admission of the counterclaim

companies.name: Howida Limited
companies.ownership_notes: Defendant whose judgment-on-admission application was dismissed

individuals.name: Dr. Conrad Murray
individuals.notes: Swore the affidavit supporting the claimant's relief-from-sanctions application

social_headline: Stroke centre dodges automatic loss, wins relief from sanctions against Howida
social_post: A procedural skirmish between DCM Acute Stroke and Vascular Intervention Centre and Howida Limited has gone the medical company's way. DCM had missed the deadline to file a defence to Howida's counterclaim, which would normally mean it was deemed to admit the counterclaim. Howida applied for judgment on that admission; DCM applied for relief from sanctions. Justice Devindra Rampersad relieved DCM from the sanction and extended its time to file, then dismissed Howida's application. DCM still has to pay the costs of both applications, and the case proceeds to a case management conference. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Failing to file a defence to a counterclaim in time can mean being deemed to have admitted it
Relief from sanctions turns on promptness, intentionality and a good explanation for the breach
Even a successful relief-from-sanctions applicant usually bears the costs of its own application
outcome: Claimant relieved from the sanction of being deemed to admit the counterclaim; time to file its defence to counterclaim extended. Defendant's judgment-on-admission application dismissed. Claimant to pay costs of both applications; matter set for a CMC on 30 June 2026.
osint_notes: Procedural ruling naming two companies (a stroke/vascular medical centre and Howida Limited) and a director. Corporate data of OSINT value; no criminal content or quantified figures.

title: Jankie v The Board of Inland Revenue
court: Court of Appeal, Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Jagdeo Jankie
individuals.notes: Private house-construction contractor of 126 Hindustan Road, New Grant; disputed his 2013 income-tax assessment

financial_figures.what_it_is: Grand total of taxes, interest and levy assessed against the appellant for income year 2013
Tax component of the assessment
Interest component of the assessment

related_litigation: Tax Appeal Board ruling of 16 April 2024 (Tax Appeal No. I 33 of 2022)
social_headline: Contractor loses appeal over a $716k income-tax assessment for 2013
social_post: A New Grant building contractor has lost his appeal against a large income-tax bill. Jagdeo Jankie challenged the Board of Inland Revenue's assessment of additional income tax for 2013, arguing the BIR's communications were too unclear to tell him how the figures were worked out. The Tax Appeal Board dismissed his application, and the Court of Appeal - with Justice Mark Mohammed writing - agreed: although the BIR's figures could have been presented more tidily, the tables, schedules and tax objection report gave him enough detail to understand the roughly $716,000 in taxes, interest and levy claimed. The appeal was dismissed; costs to be argued. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A tax assessment is not void for uncertainty merely because the figures were communicated untidily, if adequate particulars were given
The taxpayer must be able to understand the basis of the computation, not receive it in perfect form
Appeal courts are slow to overturn the Tax Appeal Board's findings unless plainly wrong
outcome: Appeal dismissed. The Board of Inland Revenue gave the appellant adequate particulars of his 2013 assessment (about $716,309 in tax, interest and levy); the assessment was not uncertain. Parties to be heard on costs.
osint_notes: Income-tax appeal naming an individual contractor (with business address) and the Board of Inland Revenue, with specific assessment figures. Financial/taxpayer data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: Pinto v The Arima Borough Corporation
court: High Court of Justice, San Fernando, Trinidad and Tobago

companies.name: The Arima Borough Corporation
companies.ownership_notes: Municipal corporation; counterclaimed unjust enrichment over the claimant's pay

individuals.name: Arnold Pinto
individuals.notes: Employee who has not reported to duty since 7 August 2018; suing over pay; failed to file a defence to the corporation's counterclaim in time

social_headline: Both sides lose their applications in Arima Corporation pay dispute
social_post: A procedural standoff between Arnold Pinto and the Arima Borough Corporation has ended with neither side getting what it wanted. Pinto, who the Corporation says has not reported to duty since August 2018, sued over his pay; the Corporation counterclaimed that paying him for services not rendered would be unjust enrichment. Pinto filed his defence to that counterclaim late and sought relief from sanctions; the Corporation asked the court to treat the counterclaim as admitted. Justice Karen Reid dismissed both applications and struck out the late defence, finding the unjust-enrichment counterclaim depended on the outcome of the main claim and could only be decided at trial. Each side bears its own costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A counterclaim that merely anticipates the outcome of the main claim may disclose no independent cause of action
Relief from sanctions can be refused even where the opposing 'deem admitted' application also fails
Unjust-enrichment claims contingent on a trial finding are dealt with after, not before, trial
outcome: Both applications dismissed: the claimant's relief-from-sanctions/extension application refused (and his late defence to counterclaim struck out), and the defendant's application to deem the counterclaim admitted also dismissed. Each party to bear its own costs.
osint_notes: Employment/procedural dispute against a municipal corporation. No commercial entities beyond the public body, no property or financial figures, no criminal content.

title: In re an application by Suruj Samaroo (Samaroo v The Attorney General)
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Suruj Samaroo
individuals.notes: Mechanic and farmer of Carlsen Field Connector Road, Chaguanas; arrested, detained and prosecuted in 2015 over a charge a Magistrate had already dismissed in 2005

financial_figures.what_it_is: Compensatory damages for breach of constitutional rights
Vindicatory damages

related_litigation: Magistrate's Court Case No. 3047/02 (charge dismissed 18 October 2005)
social_headline: State pays $225k for arresting a man over a charge thrown out a decade earlier
social_post: A Chaguanas mechanic and farmer has won $225,000 from the State after being arrested, detained and prosecuted in 2015 on a criminal charge that a Magistrate had already dismissed back in 2005. Suruj Samaroo brought a constitutional claim; the State filed no evidence, so his account stood uncontradicted. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC found his rights to liberty, protection of the law and a fair hearing were breached, and that the systemic failures - prosecuting him on a dead charge despite his repeated protests, with no explanation from the State - made the violation grave. She awarded $200,000 compensatory and $25,000 vindicatory damages, plus interest and costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Re-prosecuting someone on a charge already dismissed can breach constitutional rights to liberty and protection of the law
Where the State files no evidence, the claimant's uncontradicted affidavit is accepted
Vindicatory damages may be added where compensation alone does not mark the gravity of a rights breach
outcome: Claim succeeds. Declared that the claimant's rights under ss.4(a), 4(b), 5(2)(c)(ii) and 5(2)(h) were contravened by his 2015 arrest and prosecution on a 2005-dismissed charge. State ordered to pay $200,000 compensatory and $25,000 vindicatory damages, plus 2.5% interest from May 2015 and costs.
osint_notes: Constitutional/wrongful-prosecution claim against the State. No commercial entities or property; damages figures only. Crime context: an old dismissed charge wrongly revived (no new charge).

title: The State v Als, Joseph
court: High Court of Justice, Criminal Division, Port of Spain

individuals.name: Marvin Hosten
individuals.notes: Nephew of the offender; stabbed to death on 11 May 2020 at Edwin Roberts Crescent, La Horquetta

criminal_individuals.name: Joseph Als
criminal_individuals.notes: Aka 'Joe'. Charged with the murder of his nephew Marvin Hosten (stabbed multiple times with a knife, 11 May 2020). Pleaded guilty to manslaughter on the basis of diminished responsibility. Sentenced by Justice Nalini Singh to 8 years 8 months' imprisonment (16-year starting point reduced to 13 for mitigation, then a one-third guilty-plea discount), with the full pre-trial custody (~4 years 3 months) deducted; bonded $5,000 to keep the peace for 3 years on release.

financial_figures.what_it_is: Sum in which the offender is bonded to keep the peace for 3 years on release

social_headline: La Horquetta man jailed for stabbing nephew to death; murder reduced to manslaughter
social_post: Joseph Als, known as 'Joe', has been sentenced for killing his nephew Marvin Hosten at La Horquetta in May 2020. On the morning of the stabbing his sister noticed him acting strangely with a knife wrapped in a bandana and warned her son to be careful; soon after she left for work, the two had an altercation in the yard and Als stabbed Hosten multiple times. Charged with murder, Als pleaded guilty to manslaughter on the basis of diminished responsibility. Justice Nalini Singh set a 16-year starting point, reduced it to 13 years for his mental condition, applied a one-third plea discount to 8 years 8 months, and deducted his time on remand. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A murder charge can be reduced to manslaughter where diminished responsibility is established by a plea agreement
Sentencing starts from a notional starting point, adjusted for mitigation, then a guilty-plea discount, then credit for time served
Courts may add a peace bond and probation conditions (psychiatric follow-up, no alcohol/drugs) on release
outcome: Pleaded guilty to manslaughter (diminished responsibility) on a murder indictment. Sentenced to 8 years 8 months' imprisonment (16-year starting point reduced to 13 for mitigation, then a one-third plea discount), with full pre-trial custody (~4 years 3 months) deducted; bonded $5,000 to keep the peace for 3 years on release with probation/psychiatric conditions.
osint_notes: Domestic manslaughter (knife) sentencing. No commercial entities or property; tracked for crime intelligence (homicide reduced to manslaughter, diminished responsibility).

title: Ramsamooj, Vishnu; Ramdhan, Jairam v Ramsamooj, Lilawatie; Jagessar, Roshni; Burke, Richard
judges: The Hon. Mr. Justice Westmin R.A. James

individuals.name: Lilawatie Ramsamooj
individuals.notes: Occupier; counterclaimed equitable interest via proprietary estoppel (dismissed); ordered to vacate within 90 days

individuals.name: Richard Burke aka Lennard/Lennon Burke
individuals.notes: Restrained from entering or remaining on the subject lands

Prescribed costs on the claim payable to the claimants
Prescribed costs on the counterclaim payable to the claimants

property_lease.address_or_description: Agricultural lands at L.P. 8/1 Geerahoo Trace, PCOL Village, Fyzabad (with a wooden chattel dwelling)
property_lease.lease_terms: Claimants asserted tenancy/superior possessory title; defendants claimed equitable interest via long occupation and a gratuitous family licence
property_lease.landlord: Claimants Vishnu Ramsamooj and Jairam Ramdhan (asserting superior title)
property_lease.tenant: Defendants Lilawatie Ramsamooj and Roshni Jagessar (occupiers)
property_lease.outcome: Possession granted to claimants; first and second defendants to vacate within 90 days (may remove their chattel structure); proprietary-estoppel counterclaim dismissed; 90-day stay of execution

social_headline: Fyzabad family land feud: court orders relatives off agricultural plot, dismisses estoppel claim
social_post: A bitter family dispute over agricultural land at Geerahoo Trace, Fyzabad has ended with Justice Westmin James ordering Lilawatie Ramsamooj and Roshni Jagessar to give up possession to Vishnu Ramsamooj and Jairam Ramdhan within 90 days. The defendants' counterclaim that long occupation and money spent on a house gave them an equitable interest through proprietary estoppel was dismissed. The judge issued mutual restraining orders to keep the warring relatives apart, barred a third party, Richard 'Lennon' Burke, from the land, and awarded the claimants $5,000 nominal trespass damages plus costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A gratuitous family licence to occupy land can be revoked; long occupation alone does not confer ownership
Proprietary estoppel requires more than expenditure — a clear assurance and detrimental reliance must be proved
Courts can pair a possession order with mutual restraining orders where family relations have broken down
outcome: Possession granted to the claimants. First and second defendants ordered to vacate within 90 days (free to remove their wooden chattel structure); counterclaim for equitable interest by proprietary estoppel dismissed; mutual restraining orders made; third defendant restrained from the land; $5,000 nominal trespass damages and $14,000 + $14,000 costs awarded to claimants; 90-day stay of execution.
osint_notes: Private family land-possession dispute (Fyzabad agricultural plot). No commercial entities or banks; relevant only for property/title tracking.

title: Tiwari, Leslie; Noreiga, Robert v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
judges: The Hon. Mr. Justice Robin N. Mohammed
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Leslie Tiwari
individuals.notes: Prisoner who obtained constitutional declarations of unconstitutional delay, arbitrary detention and cruel and unusual punishment over the failure to review his sentence; awarded damages on this assessment

individuals.name: Robert Noreiga
individuals.notes: Co-applicant in materially similar matter; awarded $1,500,000 compensatory and $150,000 vindicatory damages

financial_figures.what_it_is: Compensatory damages awarded to Robert Noreiga for breaches of constitutional rights
Vindicatory damages awarded to each applicant (Tiwari and Noreiga)

related_litigation: Companion constitutional motion CV2019-04016 (Robert Noreiga), tried jointly with CV2019-04011 (Leslie Tiwari)
social_headline: State to pay millions for failing to review prisoners' sentences
social_post: The State has been ordered to pay substantial constitutional damages to two prisoners, Leslie Tiwari and Robert Noreiga, after the High Court found their rights were breached by unconstitutional delay, arbitrary detention and cruel and unusual punishment in failing to review their sentences. On this assessment of damages Justice Robin Mohammed awarded Robert Noreiga $1.5 million in compensatory damages, plus $150,000 in vindicatory damages to each man to mark the gravity of the breaches and deter repetition, with costs certified for senior and junior counsel. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Prisoners are entitled to regular reviews of their sentences after four years, conducted with natural-justice safeguards
Unconstitutional delay and arbitrary detention can attract very large compensatory awards against the State
Vindicatory damages are awarded on top of compensation to emphasise the constitutional rights and deter future breaches
outcome: Assessment of damages for two jointly-tried constitutional motions. Robert Noreiga awarded $1,500,000 compensatory damages; each applicant awarded $150,000 vindicatory damages; costs certified fit for two counsel, to be assessed in default of agreement.
osint_notes: Large constitutional damages payout by the State over prison sentence-review failures. Useful for tracking government liability; no private commercial entities.

title: Singh, Troy v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
judges: The Hon. Mr. Justice Westmin R.A. James
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Troy Singh
individuals.notes: Public officer seconded from the Prison Service to Customs and Excise; State discontinued his pensionable allowance then demanded $122,108 repayment more than a decade later

individuals.name: Stephen Jack
individuals.notes: Officer in materially indistinguishable circumstances who earlier obtained constitutional relief over the same allowance; relied on to show unequal treatment

financial_figures.what_it_is: Repayment the State demanded as alleged overpayments (memorandum set aside, null and void)
Compensatory damages awarded to the claimant
Aggravated damages awarded to the claimant
Vindicatory damages awarded to the claimant
Total damages awarded

related_litigation: Stephen Jack constitutional claim concerning the same pensionable allowance (relied on as comparator)
social_headline: State punished for clawing back officer's allowance while sparing a colleague
social_post: Justice Westmin James has found the State breached the constitutional rights of public officer Troy Singh to protection of the law and equality of treatment. After Singh was seconded from the Prison Service to Customs and Excise, the State stopped his pensionable allowance, then a decade later branded the payments overpayments and demanded $122,108 back — even though a colleague in identical circumstances, Stephen Jack, had already won constitutional relief over the very same allowance. The court set aside the repayment demand and awarded Singh $172,154 in total damages ($97,154 compensatory, $25,000 aggravated, $50,000 vindicatory), and ordered his pension and benefits recalculated. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The State must treat materially similar public officers consistently; differential treatment can breach the right to equality
A long-delayed demand to recharacterize past payments as overpayments can be struck down as unconstitutional
Aggravated and vindicatory damages may be added to compensation where State conduct is high-handed
outcome: Constitutional motion succeeded. Repayment demand of $122,108 set aside as null and void; claimant awarded $172,154 total damages ($97,154 compensatory, $25,000 aggravated, $50,000 vindicatory) plus interest; pension, gratuity and retirement benefits to be recalculated within 90 days; costs to claimant; 42-day stay.
osint_notes: Constitutional/equality ruling against the State with a named comparator (Stephen Jack). Tracks government liability over public-service pension allowances; no private commercial entities.

social_headline: Court enforces land settlement, orders six-figure payout to realty firm
social_post: Justice Westmin James has enforced a compromise agreement struck between Rajesh and Shinel Baksh and Hadeed Mohammed over a parcel of land (Lot B9), finding that correspondence in late 2025 — the acceptance of 'Option III' — formed a binding settlement. The court directed the parties to execute their respective conveyances and ordered Mohammed to pay Second Haven Realty Limited $234,100 within 60 days. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: An exchange of correspondence accepting a defined option can form a binding, enforceable settlement
Courts will hold parties to a compromise even where the underlying claim is stayed
A defaulting party's conveyance can be executed by the Registrar on their behalf
outcome: Application to enforce a compromise agreement succeeded. Parties ordered to execute deeds conveying their respective interests in Lot B9; defendant to pay Second Haven Realty Limited $234,100 within 60 days; claimants to pay $5,500 costs; 28-day stay.
osint_notes: Land sale/compromise enforcement involving realty company Second Haven Realty Limited. Useful for property/company tracking; no banks or criminal element.

individuals.notes: Built a substantial permanent dwelling on land she did not own; claim dismissed and denied compensation for failing to secure title, formalise a sale, or use s.9 Land Tenants Act, and for not renewing the statutory tenancy

individuals.name: Gyandavi Vera Raghoo
individuals.notes: Estate represented by Administrator ad Litem Rohini Baboo; held to be entitled to the land

social_headline: Woman who built house on another's land gets nothing after failing to secure title
social_post: Justice Ricky Rahim has dismissed Elizabeth Ajodha's claim over a 5,000 sq ft parcel and given judgment to the estate of Gyandavi Vera Raghoo. The court found Ajodha built a substantial, permanent home on land she did not own without first securing the title in writing, signing a formal agreement for sale, using the protection of section 9 of the Land Tenants Act, or even renewing her statutory tenancy — risks she took on herself. She was awarded no compensation for the house and ordered to pay $28,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Building on land you do not own, without securing title first, is done at your own risk and may attract no compensation
Statutory tenants must renew their tenancy and can use s.9 of the Land Tenants Act to protect improvements
Equity will not rescue a party who ignored elementary legal precautions
outcome: Reasons for an earlier order: claim dismissed; judgment for the defendant on the counterclaim declaring the statutory tenancy; no compensation for the dwelling house; claimant to pay $28,000 total costs.
osint_notes: Private land/statutory-tenancy dispute. Company named only as a boundary; no commercial or criminal significance.

title: Siberan, Rawle; Siberan, Sasha v Prabhudial, Shatri
court: High Court of Justice, Sub-Registry San Fernando
judges: The Hon. Madame Justice Karen Reid

individuals.name: Rawle Siberan
individuals.notes: Co-owner of Lot 3, Chase Village, Chaguanas; sued for specific performance of a $1,050,000 sale agreement but could not show good marketable title

individuals.name: Shatri Prabhudial
individuals.notes: Agreed to buy the land; did not complete, citing title issues and 'working out his finances'; deposit cheque went stale in escrow

financial_figures.what_it_is: Agreed sale price of Lot 3, Chase Village, Chaguanas
Deposit held in escrow by the defendant's attorney (cheque became stale)

property_lease.address_or_description: Lot 3, Chase Village, Chaguanas
property_lease.lease_terms: Agreement for sale dated 14 May 2019; completion within 90 days (by 12 August 2019)
property_lease.outcome: Sale not completed; agreement declared terminated by mutual agreement around 30 October 2019; both claim and counterclaim dismissed; each party bears own costs

social_headline: $1m Chaguanas land sale collapses; court says deal ended by mutual agreement
social_post: A claim for specific performance of a $1.05 million land sale in Chase Village, Chaguanas has failed. Justice Karen Reid found that vendors Rawle and Sasha Siberan could not show a good and marketable title, while purchaser Shatri Prabhudial had not completed, citing title concerns and his finances. With the $105,000 deposit cheque having gone stale in escrow, the court held the agreement was terminated by mutual agreement in October 2019, dismissed both the claim and counterclaim, and ordered each side to bear its own costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A vendor cannot obtain specific performance without proving a good and marketable title
A purchaser's remedy where title is defective may be limited to a refund of the deposit, not damages
Conduct can lead a court to find a sale agreement was terminated by mutual agreement
outcome: Both claim and counterclaim dismissed. The 14 May 2019 sale agreement declared terminated by mutual agreement around 30 October 2019; no money ordered paid (deposit remained with the defendant via a stale escrow cheque); each party to bear its own costs.
osint_notes: Private failed land-sale dispute. No companies, banks, or criminal element; routine property litigation.

title: In re continuous employment of Carla Trotman Parks v The Tobago House of Assembly
judges: The Hon. Mr. Justice Kevin Ramcharan
claimants: Carla Trotman Parks aka Carla Parks

individuals.name: Carla Trotman Parks
individuals.notes: Data Clerk on short-term contracts with the THA (2011-2018); brought a constitutional claim over the salary scale, gratuity and leave entitlements applied to her; claim struck out as a matter for the Industrial Court

social_headline: Tobago data clerk's pay grievance struck out — court says it belongs in Industrial Court
social_post: Justice Kevin Ramcharan has struck out a constitutional claim brought by Carla Trotman Parks, a former data clerk with the Tobago House of Assembly, over how her salary scale, gratuity and leave entitlements were calculated during years of short-term contracts. The court held that disputes about workers' terms and conditions generally belong before the Industrial Court as a trade dispute, and that invoking the constitutional jurisdiction was a misuse of process. But, noting the THA never gave Parks the courtesy of an official response over many years, the judge departed from the usual rule and made no order as to costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Workplace terms-and-conditions disputes generally belong in the Industrial Court, not a constitutional motion
Using the constitutional jurisdiction for what is really a trade dispute can be struck out as a misuse of process
A court may decline to award costs against a struck-out claimant where the public body behaved poorly
outcome: Claim struck out as a matter that should have been brought before the Industrial Court as a trade dispute; no order as to costs (court critical of the THA's failure to respond to the claimant over many years).
osint_notes: Public-sector employment/constitutional jurisdiction dispute against the Tobago House of Assembly. No commercial entities, banks or criminal element.

title: Wharton, Virgil v The Public Service Appeal Board
judges: The Hon. Mr. Justice Robin N. Mohammed

individuals.name: Virgil Wharton
individuals.notes: Former police constable dismissed by the Police Service Commission in 2004 following a larceny charge that a Magistrate dismissed without conviction in February 2004; won judicial review nearly two decades later

related_litigation: Underlying Police Service Commission dismissal decision (2004) and Public Service Appeal Board appeal decision (12 September 2019)
social_headline: Ex-constable wins after 20-year fight: court says firing breached his rights
social_post: Nearly two decades after he was dismissed from the Trinidad and Tobago Police Service, former constable Virgil Wharton has won his judicial review. He had been fired by the Police Service Commission in 2004 over a larceny charge that a magistrate had already dismissed without any conviction, and the Public Service Appeal Board upheld the dismissal. Justice Robin Mohammed declared that dismissing him without a proper hearing breached his constitutional rights to the protection of the law and equality before the law, and that the Appeal Board erred in affirming it. Compensation — including lost salary and damages for distress — was referred to a Master for assessment. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A public officer must be given a fair hearing before dismissal; skipping mandatory procedures can be unconstitutional
A dismissed criminal charge (no conviction) is a weak foundation for disciplinary dismissal
Where rights are breached, compensation can include lost salary plus damages for distress, assessed by a Master
outcome: Judicial review succeeded. Declarations granted that the PSC's dismissal without a fair hearing and without following s.129(6) breached the claimant's rights under s.4(b) of the Constitution and that the PSAB erred in affirming it; compensation referred to a Master (lost salary/allowances plus distress damages) unless agreed within 60 days; defendant to pay costs.
osint_notes: Long-running public-service/police dismissal judicial review. No commercial entities or banks; criminal charge (larceny) was dismissed without conviction.

title: Walker, Damion v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
judges: The Hon. Mr. Justice Robin N. Mohammed
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Damion Walker
individuals.notes: 41-year-old Jamaican national who was prosecuted for Robbery with Aggravation over a 2 March 2016 incident at a Lowlands, Tobago supermarket; criminal proceedings dismissed in his favour on 30 October 2018; his civil claim for malicious prosecution failed

individuals.name: PC Marcus Turpin
individuals.notes: Police constable who charged the claimant; the court did not find he lacked reasonable and probable cause or acted with malice

financial_figures.what_it_is: Prescribed costs payable by the claimant to the State after his claim was dismissed

related_litigation: Underlying criminal prosecution for Robbery with Aggravation (dismissed in the claimant's favour, 30 October 2018)
social_headline: Jamaican man's malicious-prosecution claim against the State thrown out
social_post: Damion Walker, a Jamaican national who was living in Tobago, sued the State for malicious prosecution after he was charged with Robbery with Aggravation over a 2016 supermarket incident in Lowlands — a charge later dismissed in his favour. Justice Robin Mohammed dismissed his civil claim, finding he had not shown that the prosecuting officer, PC Marcus Turpin, lacked reasonable and probable cause or acted with malice. Walker was ordered to pay $14,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A malicious-prosecution claim requires proof both of absence of reasonable and probable cause and of malice
An acquittal or dismissed charge does not by itself establish that a prosecution was malicious
Unsuccessful claimants in unquantified claims can be fixed with prescribed costs (here $14,000)
outcome: Claim for malicious prosecution dismissed. The court found the claimant failed to prove the prosecuting officer lacked reasonable and probable cause or acted with malice; claimant ordered to pay $14,000 prescribed costs.
osint_notes: Failed malicious-prosecution claim against the State (robbery charge previously dismissed). No commercial entities or banks; routine civil litigation.

title: Hospedales, Louann v Ryan-Brathwaite, Jacqueline (Wills and Probate Act s.74)
judges: The Hon. Mr. Justice Westmin R.A. James

individuals.name: Louann Hospedales
individuals.notes: Niece and co-beneficiary of the deceased; alleged she had not received her full benefit and sought an account of the estate

individuals.name: Jacqueline Ryan-Brathwaite
individuals.notes: Niece, co-beneficiary and sole executrix of the estate; court found no breach of fiduciary duty or maladministration

individuals.name: Merle Evelyn Harvey
individuals.notes: Died 1 July 2014; estate the subject of the accounting dispute; held an RBC account at 11 3rd Street, Springland, Gasparillo

banks_financial_institutions: RBC Bank Trinidad and Tobago Limited (account no. 100082075005818 in the name of Merle Evelyn Harvey / jointly with Raymond A. Harvey)
social_headline: Court dismisses niece's estate-accounting claim, allows bank inquiry by consent
social_post: A dispute between two nieces over the estate of the late Merle Evelyn Harvey has ended with Justice Westmin James dismissing the claim. Louann Hospedales had alleged she never received her full share and sought a formal accounting from her cousin Jacqueline Ryan-Brathwaite, the sole executrix. The court found no evidence of undisclosed assets, unaccounted rental income, or any breach of fiduciary duty. By consent, Hospedales was authorised to inquire, at her own expense, into the deceased's RBC bank account for any sums that might be due to the estate. No order as to costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A beneficiary seeking an estate account must point to evidence of undisclosed assets or maladministration
Courts can allow a beneficiary to make targeted bank inquiries by consent without finding wrongdoing
An executor who keeps proper records can defeat allegations of breach of fiduciary duty
outcome: Claim dismissed; court found no undisclosed assets, unaccounted income, or breach of fiduciary duty by the executrix. By consent, the claimant authorised to inquire (at her own expense) into the deceased's RBC account for sums due to the estate; no order as to costs.
osint_notes: Private estate/probate accounting dispute. References an RBC Bank Trinidad and Tobago account of the deceased; no commercial entities or criminal element.

social_post: Justice Robin Mohammed has granted summary judgment to Kennedy De Coteau, registered owner of Lot #30 Seeree Trace, Sangre Grande, ordering Lawrence Hastick to give up possession within 60 days. Hastick, the son of a former statutory tenant, argued he had a real defence based on proprietary estoppel and an alleged promise of sale, but the court held that defence had no realistic prospect of success and did not warrant a trial. He was ordered to pay the owner's costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Summary judgment can dispose of a weak proprietary-estoppel defence without a full trial
A vague alleged 'promise of sale' without supporting evidence may not raise a triable issue
The child of a former statutory tenant does not automatically inherit a right to occupy
