property_lease.address_or_description: Land at L.P. No. 1123 Eastern Main Road, Manzanilla, approx. 5,500 sq ft (portion of a larger ~16-acre parcel)
property_lease.lease_terms: Disputed ownership/possession; claimants established title
property_lease.outcome: Trespass proved; defendant ordered to deliver possession, remove poles, and restrained by injunction; damages, interest and costs awarded to claimants

social_headline: Manzanilla landowners win trespass case; squatter ordered off and to pay $30,000
social_post: After an eight-year battle, Dexter and Michelle Alexis have won their trespass claim over a parcel of land at Manzanilla. Justice Robin Mohammed found that Jeffrey James had trespassed on their land, ordered him to give up possession and remove the poles he had erected, and granted an injunction barring him from blocking the couple's access. He was ordered to pay $30,000 in damages plus interest and $14,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A landowner who proves title can recover possession, damages and an injunction against a trespasser
Pre-judgment interest can add up over a long-running case (here nearly 8 years)
Courts can order a trespasser to remove structures (e.g. poles/fencing) erected on the land
outcome: Judgment for the claimants. Defendant found to have trespassed; ordered to deliver up possession, remove poles, and restrained by injunction; awarded $30,000 damages plus $5,773.97 interest and $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Private land-trespass/possession dispute (Manzanilla). No companies, banks or criminal element.

title: McLetchie, Samuel v McLetchie, Stella Elizabeth
judges: The Hon. Mr. Justice Robin N. Mohammed
defendants: Stella Elizabeth McLetchie aka Stella Elizabeth Wint

individuals.name: Samuel McLetchie
individuals.notes: Elderly brother who claimed ownership of family land at Signal Hill, Tobago; claim dismissed save for the one lot on which his dwelling house stands

individuals.name: Stella Elizabeth McLetchie aka Stella Elizabeth Wint
individuals.notes: Elderly sister and registered owner of the subject parcel; succeeded on her counterclaim and obtained an injunction against her brother

financial_figures.what_it_is: Prescribed costs of the claim payable by the claimant
Prescribed costs of the counterclaim payable by the claimant

property_lease.address_or_description: Land at Makala Hill Drive, Signal Hill, Tobago, approx. 1,405.6 sq m (Memorandum of Transfer Vol. 1257 Folio 128 Inst. No. 74)
property_lease.lease_terms: Disputed ownership between siblings; defendant the registered owner
property_lease.outcome: Claimant declared to have no entitlement except the single lot on which his dwelling sits; permanent injunction in the defendant's favour barring him from the rest of the parcel; claimant to pay costs

social_headline: Tobago siblings' land feud: brother kept off sister's land except his own house lot
social_post: A land dispute between an elderly brother and sister at Signal Hill, Tobago has gone largely against the brother. Samuel McLetchie claimed ownership of the family parcel, but Justice Robin Mohammed dismissed his claim and gave judgment to his sister, Stella, the registered owner, on her counterclaim. The court declared Samuel had no right to the land except the single lot on which his own dwelling house stands, granted a permanent injunction keeping him off the rest of the parcel, and ordered him to pay $28,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Long occupation of family land does not override a registered owner's title
A court can recognise a limited right to the lot under one's home while denying any claim to the wider parcel
Losing both a claim and a counterclaim can mean paying two sets of prescribed costs
outcome: Claim dismissed; judgment for the defendant on her counterclaim. Declaration that the claimant has no entitlement to the Signal Hill parcel except the single lot on which his dwelling house stands; permanent injunction in the defendant's favour; claimant to pay $14,000 + $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Private sibling land-ownership dispute (Signal Hill, Tobago). No companies, banks or criminal element.

title: Telecommunications Services of Trinidad and Tobago v Telecommunications Authority of Trinidad and Tobago
claimants: Telecommunications Services of Trinidad and Tobago Limited (TSTT) - Appellant/Claimant
defendants: Telecommunications Authority of Trinidad and Tobago (TATT) - Respondent/Defendant

companies.name: Telecommunications Services of Trinidad and Tobago Limited (TSTT)
companies.ownership_notes: National telecommunications provider; challenged the regulator's 2021 interconnection Determination

companies.name: Telecommunications Authority of Trinidad and Tobago (TATT)
companies.ownership_notes: Regulator established under the Telecommunications Act Ch. 47:21; published Determination 2021/01 (16 March 2021) and a 2021 Interconnection Benchmarking Study

related_litigation: High Court Claim No. CV2021-02135 (judicial review of Determination 2021/01)
social_headline: Appeal Court quashes telecoms regulator's 2021 interconnection ruling, orders consultation
social_post: The Court of Appeal has sided with TSTT in its battle with the telecommunications regulator, TATT. At issue was TATT's Determination 2021/01, published in March 2021 alongside an interconnection benchmarking study, which set price-regulation terms for the sector. Delivering the court's judgment, Justice of Appeal Peter Rajkumar (with Henderson and Rahim JJA agreeing) granted certiorari quashing the Determination and ordered TATT, by mandamus, to publish the benchmarking study and a draft determination for proper consultation with reasonable timelines. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A regulator must follow its own published consultation procedures before making binding determinations
Courts can quash a regulatory determination (certiorari) and compel a fresh, consultative process (mandamus)
Procedural fairness applies even to expert economic/price regulation of the telecoms sector
outcome: Appeal allowed. Order of certiorari quashing TATT's Determination 2021/01; order of mandamus compelling TATT to publish the 2021 Interconnection Benchmarking Study and a draft determination for consultation in accordance with its published procedures and reasonable timelines.
osint_notes: Significant Court of Appeal regulatory ruling involving the national telecoms provider (TSTT) and the regulator (TATT). Relevant for sector/commercial intelligence; no banks or criminal element.

title: Antoine, Leroy; Antoine, Sumintra Judy v Brown, Jennifer (Administratrix of Irma Agatha Brown); Phillip, Desmond
judges: The Hon. Madame Justice Karen Reid
defendants: Jennifer Brown (Administratrix ad Litem of the Estate of Irma Agatha Brown)

individuals.name: Leroy Antoine
individuals.notes: Claimed adverse possession of ~6 acres of agricultural land at Las Lomas; succeeded after the court found 16+ years' exclusive occupation

individuals.name: Irma Agatha Brown
individuals.notes: Original owner of the disputed lands; estate represented by Administratrix Jennifer Brown

individuals.name: Desmond Phillip
individuals.notes: Held a power of attorney from the first defendant; alleged to have trespassed on and damaged crops on the disputed lands from 2014

financial_figures.what_it_is: Costs of the claim payable to the claimants
Costs of the counterclaim payable by the first defendant to the claimants

property_lease.address_or_description: Two parcels (~6 acres total) of agricultural land at No. 2 Ramoo Trace East, Las Lomas #21, Ward of Cunupia (Deed No. 9982 of 1971)
property_lease.lease_terms: Claimants rented 1977-1980 then occupied adversely without rent after the owner abandoned the land
property_lease.landlord: Estate of Irma Agatha Brown (paper owner)
property_lease.tenant: Leroy and Sumintra Judy Antoine (adverse possessors)
property_lease.outcome: Claimants declared to have been in undisturbed, exclusive and continuous occupation for more than 16 years; judgment for the claimants; counterclaim dismissed

social_headline: Couple win 6 acres at Las Lomas through adverse possession after decades of occupation
social_post: Leroy and Sumintra Judy Antoine have won title to roughly six acres of agricultural land at Las Lomas through adverse possession. Justice Karen Reid found that after renting the land between 1977 and 1980, the couple stayed on it rent-free for decades after the owner abandoned it, in undisturbed and exclusive occupation for well over the 16 years the law requires. The court gave judgment for the Antoines against the estate of the late Irma Agatha Brown and Desmond Phillip, dismissed the estate's counterclaim, and awarded the couple costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Sixteen years of undisturbed, exclusive, continuous occupation can defeat a paper owner's title
A tenancy that ends can ripen into adverse possession once rent stops and the owner abandons the land
A power of attorney from the paper owner does not defeat an established adverse-possession claim
outcome: Judgment for the claimants. Declaration that they were in undisturbed, exclusive and continuous occupation of the ~6-acre disputed lands for more than 16 years (adverse possession); first defendant's counterclaim dismissed; defendants to pay the claimants' costs ($14,000 claim + $14,000 counterclaim).
osint_notes: Private adverse-possession land dispute (Las Lomas agricultural land). No companies, banks or criminal element.

title: Jackson, Llewellyn v Jackson, Ernest; Jackson Ghent, Maxine
judges: The Hon. Mr. Justice Devindra Rampersad

individuals.name: Llewellyn Jackson
individuals.notes: Built/financed an annex on family property and was evicted in April 2021; court declared he holds an equity worth 70% of the annex's value plus trespass damages

individuals.name: Maxine Jackson Ghent
individuals.notes: Ordered to pay the claimant 70% of the annex's value (to be agreed or valued as at April 2021)

financial_figures.what_it_is: Nominal damages for trespass payable by the defendants to the claimant

property_lease.address_or_description: Annex of the disputed family property (built/financed by the claimant)
property_lease.lease_terms: Claimant asserted a proprietary equity from his expenditure on the annex; evicted April 2021
property_lease.tenant: Llewellyn Jackson (occupier who built the annex)
property_lease.outcome: Claimant declared to hold an equity and share of 70% of the annex's value; second defendant to pay that sum once agreed/valued; $20,000 nominal trespass damages and costs awarded to claimant

social_headline: Man who built family home annex wins 70% equity after eviction
social_post: A family property dispute has ended with Justice Devindra Rampersad declaring that Llewellyn Jackson, who built and paid for an annex on the family property before being evicted in April 2021, holds an equity worth 70% of that annex's value. The court directed the parties to agree a value (or have the annex valued as at April 2021), ordered the second defendant to pay him that 70% share, and awarded $20,000 nominal damages for trespass plus costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Money spent building on family land can create an equitable interest enforceable in court
Courts can quantify that interest as a percentage of the structure's value rather than ordering possession
Eviction of someone holding such an equity can attract nominal trespass damages
outcome: Claimant declared to have an equity coupled with a 70% share in the value of the annex; second defendant to pay 70% of the annex's value (agreed or valued as at April 2021); $20,000 nominal trespass damages and prescribed costs awarded to the claimant; liberty to apply.
osint_notes: Private family property/proprietary-equity dispute. No companies, banks or criminal element.

title: Khan, Kendelle; James, Lyndon v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
court: High Court of Justice, San Fernando
judges: The Hon. Madame Justice Karen Reid
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Kendelle Khan
individuals.notes: Brought a constitutional redress claim (ss.4 and 5) against the State; succeeded in part on an application to admit fresh evidence

individuals.name: Lyndon James
individuals.notes: Co-claimant in companion constitutional claim CV2018-02932 against the State

related_litigation: Companion constitutional claim CV2018-02932 (Lyndon James)
CV2020-00510 Melina Charles v Attorney General (source of the fresh affidavit/cross-examination evidence)
social_headline: Court lets claimants add fresh evidence in constitutional case against the State
social_post: In a procedural ruling in long-running constitutional claims against the State, Justice Karen Reid has partly granted an application by Kendelle Khan and Lyndon James to lead fresh evidence. The court allowed them to rely on affidavits and certified cross-examination transcripts of two witnesses drawn from a separate matter, Melina Charles v Attorney General, and set a timetable for supplemental affidavits and submissions ahead of the substantive hearing. Each side was ordered to bear its own costs of the application. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Fresh evidence may be admitted late where it is cogent and material to the case as a whole
Evidence given in a related matter can sometimes be imported into another with leave of the court
Procedural applications of this kind often end with each party bearing its own costs
outcome: Interlocutory ruling: application to lead fresh evidence succeeds in part. Claimants permitted to rely on specified affidavits and certified cross-examination transcripts from CV2020-00510 (Melina Charles v AG), with a timetable for further affidavits and submissions; each party to bear its own costs of the application.
osint_notes: Procedural/evidential ruling in consolidated constitutional claims against the State. No commercial entities, banks or named criminal conduct in this ruling.

title: Mc Comie, Christiana v Gomez, Arnella; Rouse, Reginald; Joseph, Cayce; Guardian General Insurance Limited
judges: The Hon. Mr. Justice Devindra Rampersad

companies.name: Guardian General Insurance Limited
companies.ownership_notes: Insurer joined as co-defendant; the applicant's earlier action had been dismissed and costs never satisfied

individuals.name: Christiana Mc Comie
individuals.notes: Sought to revive litigation after her earlier action was dismissed; application to extend time/proceed refused for delay, lack of cogent evidence and prejudice to the respondents (files closed and destroyed)

related_litigation: Earlier (first) action by the applicant arising from the same matter, dismissed with costs unsatisfied
social_headline: Court refuses to revive long-stalled claim; insurer and defendants get finality
social_post: Justice Devindra Rampersad has refused Christiana Mc Comie's bid to revive litigation after her earlier action was dismissed, naming Guardian General Insurance Limited among the co-defendants. Weighing the long delay, the absence of any cogent professional evidence explaining the conduct of the case, and the severe prejudice to the respondents — whose files had been closed and destroyed and whose witnesses could not reliably recall events nearly a decade old — the court held there was no adequate foundation for relief. The judge acknowledged the human cost but dismissed the application, by consent with no order as to costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Applications to extend time or revive a dismissed claim require cogent evidence and promptness
Severe prejudice to the other side — destroyed files, faded memories — can be decisive against reopening
A litigant failed by their attorney may have other avenues of redress but still cannot reopen a stale claim without foundation
outcome: Application (filed 6 May 2024) to revive/extend time dismissed. Court found undue delay, no cogent evidence on the conduct of the earlier litigation, and severe irreparable prejudice to the respondents; by consent, no order as to costs.
osint_notes: Procedural ruling refusing to revive a stale claim; names insurer Guardian General Insurance Limited as co-defendant. Relevant for insurer/litigation tracking; no criminal element.

title: Diane Parris-Hernandez v Wayne Parris

individuals.name: Diane Parris-Hernandez
individuals.role: claimant; fee simple owner under will of Muriel Parris
individuals.notes: Sister of defendant; awarded possession of land at 52 Joe Street, Dinsley Village, Tacarigua

individuals.name: Wayne Parris
individuals.notes: Claimed proprietary estoppel; counterclaim dismissed; ordered to vacate within 90 days

financial_figures.what_it_is: Mesne profits assessed ($500/month from 25 July 2024 to judgment)
Monthly mesne profits from 1 June 2026 until vacant possession
Prescribed costs of the claim (VAT exclusive)
Prescribed costs of the counterclaim (VAT exclusive)

property_lease.address_or_description: 52 Joe Street, Dinsley Village, Tacarigua
property_lease.lease_terms: Licence to occupy, revoked by notice to quit dated 25 June 2024
property_lease.outcome: Possession awarded to claimant; structure declared a chattel house; defendant to vacate and remove it within 90 days

social_headline: Sister wins family land battle over Tacarigua chattel house
social_post: A long-running family feud over land at 52 Joe Street, Dinsley Village, Tacarigua has ended in the High Court. Diane Parris-Hernandez, who inherited the property under their late mother Muriel Parris's will, sued her brother Wayne Parris for possession. He argued proprietary estoppel, claiming their mother promised he could stay for life. Justice Westmin James rejected that, declared his wooden structure a chattel house with no legal or equitable interest in the land, and ordered him to vacate and remove it within 90 days. He must also pay mesne profits of $11,016.13 plus costs of $28,000. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Proprietary estoppel requires clear assurance, reliance and detriment; mere long occupation is not enough.
A chattel house may be treated as personalty and ordered removed rather than vesting any land interest in the occupier.
outcome: Judgment for claimant: possession granted, counterclaim of proprietary estoppel dismissed, defendant to vacate within 90 days; mesne profits and $28,000 costs awarded.
osint_notes: Named property at 52 Joe Street, Dinsley Village, Tacarigua; family ownership dispute with identifiable parties and monetary awards.

title: Eugene Reynald v Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Eugene Reynald
individuals.notes: Alleged Ministry of Works and Transport drainage works on the Industry River caused erosion to lands adjoining his property at 36c Hillrise Road, Blue Range; claim dismissed

financial_figures.what_it_is: Prescribed costs awarded to the defendant

property_lease.address_or_description: 36c Hillrise Road, Blue Range (proposed 31-townhouse development)
property_lease.outcome: Claim for trespass/nuisance/negligence over river erosion dismissed; access route found to belong to the Bain family, not claimant

social_headline: Blue Range townhouse dream sinks as erosion claim against the State fails
social_post: Eugene Reynald sued the State over drainage works the Ministry of Works and Transport carried out along the Industry River around 2009, saying a partly built river wall redirected water and eroded land beside his property at 36c Hillrise Road, Blue Range, sterilising a planned 31-townhouse development. Justice Westmin James dismissed the claim. The trespass claim was statute-barred, and although nuisance could be a continuing wrong, Reynald could not prove he had any legal interest in the access route, which his own evidence showed belonged to the Bain family. He was ordered to pay $14,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Trespass claims are subject to strict limitation periods; nuisance may be continuing but still requires a proprietary interest.
A claimant must prove a legal or possessory interest in the affected land to sue in nuisance.
outcome: Claim dismissed; trespass statute-barred and nuisance unproven; claimant to pay $14,000 costs with 28-day stay.
osint_notes: Identifies proposed 31-townhouse development at Blue Range and land owned by the Bain family; infrastructure dispute with the Ministry of Works and Transport.

title: The State v Cuthbert Lee and Others
court: High Court of Justice, Criminal Division (Port of Spain)

criminal_individuals.name: Cuthbert Lee
offences: Trafficking in cocaine / possession of cocaine for the purpose of trafficking, contrary to s5(4) Dangerous Drugs Act 1991
criminal_individuals.notes: Indictment quashed on motion to quash after key prosecution witness statements ruled inadmissible under s21 AJIPA; alleged offence 2 May 2017 at Arouca

social_headline: Cocaine trafficking indictment quashed over flawed witness statements
social_post: A cocaine trafficking case against Cuthbert Lee and two others has collapsed in the High Court. The men were charged with possession of cocaine for the purpose of trafficking, allegedly committed on 2 May 2017 at Arouca. Justice Nalini Singh ruled that prosecution witness statements relied on at the committal stage did not meet the strict requirements of section 21 of the Administration of Justice (Indictable Proceedings) Act. With those statements excluded, there was no admissible evidence to prove possession, knowledge or a link to trafficking, so the Master could not properly have committed the accused. The indictment was quashed, though the ruling does not amount to a finding of innocence. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Witness statements at a sufficiency hearing must strictly comply with s21 AJIPA or risk being ruled inadmissible.
Quashing an indictment for evidential insufficiency is not a finding of factual innocence and may not bar re-charging.
outcome: Motion to quash granted; indictment quashed against all accused due to inadmissible witness statements.
osint_notes: Criminal narcotics matter; primarily procedural. Crime tags retained for tracking.

title: Donny Birjasingh (for Chanardai Birjasingh) v Varoon Ragoonanan (for Takeram Ragoonanan)
claimants: Donny Birjasingh (for and on behalf of Chanardai Birjasingh)
defendants: Varoon Ragoonanan (for and on behalf of Takeram Ragoonanan)

individuals.name: Chanardai Birjasingh
individuals.notes: Awarded possession and injunction over ~1 acre 1 rood 12 perches at Chaguanas

individuals.name: Takeram Ragoonanan
individuals.notes: Raised adverse possession; defence rejected

financial_figures.what_it_is: Prescribed costs awarded to claimant (claim deemed valued at $50,000)

property_lease.address_or_description: Approx. 1 acre 1 rood 12 perches, Ward of Chaguanas (part of Deed No. 5574 of 1954)
property_lease.outcome: Possession and injunction granted to documentary title holder; adverse possession defence failed; defendant to deliver possession within 60 days

social_headline: Paper title beats squatter's claim in Chaguanas land dispute
social_post: In a contest between documentary title and adverse possession, the paper owner has prevailed. Justice Robin N. Mohammed awarded Chanardai Birjasingh possession of about an acre of land in the Ward of Chaguanas, part of a parcel described in a 1954 deed. The defendant, represented through Takeram Ragoonanan, claimed adverse possession based on time and works done on the land, but the court held that an owner who has not slept on his rights cannot have title extinguished. An injunction was granted restraining the defendant from entering the land, who must deliver possession within 60 days and pay $14,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Adverse possession will not defeat a vigilant documentary owner who has asserted his rights.
Where a claim is non-monetary, prescribed costs are calculated on a deemed value (here $50,000) under CPR Part 67.
outcome: Judgment for claimant: possession and injunction granted, defendant to deliver up land within 60 days and pay $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Named Chaguanas land parcel and 1954 deed; adverse possession dispute between identifiable families.

title: Neil Looknanan v Public Service Commission

individuals.name: Neil Looknanan
individuals.notes: Succeeded in judicial review challenge to disciplinary charge referred to a tribunal

social_headline: Public officer wins judicial review over unlawful disciplinary charge
social_post: The Public Service Commission acted unlawfully when it preferred and referred a disciplinary charge against public officer Neil Looknanan, the High Court has found. Justice Karen Reid ruled that decisions communicated in letters dated 29 August 2023 and 3 February 2024 breached the rules of natural justice and Looknanan's legitimate expectation that regulations 90(6) and 92 of the Public Service Commission Regulations would be followed. The court quashed both decisions by certiorari and issued mandamus compelling the Commission to properly consider his representations before deciding whether he should face any disciplinary offence. The Commission was ordered to pay his costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Disciplinary bodies must follow their own regulations and afford officers a chance to make representations before charging.
Courts can extend relief to an earlier related decision where quashing only the later one would leave the breach unremedied.
outcome: Judicial review granted; decisions of 29 Aug 2023 and 3 Feb 2024 quashed; mandamus to reconsider representations; costs to claimant.
osint_notes: Public-sector disciplinary/administrative law matter; limited commercial content.

title: High Tide Project v The Judiciary of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Judiciary of Trinidad and Tobago

companies.name: High Tide Project
companies.role: claimant; non-profit company incorporated under the Companies Act Chap. 81:01
companies.ownership_notes: Non-profit company pursuing a Freedom of Information Act request against the Judiciary

individuals.name: Anderson Gordon
individuals.notes: Filed response affidavit

social_headline: FOIA fishing expedition: court refuses NGO's disclosure bid against Judiciary
social_post: Non-profit company High Tide Project is suing the Judiciary of Trinidad and Tobago after being refused access to official documents under the Freedom of Information Act. In this ruling, Justice Robin N. Mohammed dismissed the NGO's application for specific disclosure of records about how Magistrates' Courts log criminal matters and identify police complainants. The court found several requests amounted to a fishing expedition or attempt to obtain the fruits of litigation prematurely, stressing that disclosure in judicial review is exceptional, limited and court-driven. Costs were reserved and the matter set for a further case management conference in July 2026. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Disclosure in judicial review is exceptional and must be confined to what is necessary to deal fairly with the issues.
Broad, undefined document requests risk being struck out as fishing expeditions.
outcome: Specific disclosure application dismissed; costs reserved; further CMC fixed for 20 July 2026.
osint_notes: Identifies High Tide Project as a non-profit company litigating FOIA access against the Judiciary.

title: George Berrios v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: George Berrios
individuals.notes: Challenged constitutionality of Co-operative Societies Act provisions; claim found to be an abuse of process

individuals.name: Andrea McKenna
individuals.role: acting Commissioner of Co-operative Development (challenged appointment)
individuals.notes: Appointment challenge held to be an abuse of process

related_litigation: CEMCU dispute hearing of 11 April 2018; Commissioner's Order dated 18 June 2019
social_headline: Constitutional challenge to Co-operative Societies Act dismissed as abuse of process
social_post: George Berrios asked the High Court to declare sections 67(1), 74 and 81 of the Co-operative Societies Act unconstitutional, arguing they wrongly send credit union and co-operative disputes to the Commissioner for Co-operative Development. Justice Lambert Peterson found that Berrios was really trying to relitigate a 2018 CEMCU hearing and a 2019 order he had failed to appeal in time, and that judicial review was an available parallel remedy. Invoking the constitutional jurisdiction in those circumstances was an abuse of the court's process, and the appointment of acting Commissioner Andrea McKenna was likewise not properly challenged. The claim failed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Where judicial review is an adequate parallel remedy, resorting to constitutional relief is generally an abuse of process.
Constitutional motions cannot be used to bypass missed statutory appeal or judicial review time limits.
outcome: Claim dismissed as an abuse of process; constitutional relief refused where parallel judicial review remedy existed.
osint_notes: Constitutional/administrative matter touching co-operative and credit union (CEMCU) governance.

title: In re the Real Property Act (Application of Martin Steele for a vesting order)

individuals.name: Martin Steele
individuals.notes: Sought vesting order by adverse possession over 4 acres 3 roods 32 perches at Poole Valley Crown Trace, Charuma; application dismissed

individuals.name: Cletus Perry
individuals.notes: Great uncle of applicant

property_lease.address_or_description: 4 acres 3 roods 32 perches, LP 43 Poole Valley Crown Trace, Charuma (Crown Grant Vol 815 Folio 351)
property_lease.outcome: Vesting order refused; insufficient proof of 16 years continuous, exclusive adverse possession

social_headline: Rio Claro electrician loses bid to claim Charuma land by adverse possession
social_post: Martin Steele, an electrician from Rio Claro, asked the High Court for a vesting order under section 49 of the Real Property Act over nearly five acres at Poole Valley Crown Trace, Charuma, originally granted to his great uncle Cletus Perry in 1936. Steele claimed his family had occupied and cultivated the land continuously since 2006. But Justice Westmin James found the evidence fell short of the required sixteen years of continuous, exclusive possession, noting tax receipts ran only to 2009 and remained in the dead owner's name, with no utility, agricultural or improvement records. The application was dismissed with no order as to costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A vesting order by adverse possession requires clear, cogent proof of continuous and exclusive possession for the full statutory period.
Tax receipts in the paper owner's name and conclusory affidavits are not enough to extinguish title.
outcome: Application for vesting order dismissed; adverse possession not proven; no order as to costs.
osint_notes: Named land parcel at Poole Valley Crown Trace, Charuma with Crown Grant reference; family adverse-possession claim.

title: Stefan Edwards v Derron Perreira and TRINRE Insurance Company Ltd

companies.name: TRINRE Insurance Company Ltd
companies.ownership_notes: Insurer that consented to liability of its insured

individuals.name: Stefan Edwards
individuals.notes: Sought conditional leave to appeal to the Privy Council; application dismissed

individuals.name: Derron Perreira
individuals.notes: Default judgment entered against him for non-appearance

social_headline: Court of Appeal blocks Privy Council bid in TRINRE insurance assessment fight
social_post: The Court of Appeal has refused Stefan Edwards leave to take his case to the Privy Council. The dispute arose from a personal-injury matter in which default judgment was entered against insured driver Derron Perreira, while his insurer, TRINRE Insurance Company Ltd, consented to liability and was then allowed by a Master to participate in the damages assessment, including cross-examining witnesses. Edwards argued the insurer should not have been permitted to take part. Justices Moosai, Wilson and Aboud held the point raised no question of great public importance and merely concerned the Master's case management discretion, with which the court found no fault. The application was dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: An insurer that consents to liability may still be permitted to participate in the assessment of damages under the court's case management powers.
Leave to appeal to the Privy Council requires a question of great general or public importance, not mere disagreement with a discretionary case-management ruling.
outcome: Application for leave to appeal to the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council dismissed.
osint_notes: Insurance/procedural appeal involving TRINRE Insurance Company Ltd.

title: Kylise Romain v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Kylise Romain
individuals.notes: Detained as a 14-year-old child for ~42 days in the Adult Women's Prison, Golden Grove, in 2014 after being deemed 'beyond control'; awarded constitutional damages

financial_figures.what_it_is: Constitutional damages inclusive of aggravated damages
Vindicatory damages

related_litigation: Commissioner of Prisons v Seepersad [2021] UKPC 13
Attorney General v JM [2022] UKPC 54
social_headline: State to pay $400,000 for jailing 14-year-old girl in adult women's prison
social_post: The High Court has ordered the State to pay Kylise Romain $400,000 after she was held as a 14-year-old in the Adult Women's Prison, Golden Grove, for about 42 days in 2014 under a 'beyond control' order. Justice Nadia Kangaloo declared the detention unlawful and a breach of her constitutional rights under sections 4(a), 4(b) and 5(2)(b), awarding $250,000 in damages including an aggravated uplift and a further $150,000 in vindicatory damages, plus interest. The judge expressed strong displeasure that the State filed no submissions and stressed that detaining children in adult prisons is wholly antithetical to constitutional values of dignity and protection. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Detaining minors in adult custodial facilities breaches their constitutional rights and attracts substantial vindicatory damages.
The State bears a heightened duty of care toward children it detains, regardless of 'beyond control' designations.
outcome: Detention declared unlawful and unconstitutional; $250,000 constitutional/aggravated damages and $150,000 vindicatory damages awarded with interest; costs reserved.
osint_notes: Constitutional matter concerning unlawful detention of a minor; child-sensitive, kept factual.

title: Shazim Baksh v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Shazim Baksh
individuals.notes: Sued for false imprisonment and malicious prosecution after a marijuana charge; claim dismissed

individuals.name: PC Maharaj
individuals.notes: Court found reasonable and probable cause; no malice

criminal_individuals.name: Shazim Baksh
criminal_individuals.notes: Charged following detention 19-22 January 2018; charge dismissed for want of prosecution on 23 January 2023

social_headline: No payout: court rejects false imprisonment claim over dismissed marijuana charge
social_post: Shazim Baksh sued the State for false imprisonment and malicious prosecution after he was detained between 19 and 22 January 2018 and charged with possession of marijuana, a charge later dismissed for want of prosecution. Justice Betsy Ann Lambert Peterson dismissed his claim, finding that PC Maharaj had reasonable and probable cause to arrest and charge him and did not act with malice. Baksh's case was weakened by a lack of objective evidence about the circumstances of his arrest and by the absence of independent witnesses to support his account, including his claimed employment. The claim was dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A malicious prosecution claim fails where the officer had reasonable and probable cause and acted without malice.
Dismissal of the underlying charge does not by itself establish false imprisonment or malicious prosecution.
outcome: Claim for false imprisonment and malicious prosecution dismissed.
osint_notes: Civil claim arising from a dismissed narcotics charge; crime tag retained for tracking.

title: Marlon Ollivierra v The Chief of Defence Staff, The Commanding Officer of the T&T Regiment and the Attorney General
defendants: The Commanding Officer of the Trinidad and Tobago Regiment
The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Marlon Ollivierra
individuals.role: claimant; soldier seeking promotion to Warrant Officer Class I
individuals.notes: Succeeded in constitutional/judicial review claim over denied promotion and seniority

financial_figures.what_it_is: Compensatory damages for breach of constitutional rights
Vindicatory damages

related_litigation: AG v Carmel Smith [2009] UKPC 50
social_headline: Soldier wins $130,000 after Regiment denied him promotion to Warrant Officer
social_post: A member of the Trinidad and Tobago Regiment has won his constitutional challenge over a denied promotion. Marlon Ollivierra claimed the Chief of Defence Staff failed to promote him to Warrant Officer Class I and to place him correctly on the seniority list. Justice Frank Seepersad declared that the decisions violated his rights to protection of the law and to equality of treatment from a public authority under sections 4(b) and 4(d) of the Constitution. The court awarded $80,000 in compensatory damages and $50,000 in vindicatory damages, payable by the Attorney General as the representative of the State, plus costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Public authorities must follow fair procedures and natural justice when making promotion and seniority decisions.
In constitutional claims, the Attorney General, as representative of the State, is liable to satisfy damages awards rather than the agency agents.
outcome: Constitutional breach declared; $80,000 compensatory and $50,000 vindicatory damages awarded against the Attorney General with costs and 28-day stay.
osint_notes: Constitutional/employment matter involving the T&T Regiment; limited commercial content.

title: Zalika Abdool v Afzal Mohammed
court: High Court of Justice (San Fernando Sub-Registry)

individuals.name: Zalika Abdool
individuals.notes: Claimed equitable interest in land at 211B Garth Road, Williamsville; awarded compensation but no equitable interest

individuals.name: Afzal Mohammed
individuals.notes: Ordered to pay claimant a lump sum and interest, after which claimant vacates

financial_figures.what_it_is: Lump sum payable by defendant to claimant
Pre-judgment interest on the $400,000
Prescribed costs payable to claimant

property_lease.address_or_description: 211B Garth Road, Williamsville, Garth Estate, Ward of Savana Grande (~464.3 m2, Deed No. 9006 of 1974)
property_lease.outcome: Claimant declared to have no equitable interest but awarded $400,000 plus interest; she must then vacate and deliver possession

social_headline: Sister gets $400,000 but no land stake in Williamsville family property fight
social_post: A dispute between siblings over land at 211B Garth Road, Williamsville has ended with a financial settlement rather than a property stake. Zalika Abdool sued her brother Afzal Mohammed claiming she had acquired an equitable interest in the roughly 464-square-metre parcel. Justice Robin N. Mohammed declared she had not acquired any equitable interest, but ordered Mohammed to pay her a $400,000 lump sum plus $66,931.50 in interest within six months, after which Abdool must vacate and deliver up possession. Mohammed, who was unrepresented, must also pay $68,193.15 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A claimant may recover compensation for contributions to property without establishing a full equitable interest.
Courts can structure orders so payment is conditional on delivery of vacant possession.
outcome: Equitable interest claim refused, but defendant ordered to pay $400,000 plus $66,931.50 interest and $68,193.15 costs; claimant to vacate on payment.
osint_notes: Named property at 211B Garth Road, Williamsville with deed reference; family land dispute with monetary awards.

title: Ravi Balgobin Maharaj v The Minister of Finance

individuals.name: Ravi Balgobin Maharaj
individuals.notes: Won FOIA judicial review against the Minister of Finance for breach of s16 FOIA

social_headline: Minister of Finance broke FOI law by sitting on approved records, court rules
social_post: Activist Ravi Balgobin Maharaj has won his Freedom of Information case against the Minister of Finance. Justice Devindra Rampersad ruled that after the Ministry approved Maharaj's January 2022 information request, it then failed for years to actually hand over the records, breaching section 16 of the Freedom of Information Act. The court found no insurmountable logistical barrier or lawful deferment justified the delay, holding that the statute does not permit an approved request to remain outstanding indefinitely. A mandamus was granted compelling the Ministry to provide the information free of charge within 28 days, and the Minister must pay costs certified fit for Senior Counsel. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Once a FOIA request is approved, section 16 requires the information to be provided without unreasonable delay.
Public bodies cannot indefinitely defer disclosure absent a lawful, formally invoked deferment.
outcome: Judicial review granted; breach of s16 FOIA declared; mandamus to disclose within 28 days; costs to claimant fit for Senior Counsel.
osint_notes: High-profile FOIA transparency case against the Ministry of Finance; public-interest litigant.

title: Ronald Roberts v The Judicial and Legal Service Commission

individuals.name: Ronald Clive Roberts
individuals.role: intended claimant; litigant in person and next friend of his son Isaiah Jabari Emmanuel Roberts
individuals.notes: Granted leave to seek judicial review of JLSC inaction on a s137 misconduct complaint against two High Court judges

social_headline: Court grants leave to challenge JLSC silence on complaint against two judges
social_post: A self-represented litigant, Ronald Clive Roberts, has been granted leave to bring a judicial review against the Judicial and Legal Service Commission. Roberts says the JLSC has unlawfully failed to decide a formal complaint of judicial misconduct he made against two High Court judges, despite a 'Renewed Complaint' submitted in August 2025 and years of correspondence. He argues the Commission is ignoring its constitutional duty under section 137 of the Constitution, which governs the removal of judges. Justice Westmin James granted leave to proceed, ordered the claim form filed within 14 days, and fixed a case management conference for July 2026, reserving costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The JLSC has a constitutional duty under section 137 to consider properly made complaints about judicial conduct.
Persistent failure by a public body to decide a complaint can ground a judicial review for unreasonable delay.
outcome: Leave to apply for judicial review granted; directions given and costs of the leave application reserved.
osint_notes: Constitutional/judicial-accountability matter; leave-stage decision against the JLSC.

title: Samlalsingh (Executors) v The Urban Development Corporation of Trinidad and Tobago (UDeCOTT)
court: High Court of Justice (San Fernando Sub-Registry)
claimants: Michael Anthony Samlalsingh (as Executors of the Will of Bertwin Clifford Samlalsingh, deceased)
defendants: The Urban Development Corporation of Trinidad and Tobago (UDeCOTT)

companies.name: Urban Development Corporation of Trinidad and Tobago (UDeCOTT)
companies.ownership_notes: State-owned urban development corporation; alleged purchaser of the King Street property

individuals.name: Bertwin Clifford Samlalsingh (deceased)
individuals.notes: Sought specific performance of an agreement to sell King Street property to UDeCOTT for $6,000,000

financial_figures.what_it_is: Agreed/claimed purchase price for the King Street property

property_lease.address_or_description: Nos. 26, 28 and 30 King Street, San Fernando (~4,598 sq ft with building, Deed No. 26390 of 1997)
property_lease.lease_terms: Alleged agreement for sale to UDeCOTT
property_lease.outcome: Specific performance refused; no binding agreement satisfying s4 Conveyancing and Law of Property Act

social_headline: $6M King Street sale to UDeCOTT collapses as court finds no binding deal
social_post: The estate of Bertwin Clifford Samlalsingh has lost its bid to force the Urban Development Corporation of Trinidad and Tobago (UDeCOTT) to complete a $6 million purchase of property at Nos. 26, 28 and 30 King Street, San Fernando. The executors sought specific performance of an alleged agreement for sale, but Justice Christopher Sieuchand found the agreement documents had never been executed by UDeCOTT and that the parties were engaged in an acquisition by private treaty from which either side was free to walk away. With no binding contract satisfying section 4 of the Conveyancing and Law of Property Act, the claim was dismissed and the estate ordered to pay costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Specific performance requires a concluded, signed agreement satisfying s4 of the Conveyancing and Law of Property Act.
Parties negotiating a property sale by private treaty remain free to withdraw until the agreement is executed.
outcome: Claim for specific performance dismissed; no binding agreement found; claimants to pay costs (quantum reserved).
osint_notes: Commercial property dispute involving UDeCOTT and a $6M San Fernando property at 26-30 King Street.

title: Lackan Samaroo v Ramdeo Samaroo

individuals.name: Lackan Samaroo
individuals.notes: Claim over family property struck out as res judicata

individuals.name: Ramdeo Samaroo
individuals.notes: Obtained summary judgment and strike-out

financial_figures.what_it_is: Assessed prescribed costs of the claim (VAT exclusive; prescribed basis $165,484)
Costs of the application (VAT exclusive)

property_lease.address_or_description: No. 6 Satar Road, Aranguez
property_lease.lease_terms: Subject of a 2019 Consent Order in prior proceedings
property_lease.outcome: Trespass/unpaid monies claim struck out as res judicata; defendant repossessed property on 2 July 2022

related_litigation: CV2019 Consent Order dated 23 January 2019
social_headline: Family land claim over Aranguez property tossed out as already decided
social_post: A fresh lawsuit between relatives over property at No. 6 Satar Road, Aranguez has been thrown out of the High Court. Lackan Samaroo sued Ramdeo Samaroo for trespass, unpaid monies under a 2019 consent order, and damage to chattels after Ramdeo repossessed the property in July 2022. Justice Westmin James granted summary judgment for the defendant, finding the issues had already been determined in earlier proceedings and were barred by res judicata and issue estoppel, while the chattels claim lacked proper particulars. The statement of case was struck out and the claim dismissed, with Lackan ordered to pay costs of $23,675.82 plus $7,500. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Re-litigating issues already decided between the same parties is barred by res judicata and issue estoppel.
Special damages, such as damage to chattels, must be specifically pleaded and particularised.
outcome: Summary judgment for defendant; statement of case struck out and claim dismissed; claimant to pay $31,175.82 in costs.
osint_notes: Named family property at 6 Satar Road, Aranguez; repeat litigation between the Samaroos.

title: Dianne Charles and Rosalyn Charles v Philothea Charles, Ariel Charles and Precious Daniel

individuals.name: Dianne Charles
individuals.notes: Resisted defendants' strike-out application; application dismissed

property_lease.address_or_description: No. 36 Frankie Lane, La Canoa Road, Santa Cruz
property_lease.outcome: Dispute over construction works and access; defendants' strike-out application dismissed, matter proceeds

related_litigation: CV2017-02810 (injunction granted by Gobin J on 31 July 2017)
social_headline: Santa Cruz construction dispute heads to trial after strike-out bid fails
social_post: A long-running dispute between members of the Charles family over construction works and access at No. 36 Frankie Lane, La Canoa Road, Santa Cruz will proceed to trial. The defendants asked the High Court to strike out the claim as frivolous and an abuse of process, but Justice Robin N. Mohammed dismissed that application. The judge noted the defendants had failed to file witness statements, triggering automatic sanctions under the CPR, and had not applied for relief from sanctions but instead launched a late strike-out attempt at the doorstep of trial, which he viewed as a misuse of the court's process. The defendants must pay the claimants' costs, with a case management conference set for July 2026. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A strike-out application should be made early, not on the eve of trial.
A party in breach of an unless/witness-statement sanction should apply for relief from sanctions rather than attempt to strike out the opponent's claim.
outcome: Defendants' strike-out application dismissed; defendants to pay claimants' costs; CMC fixed for 6 July 2026.
osint_notes: Named Santa Cruz property at 36 Frankie Lane; family construction/access dispute.

title: Andre Cephas v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Andre Cephas
individuals.role: appellant; former Lance Corporal, T&T Defence Force
individuals.notes: Discharged on medical grounds in 2017 after a 2003 injury; constitutional claim over resettlement training dismissed

financial_figures.what_it_is: Retirement medical disability lump-sum payment received by appellant
Annual disability payment received by appellant

social_headline: Injured ex-soldier loses appeal over missed resettlement training
social_post: Former Defence Force Lance Corporal Andre Cephas has lost his appeal against the State. Cephas, who enlisted in 1998 and was seriously injured during a 2003 exercise, spent more than 2,700 days on injury leave before being discharged on medical grounds in January 2017. He argued the TTDF breached his rights by failing to provide resettlement training. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal, finding he had seven years to apply for resettlement training and simply did not, a choice the court could not relieve him from. The judges noted he was not discharged without compensation, having received a $155,134.09 disability lump sum and a $53,503.29 annual disability payment. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts will not micromanage the administrative and human-resource discretions of bodies like the Defence Force absent a legal breach.
Delay in bringing a constitutional claim, combined with receipt of compensation, may support a finding of abuse of process.
outcome: Appeal dismissed; parties to be heard on costs.
osint_notes: Constitutional/employment matter involving the T&T Defence Force; disability payments quantified.

title: Talawatie Christopher v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Talawatie Christopher
individuals.notes: Detained April 2019 during a murder-threat investigation; claim that detention was unlawful dismissed

criminal_individuals.name: Talawatie Christopher
offences: Alleged threats to life / murder-for-hire (under investigation)
criminal_individuals.notes: Detained 20 April 2019 on intelligence she paid money for a killing; released 22 April 2019 pending phone analysis; not charged in these proceedings

related_litigation: Rowley v Christo Gift
social_headline: Court of Appeal: detention over alleged murder-for-hire threat was lawful
social_post: The Court of Appeal has ruled that police lawfully detained Talawatie Christopher during a 2019 investigation into a serious threat to life. Officers acted on intelligence from the SORT unit that Christopher had paid money for a killing and that a threat communicated by phone had the potential to spiral out of control. Corporal Ramlogan detained her on 20 April 2019, gathered witness statements, and she was released two days later pending analysis of seized phones by the Cyber Crime Unit. The court found the police acted reasonably and proportionately given the information they held, and dismissed her appeal challenging the detention as unlawful. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The lawfulness of detention is judged on the information reasonably available to police at the time and the proportionality of their response.
Brief detention during a fast-moving threat-to-life investigation can be justified even without charge.
outcome: Appeal dismissed; detention held lawful; parties to be heard on costs.
osint_notes: Civil detention claim arising from a murder-threat investigation; crime tag retained for tracking.

title: Dave Mitchell and David Mitchell v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Dave Mitchell
individuals.notes: Sued for false imprisonment and malicious prosecution over 29 August 2011 arrest; claim dismissed

individuals.name: Doniphon Thompson
individuals.role: police officer (then Corporal, Reg. No. 15488, now retired Sergeant)
individuals.notes: Led the arresting party

financial_figures.what_it_is: Prescribed costs awarded to defendant

social_headline: Twin brothers lose false imprisonment claim over 2011 arrest
social_post: Twin brothers Dave and David Mitchell have lost their claim against the State for false imprisonment and malicious prosecution arising from their arrest on 29 August 2011 by a police party led by then Corporal Doniphon Thompson. Justice Robin N. Mohammed dismissed the claim after a full trial. Because the claim was for unquantified damages with no agreed value, it was deemed to be worth $50,000 for costs purposes, and the brothers were ordered to pay the State $14,000 in prescribed costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: False imprisonment and malicious prosecution claims require proof of absence of reasonable and probable cause and, for the latter, malice.
Unquantified claims are deemed valued at $50,000 for prescribed-costs purposes under CPR Part 67.
outcome: Claim for false imprisonment and malicious prosecution dismissed; claimants to pay $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Civil claim against the State arising from a 2011 arrest; limited commercial content.

title: South Arrows Archery Club v The Trinidad and Tobago Target Archery Federation
defendants: The Trinidad and Tobago Target Archery Federation (also called Trinidad and Tobago Archery Federation)

companies.name: Trinidad and Tobago Target Archery Federation
companies.ownership_notes: National archery governing body whose Executive Committee expelled the claimant club

companies.name: South Arrows Archery Club
companies.ownership_notes: Member club expelled and seeking enforcement of an arbitral award

social_headline: Court enforces arbitration award, reinstates expelled archery club
social_post: South Arrows Archery Club has won enforcement of an arbitral award against the Trinidad and Tobago Target Archery Federation. The club asked the High Court to recognise and give effect to the award and to quash the Federation Executive Committee's 29 May 2024 decision to permanently expel the club and its executive members during the arbitration. Justice Betsy Ann Lambert Peterson treated the first hearing as a trial and held that the award binds the Federation and had not been set aside, so there was no reason to delay enforcement. The club, as the successful party, was awarded costs on the scale for a claim deemed valued at $50,000. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A binding arbitral award that has not been set aside is enforceable and the court need not order a full trial to give effect to it.
Expelling a party during an ongoing arbitration may be quashed as procedurally improper.
outcome: Arbitral award recognised and enforced; expulsion decision quashed; club reinstated; costs to claimant.
osint_notes: Sports-governance/arbitration dispute between an archery club and the national federation.

title: Emile Elias and NH International (Caribbean) Limited v Radio Vision Limited and Barrington Thomas

companies.name: NH International (Caribbean) Limited (NHIC)
companies.ownership_notes: Incorporated in the Cayman Islands, registered as external company under Companies Act Chap. 81:01; principal place of business 39 Long Circular Road, St. James; seven-time TTCA Contractor of the Year

companies.name: Radio Vision Limited
companies.ownership_notes: Broadcaster found liable for defamatory publications

individuals.name: Emile Elias
individuals.role: first claimant; engineer, businessman, Executive Chairman of NHIC
individuals.notes: Founder of the TTCA and co-founder of the JCC; awarded defamation damages

individuals.name: Barrington Thomas
individuals.notes: Restrained by injunction from repeating defamatory words

financial_figures.what_it_is: General damages (incl. aggravated) to Emile Elias
General damages (incl. aggravated) to NH International (Caribbean) Ltd
Exemplary damages to Emile Elias
Exemplary damages to NH International (Caribbean) Ltd
Prescribed costs of the claim

social_headline: Businessman Emile Elias and NH International win $700,000 defamation payout
social_post: Prominent contractor Emile Elias and his company NH International (Caribbean) Limited have won a major defamation case against Radio Vision Limited and Barrington Thomas. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC found the defendants liable for broadcasts imputing theft, corruption and dishonest conduct to the claimants in connection with the Scarborough Regional Hospital Project and a land development project. Elias and NHIC were each awarded $250,000 in general damages (including an aggravated uplift) and $100,000 in exemplary damages, with the defendants jointly and severally liable, plus $109,712.33 in costs and interest. The court also granted an injunction restraining Barrington Thomas from repeating the defamatory words. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Broadcasting unproven imputations of crime or corruption against a business or its principal can attract substantial general and exemplary damages.
Courts may grant injunctions restraining the repetition of defamatory statements alongside damages.
outcome: Judgment for claimants in defamation; $700,000 total damages (general plus exemplary) jointly and severally, $109,712.33 costs and interest; injunction against further publication.
osint_notes: Names Emile Elias and NH International (Caribbean) Ltd (Cayman-incorporated contractor, 39 Long Circular Road, St James), Radio Vision Limited and Barrington Thomas; references Scarborough Hospital Project and NIPDEC; significant damages.

title: Perelion and Cooper v Vanessa Cooper (Executrix of the Estate of Cladin Cooper)
defendants: Vanessa Cooper (Executrix of the Will of Cladin Cooper)

individuals.name: Cladin Cooper (deceased)
individuals.notes: Died 4 October 2023; her 23 February 2021 will was challenged and upheld as valid

individuals.name: Vanessa Cooper
individuals.notes: Will upheld; authority as executrix confirmed

individuals.name: Bertlene Perelion
individuals.notes: Granted the portion of Platanite Trace land on which her house stands, by proprietary estoppel (defendant's concession)

financial_figures.what_it_is: Prescribed costs of the claim (VAT exclusive)
Prescribed costs of the counterclaim (VAT exclusive)

property_lease.address_or_description: Land at Platanite Trace, Rochard Road, Penal
property_lease.outcome: Will upheld; First Claimant Bertlene Perelion declared entitled to the fenced portion on which her dwelling stands by proprietary estoppel

social_headline: Children's challenge to mother's will fails, but daughter keeps her house plot
social_post: The biological children of Cladin Cooper, who died in October 2023, challenged her February 2021 will that named her niece Vanessa Cooper as sole executrix, alleging the deceased lacked mental capacity and was unduly influenced. Justice Westmin James dismissed the challenge and declared the will valid, confirming Vanessa Cooper's authority to administer the estate. However, by the executrix's concession, the court declared that one daughter, Bertlene Perelion, is entitled by proprietary estoppel to the fenced portion of land at Platanite Trace, Penal, on which she built her home. The claimants must pay $28,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Challenging a will for lack of capacity or undue influence requires cogent evidence; the presumption favours a duly executed will.
Proprietary estoppel can protect a relative who built on family land in reliance on assurances, even where a will is upheld.
outcome: Will declared valid and claim dismissed; defendant succeeds on counterclaim; First Claimant granted her house plot by proprietary estoppel; claimants pay $28,000 costs.
osint_notes: Named family land at Platanite Trace, Penal; probate/estoppel dispute over the Cooper estate.

title: Patterson Nelson v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Patterson Nelson
individuals.notes: Claims 75+ years' family occupation of 9A Fran Street, Cocoyea Village; alleges flooding from San Fernando By-Pass runoff

property_lease.address_or_description: No. 9A Fran Street, Cocoyea Village, San Fernando (downhill of the San Fernando By-Pass)
property_lease.outcome: Nuisance/negligence claim over road runoff flooding allowed to proceed; State's strike-out application dismissed

social_headline: Flooding claim over San Fernando By-Pass runoff survives strike-out
social_post: A homeowner's claim that runoff from the San Fernando By-Pass is damaging his property will go to trial. Patterson Nelson, who says he and his father have occupied land at 9A Fran Street, Cocoyea Village for over 75 years, alleges that since around 2015 surface and sub-surface water from the By-Pass has flowed onto his land, tilting his fence, leaking through his retaining wall and cracking his house. The State applied to strike out the claim as an abuse of process, but Justice Robin N. Mohammed refused, holding the matter should be resolved at trial. The State must file its defence within 42 days and pay the costs of the application. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Strike-out is reserved for plainly unsustainable claims; arguable nuisance and negligence claims should proceed to trial.
Failure to file a defence within an extended deadline can expose a defendant to judgment in default.
outcome: State's strike-out application dismissed; defence to be filed within 42 days; State to pay costs of the application.
osint_notes: Named property at 9A Fran Street, Cocoyea Village; infrastructure-runoff dispute with the Ministry of Works.

title: Jack's Tyre Traders Limited v The Ministry of Public Administration, The Ministry of National Security and the Attorney General
court: High Court of Justice (San Fernando Sub-Registry)
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

companies.name: Jack's Tyre Traders Limited
companies.ownership_notes: Owner of the Gaston Court compound in Chaguanas; leased office space to the Immigration Office and public washrooms used by the Ministry of National Security

individuals.name: Ramsamooj
individuals.role: agent who invoiced rent for claimant
individuals.notes: Invoiced at $6.10/sq ft Cabinet-approved 2007 rate

financial_figures.what_it_is: Award (VAT inclusive) for breach of contract / unjust enrichment for washroom rental
Monthly rate used to value washroom usage

property_lease.address_or_description: Public washrooms at Nos. 2-3 Gaston Court, Chaguanas (part of the Gaston Court compound housing the Immigration Office)
property_lease.lease_terms: Rental of stand-alone public washrooms by the Ministry of National Security
property_lease.outcome: Defendants ordered to pay $503,437.50 plus interest and costs

social_headline: State owes $503,000 for years of unpaid public washroom rent in Chaguanas
social_post: Jack's Tyre Traders Limited has won a $503,437.50 judgment against the State over unpaid rent for public washrooms at Gaston Court, Chaguanas. The company owns the compound where the Ministry of National Security ran the Immigration Office and used stand-alone washroom facilities. Justice Ricky Rahim found the State liable for breach of contract and unjust enrichment for the washroom usage, with the Ministry of Public Administration responsible for the leasing arrangements. Using a Cabinet-approved 2007 rate of $6.10 per square foot and a conservative $5,000 monthly figure, the court awarded $503,437.50 (VAT inclusive) plus interest from April 2017 and prescribed costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A party that uses premises without paying may be liable in unjust enrichment on a quantum meruit basis using objective market rates.
Documented, market-aligned rental rates are persuasive evidence of reasonable value.
outcome: Judgment for claimant: $503,437.50 (VAT inclusive) plus interest from 18 April 2017 and prescribed costs.
osint_notes: Names Jack's Tyre Traders Limited and the Gaston Court compound, Chaguanas; State leasing of Immigration Office space and washrooms; sizeable monetary award.

title: Miguel Reyes v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Miguel Reyes
individuals.notes: Lost 56 animals that escaped through a fence allegedly damaged by Ministry of Works contractor; awarded damages

financial_figures.what_it_is: Special damages
General damages (nominal, for trespass)
Costs

social_headline: State pays for 56 animals lost after Ministry contractor damaged farm fence
social_post: Farmer Miguel Reyes has won damages from the State after losing 56 animals from his property. Reyes claimed that in May 2015 a contractor engaged by the Drainage Division of the Ministry of Works and Transport, while desilting the La Pastora River and removing fallen burnt bamboo, damaged a portion of his fence, allowing the animals to escape. Justice Karen Reid entered judgment for Reyes, awarding $32,000 in special damages for the lost animals and a nominal $10,000 in general damages for trespass, which is actionable per se, plus interest and $14,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Trespass to land is actionable per se and attracts at least nominal damages even without proven loss.
A landowner can recover consequential losses, such as escaped livestock, flowing from negligent damage to boundary fencing.
outcome: Judgment for claimant: $32,000 special and $10,000 general damages, plus interest and $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Negligence/trespass claim over farm fence damage and lost livestock; limited commercial content.

title: Dane Daniel v The Commanding Officer of the T&T Coast Guard, the Defence Force Commissions Board and the Attorney General
defendants: The Commanding Officer of the Trinidad and Tobago Coast Guard
The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Dane Daniel
individuals.notes: Won judicial review over non-promotion to Lieutenant Commander; decision quashed and remitted; disability-equality themes

financial_figures.what_it_is: Vindicatory damages

social_headline: Coast Guard officer wins promotion review with disability-inclusion ruling
social_post: A Trinidad and Tobago Coast Guard officer, Dane Daniel, has won his judicial review over being passed over for promotion to Lieutenant Commander. Justice Westmin James quashed the decision not to recommend him and remitted the questions of promotion and appointment date to the Chief of Defence Staff for reconsideration. In a judgment emphasising that those who experience disability must be included within the nation's promise of equality and the full protection of the law, the court awarded Daniel $50,000 in vindicatory damages, payable by the Defence Force Commissions Board, plus costs, though his claim for a responsibility allowance was refused. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Promotion decisions must assess officers on merit with fairness and the full protection of the law.
Courts can quash and remit administrative decisions while awarding vindicatory damages for constitutional breaches.
outcome: Certiorari quashing non-promotion decision; matter remitted for reconsideration; $50,000 vindicatory damages and costs awarded; responsibility-allowance claim refused.
osint_notes: Constitutional/employment matter involving the Coast Guard; disability-inclusion themes.

title: Sunita Harripersad v The South-West Regional Health Authority
court: High Court of Justice (San Fernando Sub-Registry)

individuals.name: Sunita Harripersad
individuals.notes: Alleged knee injury from a student nurse opening a door at San Fernando General Hospital; claim dismissed

financial_figures.what_it_is: Prescribed costs awarded to defendant (claim valued at $43,900)

social_headline: Knee-injury claim against San Fernando hospital dismissed
social_post: Sunita Harripersad's negligence claim against the South-West Regional Health Authority has been dismissed. She said her left knee was injured on 26 February 2019 when a student nurse suddenly opened a stockroom door in the Asthma Room of the San Fernando General Hospital, where she was waiting on her husband's medical results. The Authority denied liability, arguing she was not authorised to be in the room and that the incident was fabricated. Justice Margaret Mohammed found she failed to prove a causal connection between the staff's actions and her injury. The claim was dismissed and she was ordered to pay $12,475 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A negligence claimant must prove causation linking the defendant's act to the injury on a balance of probabilities.
Unquantified personal-injury claims are valued for costs by reference to the damages contended for.
outcome: Claim dismissed for failure to prove liability/causation; claimant to pay $12,475 costs.
osint_notes: Personal-injury negligence claim against a regional health authority; limited commercial content.

title: Patrian Phillip-Roberts v The Commanding Officer of the T&T Regiment and the Attorney General
defendants: The Commanding Officer of the Trinidad and Tobago Regiment
The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Patrian Phillip-Roberts
individuals.notes: Won challenge to refusal to adjust her seniority after a maternity-related delay in qualifying for promotion

financial_figures.what_it_is: Compensatory damages
Non-pecuniary damages
Vindicatory damages

social_headline: Soldier wins maternity-discrimination ruling against the Regiment
social_post: A female soldier has won a landmark constitutional ruling against the Trinidad and Tobago Regiment over maternity. Patrian Phillip-Roberts challenged the Regiment's refusal to adjust her seniority after a delay in qualifying for promotion caused by pregnancy and childbirth. Justice Westmin James found the decision breached her rights to protection of the law, equality of treatment and respect for private and family life under sections 4(b), 4(c) and 4(d) of the Constitution, holding that the State cannot impose career disadvantage for circumstances arising from maternity. The court quashed the decision, ordered her seniority on the Sergeants' roster adjusted to her original cohort, and awarded $121,657.40 in total damages plus costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Failing to accommodate maternity-related delays in promotion can amount to unlawful discrimination breaching constitutional equality rights.
Courts can order seniority rosters corrected and award compensatory, non-pecuniary and vindicatory damages together.
outcome: Constitutional breaches declared; non-promotion/seniority decision quashed and seniority adjusted; $41,657.40 compensatory, $50,000 non-pecuniary and $30,000 vindicatory damages plus interest and costs awarded.
osint_notes: Constitutional/employment matter on maternity discrimination in the T&T Regiment.

title: RBC Royal Bank (Trinidad and Tobago) Limited v Siu Chong Investments Limited
claimants: RBC Royal Bank (Trinidad and Tobago) Limited (formerly RBTT Bank Limited)

companies.name: Siu Chong Investments Limited
companies.ownership_notes: Applied unsuccessfully for summary judgment

banks_financial_institutions: RBC Royal Bank (Trinidad and Tobago) Limited (formerly RBTT Bank Limited)
social_headline: RBC's claim against Siu Chong Investments to proceed after summary judgment refused
social_post: A dispute between RBC Royal Bank (Trinidad and Tobago) Limited, formerly RBTT Bank Limited, and Siu Chong Investments Limited is heading to a full trial. The investment company applied for summary judgment to dismiss the bank's claim, but Justice Frank Seepersad refused. He held that the case turns on the construction of a written contract and the parties' wider commercial relationship, raising questions of fact that cannot be safely resolved on the pleadings and require tested witness evidence at trial. Siu Chong Investments was ordered to pay the bank the costs of the failed application. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Summary judgment is inappropriate where the dispute turns on contract construction and the factual matrix needs to be tested at trial.
An ongoing commercial relationship can be relevant to interpreting the parties' contractual intentions.
outcome: Defendant's summary judgment application dismissed; matter to proceed to trial; defendant to pay costs of the application.
osint_notes: Banking/commercial dispute naming RBC Royal Bank (ex-RBTT) and Siu Chong Investments Limited with named officers.
