social_headline: Orbit Motors loses bid to force $8M Gulf View land sale, owes $264,000 costs
social_post: Car dealer Orbit Motors Limited and its director Garrett Ali have lost a fight to buy the Gulf View, La Romaine property they rented. The claimants sought specific performance of an alleged oral agreement to purchase two parcels at Bay Road for $8 million, after their 2018 lease expired in August 2021. Justice Karen Reid refused specific performance and found in favour of landlords Caesarmudeen and Shanaz Samad, who are entitled to $37,800 in rent arrears and mesne profits of $12,600 per month until the property is handed back. The claimants must pay $264,000 in costs, calculated on the $8 million sale price. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: An oral agreement for the sale of land is difficult to enforce by specific performance without clear evidence and part performance.
A holding-over tenant can be liable for arrears of rent and mesne profits until vacant possession is delivered.
outcome: Specific performance refused; defendants awarded rent arrears, ongoing mesne profits and possession; claimants to pay $264,000 costs.
osint_notes: Names Orbit Motors Limited and directors, an $8M Gulf View/La Romaine property, and the Samad landlords; substantial figures.

title: Joyce Renwick and Samuel Slom v Avon Products Inc. et al.

companies.name: Avon Products Inc.
companies.ownership_notes: Defendant in Florida asbestos litigation; applied in T&T to obtain evidence for those proceedings

individuals.name: Joyce Renwick
individuals.notes: Claims asbestos exposure from Johnson's Baby Powder caused her mesothelioma

related_litigation: Joyce Renwick and Samuel Slom v Avon Products, Inc., et al., Case No. 2023-11345-CA-01, Circuit Court of the Eleventh Judicial Circuit, Miami-Dade County, Florida
social_headline: T&T court orders evidence-gathering in US asbestos baby powder case
social_post: The Trinidad and Tobago High Court has been drawn into a US asbestos lawsuit. Avon Products Inc., a defendant in Florida proceedings brought by Joyce Renwick and her husband Samuel Slom, applied urgently to obtain evidence locally for that case. Renwick claims that exposure to asbestos from Johnson's Baby Powder caused her mesothelioma, while Avon contends its research shows sugar refineries are common sources of asbestos exposure and is seeking evidence relevant to that theory. Justice Avason Quinlan-Williams ordered the application served on the named organisations with a virtual return date of 8 May 2026, and directed that no steps be taken to destroy or secrete the documents in question. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: T&T courts can assist foreign litigation by ordering local evidence-gathering under letters of request.
Courts may issue preservation orders prohibiting destruction of documents pending an evidence application.
outcome: Application for evidence in aid of foreign proceedings progressed; service ordered with an 8 May 2026 return date and a document-preservation direction.
osint_notes: Cross-border asbestos/mesothelioma litigation naming Avon Products Inc. and a Miami-Dade case; local evidence-gathering against unnamed T&T organisations.

title: Victor Joseph v Public Service Commission and the Attorney General
defendants: Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Victor Joseph
individuals.notes: Challenged cancellation of his promotion from Prisons Officer I to II; claim dismissed as there was a sound basis (he was subject to a disciplinary/investigation matter)

social_headline: Prisons officer loses challenge to cancelled promotion
social_post: Prisons Officer Victor Joseph has lost his judicial review challenge to the Public Service Commission's decision to cancel his promotion from Prisons Officer I to Prisons Officer II. The promotion was reserved and then cancelled because he was the subject of a disciplinary/investigation matter that had not been resolved when the promotion was due. Justice Betsy Ann Lambert Peterson found there was a sound basis for the decision and declined to quash it, dismissing the claim. While the constitutional aspects of his case were held to be without merit and unreasonable, the court ordered him to bear only half of the defendants' costs, noting claimants should not be discouraged from pursuing matters of potential public importance. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A promotion may lawfully be deferred or cancelled where the officer is subject to unresolved disciplinary proceedings.
Courts may reduce adverse costs in public-law cases to avoid deterring genuinely arguable claims.
outcome: Claim dismissed; cancellation of promotion upheld; claimant to bear one half of the defendants' costs.
osint_notes: Public-sector promotion/judicial review matter; limited commercial content.

title: The State v Kervin Harrynarine (also called 'Duffy')
court: High Court of Justice, Criminal Division (Port of Spain)

criminal_individuals.name: Kervin Harrynarine (also called 'Duffy')
criminal_individuals.notes: Tried for stabbing death of Devon Brown; acquitted on reasonable doubt where the sole eyewitness gave inconsistent accounts and CCTV could not independently identify the stabber

social_headline: Man found not guilty of stabbing murder as eyewitness account falters
social_post: Kervin Harrynarine, known as 'Duffy', has been found not guilty of murdering Devon Brown. There was no dispute that Brown died from multiple stab wounds, but Justice Nalini Singh, sitting without a jury, held the central question was whether the State proved Harrynarine inflicted them. The prosecution relied principally on a single eyewitness, Andy Seebaran, who said he saw the accused lean over a bannister and stab the deceased, but the judge found his accounts of where he was positioned inconsistent and the CCTV footage too poor to independently identify the stabber. Left with a reasonable doubt, the court returned a verdict of not guilty. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A conviction cannot rest on a single eyewitness whose account is internally inconsistent and unsupported by independent evidence.
Poor-quality CCTV that cannot independently identify the perpetrator will not cure doubt about identification.
outcome: Verdict of Not Guilty; prosecution failed to prove beyond reasonable doubt that the accused inflicted the fatal stab wounds.
osint_notes: Murder trial ending in acquittal; crime tag retained for tracking.

title: The State v Devon Campbell
court: High Court of Justice, Criminal Division (San Fernando)

criminal_individuals.name: Devon Campbell
offences: Robbery with violence, contrary to s24(1)(b) Larceny Act
Common assault, contrary to s30 Offences Against the Person Act
criminal_individuals.notes: Domestic incident on 24 July 2022 at La Romaine against partner Sophia Goodridge-Joseph; repeated violence, used a firearm, took jewellery and cash; acquitted of demanding money with menaces; sentenced to 6 years hard labour (robbery) and 9 months (assault) concurrent, with credit for time served

social_headline: Six years for La Romaine man who robbed and beat his partner at gunpoint
social_post: Devon Campbell has been sentenced to six years' imprisonment with hard labour for robbery with violence, plus nine months for common assault to run concurrently. A jury convicted him over a 24 July 2022 attack on his partner, Sophia Goodridge-Joseph, at his La Romaine apartment, where, after confronting her about messages on her phone, he slapped her repeatedly until her lip burst, dragged her by her hair, threatened to cut her hair with scissors, used a firearm, and took her jewellery and cash. He was acquitted of a separate count of demanding money with menaces. Justice Nalini Singh said the total six-year term reflected the repeated violence, the firearm and the fear caused, with credit for time already served. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Use of a firearm and repeated violence are significant aggravating factors in sentencing for robbery.
Courts avoid double-counting the same violence across overlapping counts by ordering concurrent sentences.
outcome: Convicted of robbery with violence and common assault; sentenced to 6 years' hard labour (concurrent with 9 months), with full credit for time served; acquitted of demanding money with menaces.
osint_notes: Violent robbery/domestic violence sentencing involving a firearm; crime tags retained for tracking.

title: David Castillo Hernandez and others v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Thirteen claimants (Latin American nationals)
individuals.notes: Subjected to an unlawful search, seizure of property and detention amounting to inhuman and degrading treatment; each awarded substantial constitutional damages

financial_figures.what_it_is: Damages for unlawful detention, per claimant
Damages for unlawful seizure of property, per claimant
Vindicatory damages, per claimant

social_headline: State to pay 13 men $450,000 each over unlawful search and detention
social_post: Thirteen men have won a major constitutional case against the State over an unlawful search and detention. Justice Westmin James declared that the manner of the search breached their right to the protection of the law, that the seizure and detention of their personal property was unlawful, and that they were subjected to inhuman and degrading treatment contrary to section 5(2)(b) of the Constitution. Each claimant was awarded $275,000 for unlawful detention, $25,000 for the unlawful seizure of property and $150,000 in vindicatory damages, a total of $450,000 each, with statutory interest and costs assessed for Senior and Junior Counsel. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Unlawful searches, property seizures and degrading detention conditions can attract substantial per-claimant constitutional and vindicatory damages.
The right to protection of the law constrains how state agents conduct searches and detentions.
outcome: Constitutional breaches declared; each of 13 claimants awarded $450,000 (detention, seizure and vindicatory damages) plus interest and costs.
osint_notes: Multi-claimant constitutional matter on unlawful search/seizure/detention; large aggregate damages (~$5.85M across 13 claimants).

title: Akil Quaccoo v The Commanding Officer of the T&T Regiment and the Attorney General

individuals.name: Akil Quaccoo

financial_figures.what_it_is: Compensation for lost opportunity to pursue resettlement training

social_headline: Ex-soldier wins $44,108 for lost resettlement training but loses promotion bid
social_post: Former Defence Force Corporal Akil Quaccoo has won partial relief in his judicial review against the Regiment. He challenged his removal and reinstatement to a promotion course, his discharge on completion of service in January 2023, the curtailment of his resettlement training, and the failure to promote him to Sergeant. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC dismissed the claims for certiorari, mandamus and loss of earnings, but found he was wrongly deprived of resettlement training and awarded him $44,108 in compensation. Given the disadvantage he suffered, the defendants were ordered to pay 75% of his costs, with a costs assessment fixed for June 2026. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Curtailing an entitlement such as resettlement training can sound in compensation even where promotion challenges fail.
Partial success can still attract a substantial costs award reflecting the disadvantage suffered.
outcome: Claims for certiorari, mandamus and loss of earnings dismissed; $44,108 awarded for lost resettlement training; defendants to pay 75% of claimant's costs.
osint_notes: Constitutional/employment matter involving the T&T Regiment; limited commercial content.

title: Anthony Richardson v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.notes: Sued for assault/battery, unlawful arrest and false imprisonment over a 3 August 2013 incident at Casa Blanca Recreation Club, Point Fortin; claim dismissed

criminal_individuals.name: Anthony Richardson
offences: Possession of cocaine for the purpose of trafficking
criminal_individuals.notes: Arrested 3 August 2013 after officers found foil wrappers of suspected cocaine; arrest and detention found lawful

financial_figures.what_it_is: Prescribed costs awarded to defendant

social_headline: Court rejects brutality and false-imprisonment claim over Point Fortin drug arrest
social_post: Anthony Richardson's claim against the State for assault, unlawful arrest and false imprisonment has been dismissed. He alleged that after being arrested at the Casa Blanca Recreation Club in Point Fortin on 3 August 2013, he was beaten by officers, threatened at gunpoint and made to say his last prayer before being charged with cocaine trafficking and resisting arrest. The State said officers acting on confidential information found foil wrappers of suspected cocaine on him and used only reasonable force when he resisted. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC found the arrest and detention lawful, with no undue delay in charging, and held no false imprisonment claim arose for the period he was remanded by the Magistrate. The claim was dismissed with $14,000 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Detention pursuant to a Magistrate's remand order cannot ground a false imprisonment claim against the State.
Station diary records can be decisive evidence that an arrest and detention were lawful and timely.
outcome: Claim for assault, unlawful arrest and false imprisonment dismissed; arrest held lawful; claimant to pay $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Civil claim arising from a narcotics arrest at Point Fortin; crime tag retained for tracking.

social_headline: BAICO wins eviction of Crichlow family from two Arima Aventura Gardens units
social_post: British American Insurance Company (Trinidad) Limited (BAICO) has won summary possession of two apartments at 1800 Aventura Gardens, Moreno Street, Arima. The insurer brought proceedings under CPR Part 68 against members of the Crichlow family and other occupiers of Units 23 and 25. Justice Margaret Mohammed declared the occupiers had no claim, right or entitlement to the units and ordered them to deliver up vacant possession within 42 days, restraining them from re-entering thereafter. The court will hear BAICO separately on damages, and the defendants were ordered to pay 55% of the prescribed costs, amounting to $7,700. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Summary possession proceedings under CPR Part 68 allow an owner to recover land from occupiers without an arguable right.
Occupiers who cannot show a legal or equitable entitlement can be ordered out and restrained from re-entry.
outcome: Possession granted to claimant; occupiers declared to have no entitlement and ordered to vacate within 42 days; defendants to pay $7,700 costs; damages to be assessed.
osint_notes: Names BAICO (British American Insurance Company (Trinidad) Limited) and its Aventura Gardens property in Arima; eviction of named occupiers.

social_headline: Master sets 1.25% interest on debt default judgment
social_post: In a short assessment ruling, Master Wrenerson Lochan has fixed the pre-judgment interest owed on a debt-recovery default judgment. After Shazad Mohammed obtained judgment in default of appearance against Selina Isaac in a breach of contract and debt claim, the only outstanding question was the rate of interest. Considering prevailing economic conditions and precedent, the Master assessed pre-judgment interest at 1.25% per annum from the date the claim was served in November 2025 to the date of judgment in March 2026, quantified at just $260.45, with the statutory 5% rate to apply afterward. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts have discretion under the Supreme Court of Judicature Act to set pre-judgment interest by reference to economic conditions and precedent.
The statutory 5% rate applies to judgment debts from the date of judgment onward.
outcome: Pre-judgment interest assessed at 1.25% ($260.45); statutory 5% to apply post-judgment.
osint_notes: Routine debt-recovery interest assessment; minimal commercial detail.

social_headline: Firstline Securities must repay Gulf Insurance over unauthorised executive payment
social_post: The Court of Appeal has upheld a ruling that Firstline Securities Limited must repay Gulf Insurance Limited funds it received as a 'knowing receipt'. The money stemmed from a payment authorised by Gulf's former Chairman and Executive Director, Gerrard Lee-Inniss, without board approval, to repay sums earlier advanced to Ingla Limited and to Lee-Inniss personally. Although the court rejected liability for dishonest assistance, it agreed Firstline was a constructive trustee because it knew enough about the impropriety of the payment. Firstline was ordered to pay Gulf TTD$495,167 and USD$768,000, and its appeal was dismissed, with the orders of the trial judge affirmed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A recipient of company funds paid out improperly can be liable as a constructive trustee for 'knowing receipt'.
Executives cannot authorise payments to repay their own or related-company advances without proper board approval.
outcome: Appeal dismissed; Firstline Securities liable for knowing receipt and ordered to pay Gulf Insurance TTD$495,167 and USD$768,000; trial orders affirmed.
osint_notes: Names Firstline Securities Limited, Gulf Insurance Limited, Ingla Limited and former Gulf Chairman/Executive Director Gerrard Lee-Inniss; unauthorised executive payment and substantial sums.

social_headline: Appeal court overturns El Socorro possession order, splits estate in half
social_post: The Court of Appeal has partly overturned a ruling over a disputed property at the corner of Shillingford Lane and Boundary Road, El Socorro. Ronisha Joseph and Roneeta Deonarine had won possession at trial, relying on a 2019 Deed of Assent, with appellant Krishendaye Kissoon ordered to vacate and pay nominal damages. On appeal, the court set aside the orders declaring the respondents owners of a half-share and entitled to vacant possession, and instead declared that Kissoon is entitled to half of the estate of the late Ronnie Deonarine, while affirming the refusal of his claim to a half share in a separate parcel. The appeal was allowed in part. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A Deed of Assent does not automatically defeat a competing beneficial entitlement in a deceased's estate.
Appellate courts can restructure ownership declarations where the trial court misallocated estate interests.
outcome: Appeal allowed in part; possession orders set aside; appellant declared entitled to half the estate of Ronnie Deonarine.
osint_notes: Named El Socorro property and estate of Ronnie Deonarine; inheritance/possession dispute.

social_headline: Neighbour refused stay over $6M-plus he must repay to Claude Lawson's estate
social_post: The Court of Appeal has refused a stay to Andre Lee, a neighbour of the late Claude Lawson, who was ordered by the High Court to repay millions allegedly transferred out of the estate's bank accounts. The trial judge found Lee had unlawfully moved four sums out of the estate's accounts, namely $120,000, $5,956,422.75, USD185,395.26 and $127,575.94, and also ordered him to pay $165,000 in rent for a Hilux pickup and to return the vehicle or pay its market value. Lee sought to freeze the Republic Bank and Scotiabank funds and stay enforcement pending appeal, arguing a strong jurisdiction point, but Justice Ricky Rahim found nothing exceptional to justify a stay and dismissed the application. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A stay pending appeal requires exceptional circumstances, not merely an arguable ground.
Executors can recover funds shown to have been unlawfully transferred out of estate accounts.
outcome: Stay application dismissed; trial orders to repay estate funds (over $6M plus USD185,395.26) and pickup-related sums remain enforceable pending appeal.
osint_notes: Large alleged misappropriation from the Claude Lawson estate involving Republic Bank and Scotiabank accounts; multi-million-dollar sums and named parties.

social_headline: Tenants lose appeal over flood damage blamed on landlord J.T. Allum
social_post: The Court of Appeal has dismissed claims by two commercial tenants over flood damage in a building owned by J.T. Allum and Company Limited. On 9 December 2018, water from a PVC pipe, tap or angle valve in an upstairs unit occupied by O.T.I. Trinidad Limited flooded five suites rented by Western Industrial Solutions Limited and the premises of Debera Rampersad's clothing business, Debera Fashion Step Up and Save. The tenants argued the landlord was negligent for failing to keep the premises in good repair, but the court held a bare allegation of disrepair in the landlord's ancillary claim against O.T.I., never proven at trial, could not establish liability. The appeal was dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A landlord is not automatically liable for water damage caused by another tenant's fittings absent proof of negligence.
An allegation in a pleading is not proven merely because a defendant fails to defend an ancillary claim.
outcome: Appeal dismissed with costs; tenants' negligence/nuisance claims against the landlord fail.
osint_notes: Names commercial tenants Western Industrial Solutions Ltd, Debera Fashion, landlord J.T. Allum and Company Limited and upstairs tenant O.T.I. Trinidad Limited.

individuals.role: appellant; Superintendent and lead investigating officer
individuals.notes: Obtained and executed the search warrants

social_headline: Court of Appeal: police search of Trinidad Express breached press freedom
social_post: In a landmark press-freedom ruling, the Court of Appeal has held that police unlawfully searched the Trinidad Express. The 11 March 2020 search followed an investigative article about suspicious banking activity involving a senior police officer, with deposits flagged in suspicious activity reports to the Financial Intelligence Unit. Treating the story as evidence of unlawful 'tipping off' under the Proceeds of Crime Act, Superintendent Wendell Lucas obtained warrants and seized four USB drives from editor-in-chief Omatie Lyder's office. By a majority, the court allowed the State's appeal in part but upheld that the warrant regime was unlawfully applied and breached the respondents' constitutional right to freedom of the press, finding the warrants excessively wide. Justice Aboud dissented. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Search warrants against media houses must be specific, proportionate and reasonable, with regard to confidential sources and editorial material.
Freedom of the press constrains the State's power to use search warrants to identify journalistic sources.
outcome: Appeal allowed in part by majority, but the search warrant regime held to have been unlawfully applied, breaching the constitutional right to freedom of the press; Aboud JA dissenting.
osint_notes: Press-freedom landmark naming One Caribbean Media, Trinidad Express, editor Omatie Lyder, Supt Wendell Lucas; references FIU, suspicious banking of a senior police officer and 'tipping off' under the Proceeds of Crime Act.

title: Islyn Thomas v Christine Sergeant-Leith and Ranford Leith

individuals.name: Islyn Thomas
individuals.notes: Won possession and damages over driveway/bridge built on her Zion Hill, Belle Garden land

individuals.name: Christine Sergeant-Leith
individuals.notes: Claimed right of way; counterclaim dismissed

financial_figures.what_it_is: Special damages
General damages for trespass (incl. aggravated)
Prescribed costs of the claim
Prescribed costs of the counterclaim

property_lease.address_or_description: Lands at Zion Hill, Belle Garden, Tobago (the 'Thomas Lands'); disputed driveway and concrete bridge
property_lease.lease_terms: Defendants claimed prescriptive right of way / right of way by necessity / irrevocable licence
property_lease.outcome: Defendants' right-of-way claims rejected; ordered to remove structures, restrained by permanent injunction, and to pay damages

social_headline: Tobago landowner wins $139,000 over neighbours' driveway and bridge trespass
social_post: A Tobago landowner has won her trespass case over a driveway and concrete bridge built on her property. Islyn Thomas sued neighbours Christine Sergeant-Leith and Ranford Leith, who built the structures across her Zion Hill, Belle Garden land and claimed a right to use them as the only vehicular access to their home, asserting a prescriptive right of way, a right of way by necessity, or an irrevocable licence. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC rejected those claims, granted a permanent injunction restraining the defendants, and awarded $64,358.20 in special damages and $75,000 in general damages for trespass including an aggravated uplift, plus costs. Their counterclaim was dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Claims to a prescriptive right of way, necessity or irrevocable licence require strong evidence and will fail if unproven.
Building structures on a neighbour's land without an established right exposes the builder to removal orders and trespass damages.
outcome: Judgment for claimant: permanent injunction, removal of structures, $64,358.20 special and $75,000 general damages, plus costs; counterclaim dismissed.
osint_notes: Named Tobago property at Zion Hill, Belle Garden; neighbour right-of-way/trespass dispute with damages.

title: Construtora OAS S.A. v National Infrastructure Development Company Limited (NIDCO)

companies.name: Construtora OAS S.A.
companies.ownership_notes: Contractor alleged to have abandoned the project; arbitral award in its favour partly set aside

companies.name: National Infrastructure Development Company Limited (NIDCO)
companies.ownership_notes: State-owned developer; entitled to terminate the contract for abandonment

related_litigation: London Court of International Arbitration (LCIA) award under the construction contract
social_headline: Court of Appeal backs NIDCO against OAS, faults LCIA arbitration award
social_post: The Court of Appeal has ruled in favour of the National Infrastructure Development Company Limited (NIDCO) in its long-running dispute with Brazilian contractor Construtora OAS S.A. The case arose from a construction contract where OAS claimed NIDCO's non-payment justified slowing works, while NIDCO said OAS had abandoned the project. The court held that a 'no appeal' clause in the arbitration agreement was void as contrary to public policy, then found the LCIA tribunal made critical errors of law, including wrongly treating Interim Payment Certificate 55 as void from the start and irrationally rejecting abandonment. It held NIDCO was entitled to terminate for abandonment, dismissed OAS's appeal, and affirmed the remittal of the matter to the tribunal for reconsideration. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Clauses purporting to oust the court's supervisory jurisdiction over arbitral awards are void as contrary to public policy.
Interim payment certificates are provisionally binding unless and until corrected or set aside.
outcome: Appeal dismissed; arbitral award's key findings held to contain errors of law; NIDCO entitled to terminate for abandonment; matter remitted to the tribunal.
osint_notes: Major international construction/arbitration dispute naming Construtora OAS S.A. and NIDCO; LCIA award and a State infrastructure contract.

title: Gerard Antrobus v Janet Fortune-Philip
court: High Court of Justice (San Fernando Sub-Registry)

individuals.name: Gerard Antrobus
individuals.notes: Won trespass/nuisance claim over an encroaching concrete wall and step drain at Angelina Terrace, Morvant

individuals.name: Janet Fortune-Philip
individuals.notes: Caught by CPR sanctions for non-compliance with disclosure and witness-statement directions

financial_figures.what_it_is: Nominal damages ($30,000 trespass + $15,000 nuisance)
Prescribed costs of the claim

property_lease.address_or_description: LP No. 13 Angelina Terrace, Never Dirty, Morvant
property_lease.outcome: Defendant ordered to remove encroaching concrete wall and step drain within 60 days, restrained from further encroachment, and to pay damages

social_headline: Morvant homeowner wins removal of neighbour's encroaching wall and drain
social_post: Gerard Antrobus has won his trespass and nuisance case against neighbour Janet Fortune-Philip over structures encroaching on his land at Angelina Terrace, Never Dirty, Morvant. He complained of a concrete wall built around 1980 and a more recent step drain that crossed onto his property. The defendant filed a defence but failed to comply with the court's disclosure and witness-statement directions, falling foul of CPR sanctions. Justice Robin N. Mohammed ordered her to remove both structures within 60 days at her own expense, restrained her from further encroachment, and awarded Antrobus $45,000 in damages ($30,000 for trespass and $15,000 for nuisance) plus interest and $14,000 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Failing to comply with disclosure and witness-statement directions can result in automatic CPR sanctions that cripple a defence.
Encroaching structures can be ordered removed at the defendant's expense with trespass and nuisance damages.
outcome: Judgment for claimant: removal of encroaching structures within 60 days, permanent injunction, $45,000 damages plus interest and $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Named Morvant property at Angelina Terrace; boundary encroachment/trespass dispute.

title: Ravi Ramdial v Teaching Service Commission and the Disciplinary Tribunal members

individuals.name: Ravi Ramdial
individuals.notes: Succeeded in judicial review against the Disciplinary Tribunal's handling of a misconduct charge

social_headline: Teacher wins judicial review over disciplinary tribunal's handling of charge
social_post: Teacher Ravi Ramdial has won his judicial review against the Teaching Service Commission and the members of a disciplinary tribunal appointed to hear a misconduct charge against him. Justice Robin N. Mohammed ordered the tribunal, comprising Indra Ramoutar-Liverpool, Chander Sinanan and Mark Hansraj, to provide Ramdial with its statement of reasons within 28 days, and awarded him the costs of the judicial review proceedings. The stay on the disciplinary proceedings imposed during the litigation was lifted, allowing the tribunal to proceed properly. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Disciplinary tribunals can be compelled to give proper statements of reasons for their decisions.
Successful judicial review of procedural failings does not necessarily end the underlying disciplinary process, which may resume lawfully.
outcome: Judicial review succeeds; tribunal ordered to provide a statement of reasons within 28 days; costs to claimant; stay on disciplinary proceedings lifted.
osint_notes: Public-sector disciplinary/judicial review matter involving the Teaching Service Commission.

title: Samantha Lovelace v Javanni Sebro (Administrator ad litem of the Estate of James Sebro) and The Presidential Insurance Company Limited
defendants: Javanni Sebro (Administrator ad litem of the Estate of James Sebro, deceased)

companies.name: The Presidential Insurance Company Limited
companies.ownership_notes: Insurer joined in a running-down personal-injury claim

individuals.name: Samantha Lovelace
individuals.notes: Personal-injury claimant seeking an extension to file witness statements ahead of assessment of damages

social_headline: Master extends witness-statement deadline in road-accident injury claim
social_post: In a procedural ruling, Master Wrenerson Lochan has extended the deadline for witness statements in a road-accident personal-injury claim brought by Samantha Lovelace against the estate of James Sebro and the Presidential Insurance Company Limited. Balancing procedural fairness against the need to keep the case moving, the Master extended the time to file and exchange witness statements to 3 May 2026 and the time for evidential objections to 10 May 2026, warning that no witness without a filed statement could be called. The pre-hearing on assessment of damages was kept fixed for 11 May 2026. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts balance procedural fairness against expedition when deciding whether to extend witness-statement deadlines.
A party who fails to file a witness statement by the extended deadline may be barred from calling that witness.
outcome: Witness-statement and evidential-objection deadlines extended; assessment of damages pre-hearing kept on 11 May 2026.
osint_notes: Procedural personal-injury/insurance matter naming Presidential Insurance Company Limited.

title: Allison Henry v North Central Regional Health Authority
court: High Court of Justice (San Fernando Sub-Registry)

individuals.name: Allison Henry
individuals.notes: Injured at work on 3 June 2015, exacerbating a pre-existing injury; liability split 70/30

social_headline: Health authority 70% liable for worker's on-the-job injury
social_post: A health-sector worker has largely succeeded in her workplace negligence claim. Allison Henry sued the North Central Regional Health Authority for injuries suffered on 3 June 2015 that exacerbated a pre-existing condition. Justice Ricky Rahim found the primary responsibility lay with the Authority due to systemic deficiencies in its equipment and work procedures, but reduced her award by 30% for contributory negligence, noting she had not remained seated, failed to communicate with the driver, and deviated from policy on handling an infant despite knowing her own condition. Liability was apportioned 70% to the Authority and 30% to Henry, with damages to be assessed and 70% of costs awarded to her. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Employers can be liable for workplace injuries caused by systemic deficiencies in equipment and procedures.
Damages may be reduced for contributory negligence where the employee disregards known risks or policy.
outcome: Negligence established; liability apportioned 70% defendant / 30% claimant; damages to be assessed; 70% of costs to claimant.
osint_notes: Workplace personal-injury negligence claim against a regional health authority.

title: Miranda Leiba v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Miranda Leiba
individuals.notes: Arrested by the Professional Standards Bureau over a missing TTPS Galil firearm; claim for unlawful arrest/false imprisonment dismissed

criminal_individuals.name: Miranda Leiba
offences: Suspected misappropriation of a TTPS Galil firearm (#6698) and 70 rounds of 5.56 ammunition
criminal_individuals.notes: Detained 115 hours (3-8 January 2022) by the Professional Standards Bureau; civil claim challenging arrest dismissed

financial_figures.what_it_is: Prescribed costs awarded to defendant (claim valued at $365,000)

social_headline: Officer loses suit over 115-hour detention in missing police rifle probe
social_post: A serving police constable's claim against the State over her detention in a missing-firearm investigation has been dismissed. Miranda Leiba was arrested on 3 January 2022 by the Professional Standards Bureau on suspicion of involvement in the misappropriation of a TTPS Galil rifle and 70 rounds of ammunition, and was held for 115 hours before her release on 8 January. She sued for negligence, unlawful arrest, false imprisonment and conversion. Justice Margaret Mohammed found she failed to establish liability and dismissed the action. Because her counsel valued the claim at $365,000, she was ordered to pay the State $58,000 in prescribed costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A lawful arrest on reasonable suspicion defeats claims for unlawful arrest and false imprisonment.
Costs in unquantified claims can be substantial where the claimant's own valuation is high.
outcome: Claim dismissed for failure to prove liability; claimant to pay $58,000 costs.
osint_notes: Civil claim arising from a police firearm-misappropriation investigation; crime tag retained for tracking.

property_lease.lease_terms: Defendants in occupation since 1984
property_lease.outcome: Judgment for registered owners; defendants to vacate by 30 October 2026 and sell their dwelling house to the claimants at a valuer's price

social_headline: Registered owners win Sangre Grande land held by occupiers since 1984
social_post: Farmers Bharat and Harrack Lochan, the registered proprietors of land at No. 34 Mookram Trace, Sangre Grande, have won possession against O'Neil and Tara Sooknanan, who had occupied the disputed 2½-lot parcel since 1984. Justice Margaret Mohammed struck out the defendants' defence and counterclaim and entered judgment for the owners. The Sooknanans must vacate by 30 October 2026 and sell their dwelling house on the land to the Lochans at a price set by an agreed valuer, with the valuation cost deducted from the purchase price. Mesne profits, damages and costs are to be assessed by a Master. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Registered documentary title can defeat long occupation where an adverse possession defence is struck out.
Courts can structure relief so long-standing occupiers sell improvements to the owner at a valued price.
outcome: Defence/counterclaim struck out; judgment for claimants; defendants to vacate by 30 October 2026 and sell their house to claimants at a valued price; damages/mesne profits/costs to be assessed.
osint_notes: Named Sangre Grande land at Mookram Trace with title reference; long-occupation possession dispute.

title: Johdansingh Mahabirsingh v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Johdansingh Mahabirsingh
individuals.notes: Won malicious prosecution claim after being charged with marijuana possession on a manufactured oral statement; charge dismissed in 2017

criminal_individuals.name: Johdansingh Mahabirsingh
criminal_individuals.notes: Charged 31 December 2012 though nothing illegal was found on him; case dismissed on a no-case submission 14 June 2017; police manufactured an oral statement

criminal_individuals.name: Faizudeen Boodoo
criminal_individuals.notes: Marijuana was found on Boodoo's person at the time of the joint arrest

financial_figures.what_it_is: Special damages (legal fees)
General damages (incl. aggravated uplift)
Exemplary damages

social_headline: Man wins $133,000 after police invented statement to charge him with marijuana
social_post: Johdansingh Mahabirsingh has won damages for malicious prosecution after police manufactured an oral statement to charge him. He was arrested on 31 December 2012 while walking with a companion, Faizudeen Boodoo; marijuana was found on Boodoo, but nothing illegal was found on Mahabirsingh, whose bag held only dirty clothes and provisions. He was nonetheless charged with possession of marijuana and prosecuted at the Rio Claro Magistrates' Court until the case was dismissed on a no-case submission in 2017. Justice Joan Charles found the prosecution malicious and based on a fabricated statement, awarding $8,000 special, $100,000 general (with aggravation) and $25,000 exemplary damages, plus interest and costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Charging a person on a manufactured statement without reasonable cause supports malicious prosecution and exemplary damages.
Being in the company of someone found with drugs is not, without more, reasonable cause to charge.
outcome: Malicious prosecution established; $8,000 special, $100,000 general and $25,000 exemplary damages awarded with interest and costs.
osint_notes: Malicious prosecution arising from a narcotics charge; crime tags retained for tracking.

title: Sumatie Maharaj v Darshan Maharaj (Executor of the Estate of Dhanraj Maharaj) and Sue's Auto Rentals Limited
citation: Civil Appeal No. CA S410 of 2017 (CV2013-02861)
claimants: Sumatie Maharaj (also called Sumatie Seetaram / Sumatie Seetatam Maharaj) (appellant)
defendants: Darshan Maharaj (by himself and as Executor of the Estate of Dhanraj Maharaj)

companies.name: Sue's Auto Rentals Limited
companies.role: second respondent; company linked to the estate
companies.ownership_notes: Company whose funds were inferred to have financed properties the appellant put in her own name

individuals.name: Sumatie Maharaj
individuals.notes: Share of the estate/assets assessed at 25%; found not a reliable witness; appeal dismissed

social_headline: Appeal court keeps partner's estate share at 25% in Maharaj inheritance fight
social_post: The Court of Appeal has upheld a 25% share for Sumatie Maharaj in her long-running dispute over the estate of Dhanraj Maharaj, who died in 2011. The case involved the estate, the executor Darshan Maharaj, and Sue's Auto Rentals Limited. The trial judge found the appellant was not a reliable witness, inferred that funds from the company financed three properties she had put in her own name to the exclusion of the deceased, and concluded she had benefitted disproportionately. Holding that the judge's careful 25% assessment was one a reasonable judge could reach, the Court of Appeal dismissed her appeal, with costs to be argued. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Appellate courts will not disturb a trial judge's share assessment unless no reasonable judge could have reached it.
Putting company-financed assets in one's own name can reduce an estate-share entitlement.
outcome: Appeal dismissed; appellant's 25% share entitlement upheld; parties to be heard on costs.
osint_notes: Estate/company dispute naming Sue's Auto Rentals Limited and the Dhanraj Maharaj estate; assets traced to company funds.

title: Natasha Charles v Airports Authority of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: Airports Authority of Trinidad and Tobago

companies.ownership_notes: Suspended the claimant and investigated her without proper notice

individuals.notes: Won judicial review of her suspension and the Authority's investigative process

social_headline: Airports Authority worker wins review of suspension and secret probe
social_post: An Airports Authority of Trinidad and Tobago employee, Natasha Charles, has succeeded in her judicial review challenge to how she was disciplined. She challenged the Authority's decisions to suspend her from all duties pending investigation, to investigate allegations against her without giving proper notice, and to refuse to tell her the specific steps and procedures of its investigative and disciplinary processes. Justice Joan Charles granted relief, including an order of mandamus directing the Authority to cease the investigations and an order that the suspension be expunged from her employment record, with costs to be paid to her. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Employers must give proper notice and disclose their disciplinary procedures before investigating or suspending staff.
Unlawful suspensions can be quashed and expunged from an employee's record on judicial review.
outcome: Judicial review granted; suspension and investigation decisions impugned; mandamus to cease investigations, suspension expunged, and costs to claimant.
osint_notes: Public-sector employment/judicial review matter involving the Airports Authority of T&T.

Prescribed costs in each claim, and of each counterclaim

property_lease.address_or_description: Lot 1D (and Plots 1 and 1D) forming part of a larger parcel owned on paper by Phillip Ramcharitar
property_lease.lease_terms: Adverse possession in excess of 16 years
property_lease.outcome: Title extinguished by adverse possession; judgment for claimants; defendant liable for crop destruction

social_headline: Squatter's 16-year possession beats paper owner who destroyed his crops
social_post: In two consolidated land claims over one large parcel, Justice Joan Charles ruled in favour of the Ramdeo family against paper owner Phillip Ramcharitar. The judge found Ramcharitar was not a witness of truth, having denied the contents of his own defence and witness statement under cross-examination. She held that Ramdeo had extinguished Ramcharitar's title to Lot 1D by adverse possession exceeding sixteen years, and that Ramcharitar had unlawfully destroyed crops on Plots 1 and 1D. Judgment was entered for the claimants in both claims, the counterclaims dismissed, with $25,000 trespass damages and $14,000 costs awards in each matter. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Sixteen-plus years of continuous, exclusive possession can extinguish a paper owner's title.
A landowner who destroys an adverse possessor's crops can be liable in trespass.
outcome: Judgment for claimants in both claims; adverse possession of Lot 1D established; counterclaims dismissed; $25,000 trespass damages and $14,000 costs awards.
osint_notes: Adverse possession dispute over a large parcel; crop-destruction trespass; identifiable parties.

title: Everwell Medical Limited v Zarif Matthew Abdul

companies.name: Everwell Medical Limited
companies.ownership_notes: Company that filed the claim in person without an attorney and without a valid board resolution authorising the director who signed

individuals.name: Zarif Matthew Abdul

related_litigation: CV2025-05230 (first action); Order of Gobin J dated 22 December 2025
social_headline: Company's self-filed claim struck out for skipping a lawyer and board approval
social_post: A claim by Everwell Medical Limited against Zarif Matthew Abdul has been struck out. The defendant argued the company had filed the action in person without an attorney, that the director who signed, Michael Lopez, lacked a valid board resolution authorising him, that earlier proceedings and a December 2025 order of Gobin J had not been disclosed, and that the claim was not properly served. Justice Margaret Mohammed struck out the claim form and statement of case filed on 31 December 2025 and ordered the company to pay the costs of the application. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A company generally cannot conduct litigation in person; it needs an attorney and a valid board resolution authorising the signatory.
Failing to disclose prior related proceedings and orders can lead to a claim being struck out.
outcome: Claim form and statement of case struck out; claimant company to pay the costs of the application.
osint_notes: Names Everwell Medical Limited and directors Michael Lopez and Joel Joseph; procedural strike-out.

title: Public Services Association of Trinidad and Tobago v Water and Sewerage Authority
claimants: Public Services Association of Trinidad and Tobago (appellant)

companies.ownership_notes: Interdicted the worker on half pay following a misconduct investigation

individuals.name: Simon Bahaw
individuals.notes: Interdicted from duty on half pay in May 2018 after a misconduct report

related_litigation: Industrial Court judgment dated 29 January 2021
social_headline: Appeal court sends WASA worker's interdiction case back to Industrial Court
social_post: The Court of Appeal has ordered a fresh hearing in a dispute between the Public Services Association and the Water and Sewerage Authority over the interdiction of worker Simon Bahaw. WASA told Bahaw in May 2018 that it had received an investigative report on misconduct allegations, then charged him and interdicted him from duty on half pay. The PSA argued the collective agreement was breached. Setting aside the Industrial Court's January 2021 judgment, the Court of Appeal held it was not for it to substitute its own view on the merits and remitted the matter to be heard afresh before a differently constituted panel, noting it does not usually award costs in Industrial Court cases. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Appellate courts will remit, rather than re-decide, matters within the Industrial Court's specialist jurisdiction.
Interdiction on half pay must comply with the governing collective agreement.
outcome: Appeal allowed to the extent that the Industrial Court judgment is set aside and remitted for fresh hearing before a differently constituted panel.
osint_notes: Industrial/labour matter involving the PSA and WASA; remitted for rehearing.

title: Clayton Andrews v The Attorney General and The Public Service Appeal Board
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Clayton Andrews
individuals.notes: Won appeal; Public Service Appeal Board's decline of appellate jurisdiction held to breach his s4(b) rights

related_litigation: Audine Mootoo v The AG & The PSC, Civil Appeal No. 38/2009
social_headline: Appeal court: denying officer an appeal breached his constitutional rights
social_post: Clayton Andrews has won his long-running appeal against the State and the Public Service Appeal Board. The central issue was whether the Board, by declining appellate jurisdiction and so denying him an avenue of appeal, breached his right to the protection of the law under section 4(b) of the Constitution. The Court of Appeal agreed, allowing the appeal, setting aside the trial judge's dismissal of his claim, and declaring that his constitutional right had been infringed. The questions of compensatory and additional vindicatory damages were referred to a Master of the High Court for assessment, with costs to be argued. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Wrongly declining appellate jurisdiction can breach the constitutional right to the protection of the law.
Appellate courts may refer the quantum of constitutional damages to a Master for assessment.
outcome: Appeal allowed; dismissal set aside; s4(b) breach declared; compensatory and vindicatory damages referred to a Master for assessment.
osint_notes: Public-sector constitutional/administrative appeal; limited commercial content.

title: Ronald Singh v Marlon Superville and Colonial Fire and General Insurance Company Limited
court: High Court of Justice (San Fernando Sub-Registry)
defendants: Colonial Fire and General Insurance Company Limited

companies.name: Colonial Fire and General Insurance Company Limited
companies.ownership_notes: Motor insurer; claim against it struck out as abuse of process once the claim against the insured failed

individuals.name: Ronald Singh
individuals.notes: Claim struck out for non-service on the first defendant and abuse of process against the insurer

individuals.name: Marlon Superville
individuals.notes: Claim automatically struck out for non-service under CPR 8.13

banks_financial_institutions: Colonial Fire and General Insurance Company Limited
social_headline: Motor claim collapses after driver never served, insurer freed too
social_post: Ronald Singh's claim arising from a motor matter has been struck out entirely. Because he never served his claim on the first defendant, Marlon Superville, within the time allowed, Justice Margaret Mohammed held the claim against Superville was automatically struck out under CPR Part 8.13. With no possible judgment against the insured driver, the judge ruled it would be an abuse of process to let the claim continue against his insurer, Colonial Fire and General Insurance Company Limited, and struck that out too. Several paragraphs of the claimant's affidavit were also struck out, and the parties will be heard on costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Failing to serve a claim within the CPR time limit results in automatic striking out against that defendant.
A claim against an insurer can fall away as an abuse of process where no judgment is possible against the insured.
outcome: Claim against the insured automatically struck out for non-service and against the insurer for abuse of process; parts of the claimant's affidavit struck out; costs to be argued.
osint_notes: Procedural motor-insurance matter naming Colonial Fire and General Insurance Company Limited.

title: Persad Ramroop v PC Pramanan Rajpath, The Commissioner of Police and the Attorney General
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Persad Ramroop
individuals.notes: Won detinue/conversion claim for the non-return of his firearm and ammunition left at a restaurant and held by police

social_headline: Police ordered to return gun owner's firearm or pay damages
social_post: Persad Ramroop has won his claim in detinue and conversion against the State over his firearm. He had accidentally left the firearm and ammunition at a restaurant, which handed them to the police, who then kept them. Justice Devindra Rampersad gave judgment for Ramroop against the Attorney General, ordered the firearm and ammunition returned by 20 April 2026, and provided that, in default, the State must pay damages for conversion and loss of use, with damages for detinue running from January 2024. The assessment of damages was referred to a Master, and costs reserved. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Police who retain lawfully owned property without justification can be liable in detinue and conversion.
Courts can order return of property by a deadline, with damages payable in default.
outcome: Judgment for claimant; firearm and ammunition to be returned by 20 April 2026 or damages for conversion payable; detinue damages to be assessed by a Master.
osint_notes: Detinue/conversion claim over a lawfully owned firearm retained by police; limited commercial content.

title: Vijesh Mahadeo v The Ministry of Education and Teaching Service Commission

related_litigation: CV2016-01496 (Order of Rahim J dated 5 December 2016)
social_headline: Teacher wins retroactive Teacher III promotion fight against Education Ministry
social_post: Computer Science teacher Vijesh Mahadeo has won his judicial review against the Ministry of Education and the Teaching Service Commission over their failure to assess and retroactively promote him to Teacher III (Secondary) in Computer Science/Information Technology. Justice Karen Reid granted relief, ordering among other things that he be paid special damages reflecting the salary and increment difference between Teacher II and Teacher III from December 2016 to the present, with no double payment if he is later retroactively promoted for the same period. The defendants were ordered to pay his costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Public bodies can be compelled to assess and retroactively promote employees where they unlawfully fail to do so.
Courts guard against double recovery by offsetting retroactive salary already ordered.
outcome: Judicial review succeeds; relief including retroactive salary/increment differential and costs awarded to the claimant.
osint_notes: Public-sector employment/judicial review matter involving the Ministry of Education and Teaching Service Commission.

title: Quincy Wilson v National Association of Athletics Administrations of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: National Association of Athletics Administrations of Trinidad and Tobago (NAAATT)

companies.ownership_notes: Organiser of the National Open Senior Championships; sued in negligence

individuals.name: Quincy Wilson
individuals.role: claimant; professional discus thrower and national record holder
individuals.notes: Alleged he slipped in an unsafe discus circle at Hasely Crawford Stadium; claim dismissed

social_headline: National discus record holder loses injury claim over stadium throwing circle
social_post: Professional discus thrower and national record holder Quincy Wilson has lost his negligence claim against the National Association of Athletics Administrations of Trinidad and Tobago. Wilson said he slipped and fell during the men's discus final at the 2019 National Open Senior Championships at the Hasely Crawford Stadium because of the unsafe condition of the throwing circle. NAAATT accepted it owed competitors a duty of care but said it had reasonably prepared and inspected the area. Justice Nadia Kangaloo found Wilson failed to prove either a breach of duty or that any act of the Association caused his fall, and dismissed the claim, while noting nothing detracted from his accomplishments as an athlete. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: An organiser's duty of care does not make it liable without proof of breach and causation.
A sports injury claim fails where the claimant cannot show the venue condition caused the fall.
outcome: Claim dismissed for failure to prove breach of duty or causation; costs reserved pending agreement.
osint_notes: Sports-injury negligence claim against the national athletics association; limited commercial content.

title: Alvin Ragoo v Dindial's Hardware Limited

companies.name: Dindial's Hardware Limited
companies.ownership_notes: Operated a hardware store (formerly at Maraval); employed the claimant as a truck driver

individuals.name: Alvin Ragoo
individuals.notes: Injured when the employer's truck's brakes failed during a delivery; awarded damages

financial_figures.what_it_is: Future loss of earnings
Special damages
Interest on special damages (2% from 4 Aug 2017 to judgment)
Prescribed costs (VAT inclusive)

social_headline: Hardware firm liable after truck's brakes failed, injuring its driver
social_post: A former truck driver has won substantial damages against his employer, Dindial's Hardware Limited. Alvin Ragoo was seriously injured when, while making deliveries, the brakes on the company's truck failed and he swerved into a wall to avoid an oncoming vehicle. Justice Joan Charles found the employer liable for knowingly permitting or instructing him to use a faulty truck. The court awarded $53,235 in future loss of earnings, $295,547.50 in special damages with $51,419.44 interest, plus general damages, and costs of $95,220.17, with a 42-day stay of execution. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Employers must not require workers to operate equipment they know to be defective.
Employer's liability claims can yield significant awards for loss of earnings and special damages.
outcome: Judgment for claimant; employer liable for the faulty-truck accident; damages including $53,235 future earnings, $295,547.50 special (plus interest) and $95,220.17 costs.
osint_notes: Names Dindial's Hardware Limited; employer-liability accident with sizeable damages.

title: Dufferin Campbell v The Commissioner of Police

individuals.name: Dufferin Campbell
individuals.notes: Challenged his ill-health retirement under regulation 34; claim dismissed as devoid of merit

social_headline: Officer's challenge to ill-health retirement dismissed
social_post: Police officer Dufferin Campbell has lost his challenge to being retired on the grounds of ill-health. He argued the Commissioner of Police's decision to retire him under regulation 34 of the Police Service Regulations 2007, effective March 2024 without his written consent, was unreasonable, contrary to natural justice, an abuse of power and a breach of his constitutional rights. Justice Frank Seepersad held that a court cannot step into the Commissioner's shoes to reinstate an officer or block a retirement where the conditions under regulation 34 are met, though the Commissioner must act with prudence to protect institutional integrity. Finding the claim devoid of merit, he dismissed it with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts defer to the Commissioner of Police's statutory discretion where the regulatory preconditions for retirement are satisfied.
Judicial review cannot be used to compel reinstatement of an officer lawfully retired on ill-health grounds.
outcome: Claim dismissed as devoid of merit; ill-health retirement upheld; costs to defendant.
osint_notes: Public-sector employment/judicial review matter involving police ill-health retirement.

property_lease.outcome: Summary judgment for claimant; injunctions restraining defendant from entering/trespassing; $15,000 nominal trespass damages

social_headline: Landowner wins summary judgment over Guaracara trespass
social_post: Naurathan Ramsamooj has won summary judgment against Tony Bhagan over trespass on a 10-acre parcel at No. 42 Rebecca Richmond Road, Guaracara. Ramsamooj sought a declaration of ownership and injunctions to keep Bhagan off the land. Justice Robin N. Mohammed granted the injunctions restraining Bhagan from entering, remaining on or trespassing on the subject lands, awarded $15,000 in nominal trespass damages, and dismissed Bhagan's application to strike out the claim. Bhagan was ordered to pay the claimant's costs of the claim and the various interlocutory applications. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Summary judgment is available where a defendant has no realistic prospect of defending a clear trespass claim.
Injunctions plus nominal damages are typical remedies for established trespass to land.
outcome: Summary judgment for claimant; injunctions granted; $15,000 nominal trespass damages; defendant's strike-out dismissed and costs awarded to claimant.
osint_notes: Named 10-acre Guaracara parcel at Rebecca Richmond Road; trespass/possession dispute.

title: Mikali Hamel-Smith v The Nursing Council of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Nursing Council of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Mikali Hamel-Smith
individuals.notes: Challenged refusal to register her; claim dismissed as the Council's decision was open to it

social_headline: Court upholds Nursing Council's refusal to register applicant
social_post: Mikali Hamel-Smith has lost her challenge to the Nursing Council of Trinidad and Tobago's refusal to register her as a nurse. The case turned on whether her Associate of Science in Nursing, recognised by the Accreditation Council of Trinidad and Tobago, satisfied the registration requirements under section 16 of the governing statute, and whether she met the 'fit and proper' standard. Justice Devindra Rampersad held the Council's decision to refuse registration was not unreasonable or an improper exercise of discretion, as it took relevant considerations into account, and the court could not mandate it to reconsider. The claim was dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Professional registration bodies are entitled to deference where they consider relevant statutory criteria.
Judicial review cannot substitute the court's view for a reasonable regulatory decision on qualifications.
outcome: Claim dismissed; refusal of nursing registration upheld; claimant to pay costs.
osint_notes: Professional registration/judicial review matter involving the Nursing Council; limited commercial content.

title: Nicholas Roopchand v The Commissioner of Police

individuals.name: Nicholas Roopchand
individuals.notes: Won judicial review over a 6.5-year delay in deciding his firearm permit/licence applications

individuals.name: Vinai Pakeerah
individuals.notes: Had a similar application processed and licence issued

individuals.name: Dr. Dinesh Martin
individuals.notes: Had a similar application processed and licence issued

social_headline: Court faults six-year police delay on firearm licence application
social_post: Nicholas Roopchand has won his judicial review against the Commissioner of Police over a years-long delay in deciding his firearm applications. He had applied for a Provisional Firearms Permit and a Firearm User's Licence back in March 2017, and more than six and a half years later still had no decision, while two other applicants, Vinai Pakeerah and Dr Dinesh Martin, had their later applications processed and licences issued. Justice Joan Charles granted declarations that the prolonged delay was unfair, irrational and unreasonable and amounted to unequal treatment by a public body, and ordered the Commissioner to pay Roopchand damages and costs to be assessed by a Master. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Public bodies must decide applications within a reasonable time; years of unexplained delay is unlawful.
Treating similar applicants differently can amount to unlawful inequality of treatment.
outcome: Judicial review granted; declarations of unreasonable delay and unequal treatment; damages and costs to be assessed by a Master.
osint_notes: Judicial review over firearm-licence processing delay; comparators named; limited commercial content.

title: Patrick Trumpet v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Patrick Trumpet
individuals.notes: Partly succeeded in claim arising from his detention at the Four Roads Police Station

financial_figures.what_it_is: General damages
Exemplary damages
Prescribed costs awarded to claimant

related_litigation: The Attorney General v Joel Roop, Civil Appeal No. P182 of 2015
social_headline: Detainee at Four Roads station wins $35,000 over breach of his rights
social_post: Patrick Trumpet has partly succeeded in his claim against the State over his treatment as a detainee at the Four Roads Police Station. Although he lost on most of his claim, Justice Devindra Rampersad ruled in his favour on an important aspect concerning the duty owed to him while detained under inquiries, awarding $20,000 in general damages with interest and $15,000 in exemplary damages. Reflecting the mixed outcome, the judge awarded Trumpet a modest $10,250 in costs rather than requiring him to pay the State's, given that the point he won had not been fully addressed by the parties until the court raised it. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The State owes duties to detainees held under inquiry, breach of which can attract exemplary damages.
Costs can reflect partial success, especially where the winning point was raised by the court.
outcome: Partial success; $20,000 general and $15,000 exemplary damages awarded with $10,250 costs to claimant.
osint_notes: Civil detention claim against the State; partial success; limited commercial content.

title: Helene Morrison v The Attorney General and The Commissioner of Police
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Helene Morrison
individuals.notes: Retired on medical grounds in 2020; won breach-of-contract claim over delayed retirement benefits, with the State's set-off limited to lawful deductions

social_headline: Retired police sergeant wins fight over withheld pension and gratuity
social_post: Former Police Sergeant Helene Morrison has won her claim against the State over her retirement benefits. Morrison, who retired on medical grounds in February 2020, said the failure to compute and pay her pension and gratuity, and the attempt to set off an alleged indebtedness against them, breached her contract and Regulation 183 of the Police Service Regulations. Justice Westmin James gave judgment for Morrison, ordering payment of her benefits less only lawful deductions from December 2017 and less interim payments, with interest, but dismissed her claims for aggravated, exemplary and constitutional relief. She was awarded costs on the total sum. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The State cannot indefinitely withhold retirement benefits or set off unproven debts against them.
Contractual and regulatory pension entitlements can be enforced even where constitutional and exemplary claims fail.
outcome: Judgment for claimant on contract; benefits payable less lawful deductions plus interest; aggravated, exemplary and constitutional claims dismissed; costs to claimant.
osint_notes: Pension/retirement-benefits dispute for a former Police Sergeant; limited commercial content.

property_lease.address_or_description: 76 Independence Square, Port of Spain (occupied by Triples Sizzling Tasty Doubles and a Hot Roti shop)
property_lease.outcome: First defendant's bid to set aside the judgment of 11 February 2026 dismissed; judgment and consequential orders remain in force

social_headline: Judgment over Independence Square doubles-and-roti premises stands
social_post: Justice Westmin James has refused to set aside a judgment over commercial premises at 76 Independence Square, Port of Spain, occupied by food vendors including Triples Sizzling Tasty Doubles and a Hot Roti shop. After judgment was entered for claimant Dinora Rojas on 11 February 2026, the first defendant, Dion Legall, applied to have it set aside. Treating the setting aside of a judgment as an equitable, discretionary remedy that takes account of the applicant's conduct, the court declined to exercise its discretion and dismissed the application, leaving the judgment and consequential orders in full force, with the first defendant ordered to pay the costs of the application. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Setting aside a regular judgment is a discretionary equitable remedy in which the applicant's conduct matters.
A weak or unexplained application to set aside judgment will be dismissed with costs.
outcome: Application to set aside the 11 February 2026 judgment dismissed; judgment remains in force; first defendant to pay costs.
osint_notes: Names 76 Independence Square premises and food vendors Triples Sizzling Tasty Doubles and Hot Roti; commercial occupation dispute.

title: Reynold Rice v The Commissioner of Police
court: High Court of Justice (Port of Spain)

individuals.name: Reynold Rice
individuals.notes: Sought habeas corpus to be released from custody; application dismissed

criminal_individuals.name: Reynold Rice
offences: Suspected manufacture of marijuana / dangerous drugs offences (under investigation)
criminal_individuals.notes: Held after the IOU allegedly found a marijuana chemical farm at No. 5 Flamingo Street, Orange Grove Road, Trincity; habeas corpus application dismissed

social_headline: Habeas corpus refused for man held over Trincity marijuana 'chemical farm'
social_post: A High Court judge has refused to release Reynold Rice on a habeas corpus application after his detention in a drugs investigation. The application stemmed from a search at an apartment at No. 5 Flamingo Street, Orange Grove Road, Trincity, where members of the police Intelligence Operations Unit allegedly discovered a marijuana chemical farm and seized items linked to manufacturing marijuana. Justice Christopher Sieuchand dismissed Rice's application for a writ of habeas corpus seeking his immediate release, and ordered him to pay the Commissioner's costs, with the quantum reserved. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Habeas corpus will not secure release where there is a lawful basis for the detention.
Challenges to detention are distinct from the merits of any underlying criminal charge.
outcome: Habeas corpus application dismissed; applicant remains in custody and ordered to pay costs.
osint_notes: Habeas corpus arising from a marijuana-manufacturing investigation at Trincity; crime tag retained for tracking.

title: Keith Khan v Water and Sewerage Authority of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: Water and Sewerage Authority of Trinidad and Tobago (WASA)

companies.name: Water and Sewerage Authority of Trinidad and Tobago (WASA)
companies.ownership_notes: Argued unsuccessfully that the dispute was a trade dispute for the Industrial Court

individuals.name: Keith Khan
individuals.notes: Resisted WASA's jurisdiction challenge; claim allowed to proceed in the High Court

social_headline: High Court keeps jurisdiction over retired WASA worker's claim
social_post: The High Court has rejected WASA's bid to throw out a claim by retired employee Keith Khan on jurisdiction grounds. The Water and Sewerage Authority argued the dispute was really a 'trade dispute' that should have been reported to the Minister and decided by the Industrial Court, not the High Court. Justice Robin N. Mohammed disagreed, refused WASA's application to dispute jurisdiction, and held its accompanying application for relief from sanctions was misconceived. WASA was ordered to file its defence within 28 days and pay the costs of the application, with a case management conference set for October 2026. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Not every dispute involving an employer is a 'trade dispute' for the Industrial Court; matters of legal interpretation can remain in the High Court.
A jurisdiction challenge that fails can result in directions to file a defence and a costs order.
outcome: WASA's application to dispute jurisdiction and for relief from sanctions dismissed; defence to be filed within 28 days; costs to claimant.
osint_notes: Jurisdiction dispute involving WASA and a retired employee; limited commercial content.

title: Dhanash Teemul v Reynold Teemul
court: High Court of Justice (San Fernando Sub-Registry)

individuals.name: Dhanash Teemul
individuals.notes: Successfully resisted application to set aside a default judgment

individuals.name: Reynold Teemul
individuals.notes: Application to set aside default judgment dismissed

social_headline: Default judgment stands as bid to reopen Teemul family case fails
social_post: A default judgment entered in favour of Dhanash Teemul against Reynold Teemul will stand. After the default judgment was entered in February 2024 for failure to file a defence in time, Reynold Teemul applied to set it aside and to file a defence out of time. Justice Robin N. Mohammed found the application lacked a proper evidential foundation and dismissed it, confirming the default judgment in full force. Because the defendant was legally aided by the Legal Aid and Advisory Authority, the court made no order as to costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: An application to set aside a default judgment needs a proper evidential basis and a defence with a realistic prospect of success.
Courts generally avoid making costs orders against legally aided parties.
outcome: Application to set aside default judgment dismissed; default judgment confirmed; no order as to costs.
osint_notes: Procedural default-judgment dispute between family members; limited commercial content.

title: Narace Dwarpaul v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Narace Dwarpaul
individuals.notes: Won malicious prosecution, wrongful arrest and false imprisonment claim arising from his 11 June 2016 arrest

financial_figures.what_it_is: General damages
Pre-judgment interest on general damages

Prescribed costs

social_headline: State pays $92,500-plus for wrongful arrest and malicious prosecution
social_post: Narace Dwarpaul has won damages against the State for malicious prosecution, wrongful arrest and false imprisonment over his arrest on 11 June 2016. After a trial in which Police Constable Sujeet Ramcharan and an Assistant Superintendent gave evidence for the State, Justice Robin N. Mohammed found in Dwarpaul's favour and awarded $85,000 in general damages plus $13,332.19 interest, and $7,500 in special damages with further interest. The claims for aggravated and exemplary damages were dismissed, and the State was ordered to pay $25,023.10 in prescribed costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Malicious prosecution requires proof of absence of reasonable and probable cause and malice.
A successful false-imprisonment claimant may recover general and special damages even where aggravated/exemplary claims fail.
outcome: Judgment for claimant: $85,000 general and $7,500 special damages (plus interest) and $25,023.10 costs; aggravated/exemplary claims dismissed.
osint_notes: Civil claim against the State for wrongful arrest/malicious prosecution; limited commercial content.

title: Devin Reid v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Devin Reid
individuals.role: claimant; former member of the T&T Defence Force
individuals.notes: Constitutional motion over delayed terminal benefits dismissed, but awarded costs

social_headline: Ex-soldier loses terminal-benefits motion but is awarded costs
social_post: Former Defence Force member Devin Reid has lost his constitutional motion over the delay in remitting his terminal benefits, but the court still awarded him costs. Reid complained about the delay in paying benefits due under the Defence (Pensions, Terminal and Other Grants) Regulations. An earlier 2024 ruling had already struck out his section 4(a) claim, since military officers cannot sue to recover lost income or emoluments. Justice Karen Reid dismissed the remaining claim but, noting his conduct was reasonable and that the delay in receiving his benefits had contributed to his financial difficulties, ordered the State to pay his costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Military officers generally cannot sue to recover lost income or emoluments by constitutional motion.
Costs can be awarded to an unsuccessful claimant where their conduct was reasonable and circumstances justify it.
outcome: Claim dismissed; costs nonetheless awarded to the claimant.
osint_notes: Constitutional motion on military terminal benefits; limited commercial content.

social_headline: Longdenville landowner wins possession and injunction against trespasser
social_post: Harold Manoo has won his claim to land at 77 Ravine Sable Road, Longdenville, against Devanand Mangram. Manoo traced his title to a 1974/1975 deed under which he and several relatives became seised of the disputed lands in fee simple. Justice Karen Reid upheld his entitlement to possession and, after 30 days, restrained Mangram, his servants and agents from entering, remaining on, building on or otherwise interfering with the land. Mangram was ordered to pay $10,000 in nominal damages and $14,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Documentary title under a registered deed strongly supports a possession claim.
Trespass to land attracts injunctive relief and at least nominal damages.
outcome: Judgment for claimant; possession upheld, permanent injunction after 30 days, $10,000 nominal damages and $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Named Longdenville property at 77 Ravine Sable Road with deed references; trespass/possession dispute.
