social_headline: Both sides lose in car-sale dispute with Prime Autos
social_post: A dispute over an oral car-sale agreement between Terrence Hoyte and dealer Prime Autos Limited has ended with neither side winning. Hoyte sued to recover monies he said were due from Prime Autos, claiming $60,000 in total including $31,950 from the sale, while the dealer counterclaimed for storage fees. Justice Robin N. Mohammed found that the proceeds from the sale of the vehicle exceeded the reasonable storage fees the dealer proved, so the dealer suffered no net loss, and dismissed both Hoyte's claim and Prime Autos' counterclaim, ordering each party to bear its own costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Oral sale agreements are enforceable but require clear proof of the agreed terms and resulting loss.
Where claim and counterclaim each fail, courts may order each party to bear its own costs.
outcome: Both claim and counterclaim dismissed; no order as to costs.
osint_notes: Names Prime Autos Limited (motor dealer); vehicle-sale contract dispute with quantified sums.

social_headline: Retired customs boss wins big payout for wrongful arrest by police
social_post: Peter Mohammed, a retired Assistant Comptroller of Customs and Excise, has won substantial damages against the State and two police officers, PCs Julio Villafana and Kimraj Ramlochan, for assault and battery, wrongful arrest, false imprisonment and malicious prosecution stemming from his 2013 arrest. Justice Robin N. Mohammed awarded general damages with interest, $75,000 in exemplary damages, and special damages of $428,760.50 plus interest, while dismissing his claims for loss of gratuity and pension. The defendants were also ordered to pay prescribed costs of $87,678.94. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Police misconduct in arrest and prosecution can attract exemplary damages and substantial special damages.
Claims for loss of pension and gratuity require distinct proof and may fail even where the main claim succeeds.
outcome: Judgment for claimant: general damages plus $75,000 exemplary and $428,760.50 special damages (with interest) and $87,678.94 costs; loss of gratuity/pension claims dismissed.
osint_notes: Civil claim against the State and named officers for wrongful arrest; sizeable special-damages award.

title: Teckla Reason v The Teaching Service Commission

individuals.name: Teckla Reason
individuals.notes: Won FOIA judicial review compelling disclosure of records about successful candidates' qualifications and scores

social_headline: Teaching Service Commission ordered to disclose candidates' scores and certificates
social_post: Teckla Reason has won her Freedom of Information judicial review against the Teaching Service Commission. After the Commission failed to fully answer her September 2022 FOIA request, Justice Karen Reid granted an order of mandamus compelling it to provide, within 21 days, the requested documents and information, copies of the actual certificates and qualifications of the successful candidates, and the scores of each successful candidate. The Commission was ordered to pay her costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: FOIA can compel disclosure of qualifications and scores of successful candidates in public recruitment.
Public bodies that fail to meet FOIA obligations risk mandamus orders and costs.
outcome: Judicial review granted; mandamus to disclose requested records, certificates and candidate scores within 21 days; costs to claimant.
osint_notes: FOIA transparency case against the Teaching Service Commission.

title: Dev-Anand Taklalsingh v Assuria Life T&T Limited

financial_figures.what_it_is: Prescribed costs awarded to defendant

social_headline: Policyholder's claim over lapsed Assuria life policy struck out
social_post: Dev-Anand Taklalsingh's breach-of-contract claim against Assuria Life T&T Limited has been struck out. The insurer said the Universal Life policy lapsed on 20 May 2022 because there were insufficient funds to cover the rising cost of insurance, a situation worsened by a substantial partial surrender of the policy's cash value back in 2004, and that Taklalsingh declined options to reinstate it in 2023. Justice Westmin James agreed this was a clear and obvious case where the claim was unsustainable as a matter of law, struck it out, entered judgment for Assuria, and ordered the claimant to pay $15,049.14 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A universal life policy can lapse when its cash value cannot cover rising cost-of-insurance charges.
A claim with no realistic legal basis may be struck out as an abuse of process before trial.
outcome: Claim struck out; judgment for the insurer; claimant to pay $15,049.14 costs.
osint_notes: Names Assuria Life T&T Limited; lapsed Universal Life policy dispute.

social_post: The estate of Sumintra Bissessar, represented by Dale Ramdeen, has partly won a Freedom of Information judicial review against the Ministry of Planning and Development. Justice Devindra Rampersad set aside the impugned decision and ordered the Ministry to give the claimant access, within 28 days, to the documents requested at items 2 and 3 of her FOIA request under sections 16 and 18 of the Freedom of Information Act. Courts can compel ministries to provide access to specific requested records under the FOIA.

social_headline: Inmate's assault claim against prison officers dismissed
social_post: Jehrome Wharwood's claim that he was beaten by prison officers at the Maximum Security Prison has been dismissed. He alleged that on 7 January 2018, while inmates in his cell were getting haircuts before a court hearing, officers assaulted him. The State said the officers used only reasonable force to restrain him and that he had exaggerated the incident and his injuries. Justice Margaret Mohammed accepted the State's case and dismissed the claim. Because his counsel valued the claim at $252,500, he was ordered to pay the State $46,750 in prescribed costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Prison officers may use reasonable force to restrain inmates without incurring liability.
Exaggerated injury claims can undermine credibility and lead to dismissal with substantial costs.
outcome: Claim dismissed; reasonable force found; claimant to pay $46,750 costs.
osint_notes: Inmate assault claim against the State; limited commercial content.

social_headline: Gasparillo homeowners win breach case against electrical contractor
social_post: Samdai and Randy Sooknanan have won their breach-of-contract claim against Kemetic Electrical Wiring Services, a sole trader run by Godfrey Cupid, over electrical works at their property at 73 Bonne Aventure Road, Gasparillo. After hiring the contractor in mid-2019 for works on a building under construction, the Sooknanans terminated the agreement in October 2019. Justice Margaret Mohammed declared the contractor had breached the agreement, entitling the claimants to terminate, awarded them $10,000 in nominal damages, dismissed the contractor's counterclaim, and ordered it to pay $3,000 in costs on the claim and $20,250 on the counterclaim. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A party can lawfully terminate a contract where the other side is in breach.
A failed counterclaim can carry significant costs based on the amount counterclaimed.
outcome: Judgment for claimants; breach declared; $10,000 nominal damages; counterclaim dismissed; contractor to pay $23,250 total costs.
osint_notes: Names Kemetic Electrical Wiring Services and principal Godfrey Cupid; Gasparillo property at 73 Bonne Aventure Road.

social_headline: Neighbours lose bid to block access road in bitter Couva land feud
social_post: A bitter dispute between neighbouring landowners in Mc Bean, Couva, over a narrow access trace has ended against the family claiming it exclusively. The legal personal representatives of the estate of Ramchandar Mewah and a beneficiary sought a permanent injunction to stop neighbour Randolph Rampersad from using the trace connecting the Calcutta No. 1 Main Road to their land, alleging trespass after he broke down a gate. Justice Robin N. Mohammed rejected their exclusive-ownership claim, ordered the claimants to remove all gates, chains, fences and obstructions from the trace within 14 days, and directed them to pay the defendant $28,000 in costs plus $7,500 toward the joint expert's fee. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A claim to exclusive ownership of a shared access road requires clear title evidence.
A party who unlawfully obstructs a right of way can be ordered to remove the obstructions and pay costs.
outcome: Claimants' exclusive-ownership/trespass claim dismissed; claimants ordered to clear obstructions and pay $28,000 costs plus $7,500 expert-fee reimbursement.

social_headline: $19.5M Point Lisas land appeal: Tobago Gas wins leave to amend grounds
social_post: The Court of Appeal has allowed Tobago Gas Trading and Logistics Limited to amend its grounds of appeal in a major Point Lisas land dispute. At trial, the company was ordered to deliver up vacant possession of about 13.71 hectares of land to Point Lisas Industrial Port Development Corporation Limited (PLIPDECO), remove all structures and equipment, and pay more than TT$19.5 million in mesne profits, interest and costs. In this procedural ruling, Justice of Appeal Donaldson-Honeywell granted permission to amend the grounds and set a timetable, with the substantive appeal fixed for hearing on 3 December 2026. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Appellate courts may permit amendment of grounds of appeal where it serves the just determination of the appeal.
Large mesne-profits awards turn on the period and value of wrongful occupation of commercial land.

social_headline: State liable for malicious prosecution over 2009 sexual-assault and robbery charges
social_post: Devin Kern Glasgow has won damages against the State for malicious prosecution. In December 2009, a police officer laid five serious charges against him, including grievous sexual assault, sexual intercourse without consent, buggery and two counts of breaking and entering and robbery. After those charges were not sustained, Glasgow sued, and Justice Robin N. Mohammed found the prosecution malicious, awarding general, aggravated, special and exemplary damages, with interest, and prescribed costs of $62,219.70. The judgment carries a 28-day stay of execution. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Even very serious charges, if pursued without reasonable and probable cause and with malice, can ground a malicious prosecution claim.
Malicious prosecution damages can include aggravated and exemplary heads with interest.
outcome: Judgment for claimant in malicious prosecution; general, aggravated, special and exemplary damages with interest, plus $62,219.70 costs and a 28-day stay.
osint_notes: Malicious prosecution arising from serious sexual-offence and robbery charges; crime tags retained for tracking; child/sexual-sensitive content kept factual.

social_headline: Court won't jail company director over unpaid judgment debt
social_post: The High Court has declined to commit a company director to prison over an unpaid judgment debt. The Oilfield Workers Trade Union, acting for worker Andre Burkett, sought to commit Anthony Greene, a director of The Asset and Risk Engineering Group Limited, for contempt after the company failed to satisfy court orders to pay a judgment debt. Treating the matter as a judgment summons, Justice Ricky Rahim held that committing someone for non-payment of a money judgment simpliciter would undermine the protections Parliament built into the Debtors Act, and declined to do so, also refusing the company's late bid to file affidavits. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Imprisonment for mere non-payment of a money judgment is constrained by the Debtors Act.
Contempt/committal is not a routine enforcement tool for ordinary money judgments.
outcome: Committal of the director declined; the court would not imprison for non-payment of a money judgment simpliciter.
osint_notes: Names The Asset and Risk Engineering Group Limited and director Anthony Greene; OWTU judgment-debt enforcement.

social_headline: Contractor's $80M-plus claim against Tobago House of Assembly dismissed
social_post: A contractor's massive claim against the Tobago House of Assembly has been thrown out. Raghunath Singh and Co. Ltd. sought more than $27 million in special damages plus over $53 million in interest from the THA arising from a construction contract. Justice Frank Seepersad dismissed the claim, holding that parties who agree contractual mechanisms for certification and dispute resolution must observe them, and that courts should not allow stale claims to be resurrected long after the contractual machinery has ceased to operate and the evidential foundation for fair adjudication has diminished. The contractor was ordered to pay $636,590.07 in prescribed costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Construction claims must follow the contract's certification and dispute-resolution machinery and be pursued promptly.
Courts are wary of resurrecting stale, very large claims where contemporaneous evidence has faded.
outcome: Claim dismissed as stale; contractor to pay $636,590.07 prescribed costs.
osint_notes: Names Raghunath Singh and Co. Ltd. and the Tobago House of Assembly; construction claim exceeding $80M (damages plus interest) dismissed.

The defendants applied under CPR Part 26.2 to strike out his statement of case, but Justice Robin N. The defendants were left to deal with the costs of the unsuccessful application. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.

A surviving claim proceeds to case management with costs of the failed application typically following the event.

social_headline: Court orders Registrar General to register disputed Rio Claro estate assent
social_post: A beneficiary has won a judicial review compelling the Registrar General to register a Memorandum of Assent over estate land at 273 Tabaquite Road, Rio Claro. The Registrar General had raised a Real Property Act query, treating the parcel as part of the residue of the estate of Mohanlal Kissoonsingh and insisting all parties execute the assent, before later varying its position. Justice Robin N. Mohammed granted an order of mandamus compelling the Registrar General to register the Memorandum of Assent dated 7 April 2021 and endorse it on the Certificate of Title, with costs certified fit for Senior and Junior Counsel. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Mandamus can compel the Registrar General to register a duly executed instrument where queries are unfounded.
Persistent or shifting registry queries that frustrate registration may be challenged by judicial review.
outcome: Judicial review granted; mandamus to register the Memorandum of Assent and endorse the Certificate of Title; costs certified for Senior and Junior Counsel.

social_headline: State refused relief from sanctions after missing affidavit deadline
social_post: The State has been refused relief from sanctions in a claim brought by Clayton Lucas. The Attorney General applied a year late to be allowed to file the affidavit of Sheldon Von Sama, which should have been filed by June 2025. Justice Margaret Mohammed noted the delay had already caused the 27 May 2026 trial date to be lost and that granting the application would cause yet more delay, since the claimant would then need to respond and possibly seek to cross-examine the witness. She dismissed the State's application and ordered it to pay the claimant's costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Relief from sanctions requires a prompt application and a good explanation for the breach.
Applications that would derail a fixed trial date face significant resistance from the court.
outcome: State's application for relief from sanctions and extension of time dismissed; costs to claimant.
osint_notes: Procedural relief-from-sanctions ruling against the State; limited commercial content.

social_headline: Excavator seizure claim against the State dismissed
social_post: Rivulet Investment Group Limited has lost its detinue and conversion claim against the State over a seized New Holland Evo 215C excavator. The company said police of the Trinidad and Tobago Police Service seized and detained its excavator, and the matter went to trial on liability against the Attorney General. After the claimant's sole witness testified, the State chose not to cross-examine and made a no-case submission, calling no evidence. Justice Robin N. Mohammed found the claimant had not proven its case against the State and dismissed the amended claim, ordering it to pay $14,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A claimant bears the burden of proving detinue or conversion even where the defendant calls no evidence.
A no-case submission can succeed where the claimant's own evidence fails to establish liability.
outcome: Claim against the State (third defendant) dismissed; claimant to pay $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Names Rivulet Investment Group Limited, Arabco Company Limited and Husayn Al-Fayed; seized New Holland excavator.

social_headline: Father wins stake in Petit Valley house his daughter bought from Sir Ellis Clarke's estate
social_post: A family property dispute over land once owned by former President Sir Ellis Clarke has been resolved with a split outcome. Dennis Prince built the upstairs of a concrete house on land at Simeon Road, Petit Valley, which his daughter Lauren Prince later bought in 2021 from Sir Ellis Clarke's estate without his knowledge and then advertised for sale. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC declared that Dennis Prince holds an equitable interest in the upstairs portion he built, while Lauren Prince holds the freehold and an equitable interest in the downstairs. He and his family may remain as licensees, and if the land is sold she must pay him $200,000 or the market value of the upstairs, whichever is greater. Each party bears its own costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Building on another's land in reliance on family arrangements can found an equitable interest by proprietary estoppel.
Courts can protect a contributor's interest through occupation licences and payment obligations on sale.
outcome: Claimant granted an equitable interest in the upstairs portion; freehold held by first defendant; occupation as licensees; $200,000 or market value payable on sale; each party bears own costs.
osint_notes: Named Petit Valley property formerly owned by Sir Ellis Clarke; family proprietary-estoppel dispute with payment on sale.

The negligence and occupier's-liability case drew on police station diaries, prisoner register extracts and the claimant's medical file from the North Central Regional Health Authority, with five witnesses giving evidence for Peters and none for the State. social_headline: Court of Appeal upholds $400,000 unfair-dismissal award against S.M. Jaleel
social_post: The Court of Appeal has dismissed S.M. Jaleel and Company Limited's appeal against an Industrial Court ruling that its former HR Manager, Allan Raghunanan, was dismissed in circumstances that were harsh and oppressive. Raghunanan, who rose to Vice President of Human Resources, said Chairman Dr. A. Mohammed forced him out in 2010. The Industrial Court awarded him $400,000, which remained unpaid even after a stay was refused in 2022. Justice Pemberton, with Wilson and Henderson JJA agreeing, held the Court cannot rehear findings of fact and affirmed the award, with no order as to costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.

social_headline: State ordered to pay millions after police failures preceded woman's domestic-violence murder
social_post: A High Court judge has assessed damages against the State over the 2017 murder of Samantha Stacey Isaacs, killed by her former partner Kahriym Garcia after police repeatedly failed to act on her reports of abuse. Justice Robin Mohammed had earlier found the TTPS failed to investigate her first three reports, treated her fourth report perfunctorily, never prosecuted Garcia, and that a Magistrate prematurely dismissed her protection-order application. The Court awarded $960,000 for lost years, $450,000 and $600,000 for constitutional breaches, and $500,000 vindicatory damages, plus interest, to her mother Tot Lampkin and orphaned son Kaiden Garcia. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: State can be liable in constitutional damages for police/judicial failures in domestic-violence cases
Vindicatory damages available to mark the gravity of systemic State failures

social_headline: Tobago family land feud: court splits Windward Road acre after decades of dispute
social_post: A long and acrimonious family land dispute over a one-acre parcel along the Windward Road in Mt. St. George, Tobago has been resolved by Justice Ricky Rahim. Glen Quashie-Romeo claimed title to the whole acre; her relatives, the Quashie defendants, claimed adverse possession. The Court declared Quashie-Romeo the paper-title owner of the surveyed 5,137-square-metre parcel, save for the house spot the first three defendants occupy, whose title was extinguished by adverse possession. The fourth defendant's attempted new structure was rejected. The defendants must pay $3,000 nominal damages for cutting down an electricity post. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Adverse possession can extinguish paper title to a specific occupied house spot only
Vague claims of possession over wider land will fail without cogent evidence
outcome: Claimant declared owner except for defendants' adversely-possessed house spot; $3,000 nominal trespass damages

social_headline: Three men sentenced for 2019 Freeport wake stabbing that killed 'Pedro'
social_post: Justice Nalini Singh has sentenced three men over the November 2019 killing of Bissram Ramkissoon, known as 'Pedro', at a wake in Freeport. Clarence Frederick ('Tallman'), Errol Cruickshank ('Hulk') and Steven Ronald Singh ('Smeagle') pleaded guilty to unlawful act manslaughter and to maliciously wounding Jim Jones Narine, on an agreed 24-year starting point. According to the plea facts, Singh handed a knife to Cruickshank, Frederick restrained Pedro in a hug, and Cruickshank stabbed him fatally. Frederick received 14 years and the other two 15 years four months, each less roughly six-plus years already served. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Guilty pleas attract a one-third sentencing discount
Joint participation (restraining, supplying weapon) grounds manslaughter liability for all
outcome: All three convicted on guilty pleas; sentenced to 14 years (Frederick) and 15 years 4 months (Cruickshank, Singh), less time served

social_post: Justice Westmin James has ruled that Cletus Sheppard acquired ownership of No. 9 Bushe Street North, Petit Bourg, San Juan, by adverse possession. Sheppard occupied the abandoned property from 2003, parking vehicles, hosting family events and running a full-time welding and fabrication business, hiring watchmen for security. The heirs of the late owners James and Eileen Brown claimed his use was merely permissive, but the Court found continuous factual possession with intention to possess for over 16 years, extinguishing the estate's paper title under the Real Property Limitation Act. Sheppard was awarded possession and $500 nominal trespass damages. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: 16 years' open, exclusive possession can extinguish a paper owner's title

social_headline: Court refuses habeas corpus for Hadeeds held over alleged plot to assassinate PM
social_post: Justice Frank Seepersad has dismissed applications by Dominic and Genevieve Hadeed for a writ of habeas corpus and interim relief challenging their detention under the Emergency Powers Regulations 2026. The pair were held during the State of Emergency declared in March 2026, with authorities alleging intelligence of a plot to assassinate the sitting Prime Minister and senior members of her government. Represented by a senior legal team including Douglas Mendes SC, Gilbert Peterson SC and Faris Al-Rawi SC, they argued their detention was unlawful. The Court found a serious issue to be tried but held the balance of convenience favoured continued detention, directing a judicial review hearing for 27 July 2026. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts weigh national-security intelligence heavily in emergency-detention habeas applications
A serious issue to be tried does not guarantee interim release
outcome: Habeas corpus and interim relief dismissed; judicial review claim to proceed to hearing 27 July 2026
osint_notes: High-profile State of Emergency detention of Dominic and Genevieve Hadeed tied to alleged assassination plot against the Prime Minister; senior counsel involved

social_headline: Sangre Grande adverse-possession claim fails; buyer gets Lot 106
social_post: Justice Devindra Rampersad has ruled for Ronelle Mohammed in a long-running Sangre Grande land dispute. Ayoola Brathwaite claimed adverse possession of Lot 106 at Blake Avenue Extension, relying on her late father Oswald Brathwaite's cultivation of the land from 1984 and a foundation he built in 1991. Mohammed had bought the lot from Park Enterprises Ltd for $260,000 in 2015 after a mortgage survey. The Court found the defendant's evidence of possession vague and inconsistent, an abandoned intention rather than continuous occupation, and struck out her counterclaim, ordering her to deliver up possession and pay $28,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: An unfinished foundation shows intention, not continuous possession
Adverse possession requires precise, cogent evidence of exclusive occupation
outcome: Judgment for second claimant; adverse-possession counterclaim struck out; possession ordered; costs $28,000
osint_notes: Named company Park Enterprises Ltd and land sale; adverse possession

social_headline: Brother v brother: Operations Manager loses constructive-dismissal case against family firm
social_post: Justice Westmin James has dismissed a constructive-dismissal claim by Rabichand Tirbany against Monar Industrial Services (Caribbean) Limited, run by his younger brother, Managing Director Mr. Deonath. Tirbany, Operations Manager for 17 years on $22,000 a month, said a hostile June 2024 meeting restricted him to field-only work and sidelined him, forcing his resignation. The Court found he voluntarily resigned to pursue his own new company, RAV4 General Contracting Services Ltd, and that no repudiatory breach occurred. Even if it had, he was paid in full through his notice period and suffered no recoverable loss. His claim was dismissed with $14,000 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Constructive dismissal requires a repudiatory breach, not mere dissatisfaction
Claimants must disclose post-termination earnings to prove loss
outcome: Claim dismissed; costs $14,000 to defendant; 28-day stay

social_headline: State pays Khan brothers for wrongful arrest in botched 2019 Carapichaima raid
social_post: Master Rishma Ramrattan has assessed damages for Wafiq and Siddiq Khan, wrongly arrested after a November 2019 police raid at their Carapichaima home where officers in black scaled the wall. Officers claimed to find two rounds of ammunition and marijuana in an outside box and charged the family with firearm, ammunition and marijuana possession, but the DPP discontinued all charges within weeks. Liability was conceded by consent. Each brother was awarded $60,000 for false imprisonment and $75,000 for malicious prosecution, plus interest and costs. Related claims by other family members were assessed separately. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The State concedes liability where charges are discontinued without basis
Special-damages claims fail without documentary proof

social_headline: CCN and journalist Mark Bassant ordered to pay $350,000 for branding man an ISIS terrorist
social_post: Justice Avason Quinlan-Williams has found C.C.N. Television Limited and journalist Mark Bassant liable in defamation over the 2015 'ISIS Files Part I' broadcast, which named Clint Rivers among Trinidadians said to be lured to jihad in Syria. Rivers, a follower of Islam, had actually travelled abroad for pilgrimage in 2014. The Court held the defence of Reynolds (responsible journalism) privilege failed and that accusing a citizen of being an ISIS terrorist carries immense social stigma. Rivers was awarded $350,000 in general damages inclusive of aggravation, with costs, and a 30-day stay of execution. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Responsible-journalism (Reynolds) privilege requires genuine verification
Serious false allegations attract high defamation awards
outcome: Judgment for claimant; $350,000 general damages inclusive of aggravation; costs; 30-day stay
osint_notes: Defamation against CCN Television and named journalist Mark Bassant over 'ISIS Files' broadcast

social_headline: Tour firms win $495,000 refund over faulty digital billboard screens
social_post: Justice Westmin James has ordered Creation Group Limited, Creation Advertising Limited and director Joey Sukhdeo to jointly and severally refund $495,000 to Unlimited Tours and Travels Limited and Krystal Tours Vacations Limited. The travel companies bought two dual-sided digital advertising screens in 2018 that repeatedly malfunctioned and were neither of merchantable quality nor fit for purpose. The Court rejected the defence that voltage problems at the claimants' premises were to blame, but dismissed a larger $1.449 million loss-of-profits claim as unproven and the fraudulent-misrepresentation claim. Costs of about $73,000 were awarded. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Suppliers are liable for goods not of merchantable quality under the Sale of Goods Act
Loss-of-profits claims fail without evidence of net profit
outcome: Judgment for claimants; $495,000 damages (joint and several); loss-of-profits and fraud claims dismissed; ~$73,000 costs

social_headline: Appeal Court: accused have no right to appeal a Master's referral to trial on corruption charges
social_post: The Court of Appeal has dismissed appeals by Wendell Diaz and Michael Arjune, both indicted for corruption under the Prevention of Corruption Act, who challenged a Master's decision to refer them for trial in the High Court after a sufficiency hearing. Delivering the judgment, Justice Mark Mohammed held that an accused has no right of appeal under the Supreme Court of Judicature Act against such a referral under the Administration of Justice (Indictable Proceedings) Act, so the Court of Appeal had no jurisdiction. A trial judge also cannot quash an indictment for insufficient evidence. The men can still raise no-case submissions at trial. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: No right of appeal exists against a Master's committal for trial under AJIPA
Accused must challenge weak evidence via no-case submissions, not appeal
outcome: Appeals dismissed for want of jurisdiction
osint_notes: Procedural criminal appeal; corruption charges against named accused

social_headline: Tobago mother-in-law's eviction of ex-daughter-in-law paused pending appeal
social_post: The Court of Appeal has granted a conditional stay halting the eviction of Jerenia Robley-Guy from a two-storey house at Bethlehem Road, Bethel, Tobago, where she has lived since 1985. Her former mother-in-law, Romelda Guy, holds paper title and obtained a varied consent order naming Robley-Guy among those to be removed, though Robley-Guy was never a party to the original proceedings. Justice Pemberton, with Henderson and Rahim JJA, set aside a single judge's refusal of a stay, finding eviction could cause irreversible loss before her arguable appeal is heard. The stay is conditional on preserving the property, with the appeal expedited. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts may grant conditional stays where eviction risks irreversible loss
Consent orders bind only through proper parties and procedure
outcome: Conditional stay of execution granted; appeal expedited; single judge's costs order set aside
osint_notes: Tobago family property/eviction dispute

social_headline: Prison officer loses NIS benefits fight over employer's unfilled form
social_post: Justice Devindra Rampersad has dismissed a judicial review by Prison Officer Shanelle Melville against the National Insurance Board and the NIS Appeals Tribunal, which refused her injury and disablement benefit claims as out of time. Melville was injured on duty in July 2015 but her injury-benefit form was incomplete because her employer never completed the required employer's section. The Court held the regulations gave it no discretion to relieve her, even though the fault lay with the employer, and dismissed the claim with $30,100 in costs. In a pointed postscript, the judge urged lawmakers to reform provisions that penalise insured workers for their employer's default. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: NIS benefit time limits run against the insured even when the employer causes delay
Judges may flag harsh legislation for reform via postscript
outcome: Judicial review dismissed; costs $15,050 to each defendant ($30,100 total)
osint_notes: Judicial review of NIBTT benefit refusal

social_headline: Family bid to unravel D'Abadie land settlement struck out as abuse of process
social_post: Justice Westmin James has struck out a claim by Hemant Danpath and Rampatie Ramkissoon seeking to set aside a January 2022 consent order over property at Piarco Old Road, D'Abadie. They alleged their former attorney misled them into surrendering interests worth over $500,000 for just $10,000 while also owing $54,000 in costs. Their relative Sookdeo Danpath produced their signed instructions authorising the very settlement. The Court held that dissatisfaction with one's own attorney's advice is not a basis to reopen a perfected consent order, and the proceedings were a manifestly groundless abuse of process, struck out with $7,700 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A perfected consent order can rarely be set aside absent fraud or duress
Complaints about your own lawyer's advice will not reopen a settlement
outcome: Statement of case struck out; claim dismissed as abuse of process; costs $7,700
osint_notes: Family land settlement dispute with figures; D'Abadie property

social_headline: Man acquitted of rape but convicted of stealing ex-partner's phone
social_post: Justice Nalini Singh has delivered a split verdict in the trial of Iran Williams, charged with rape and larceny arising from an April 2025 incident involving a former partner. On the rape count, the judge found reasonable doubt, citing significant omissions and inconsistencies in the complainant's account, notably a grocery trip absent from her first statement, and the absence of independent evidence such as DNA, CCTV or phone records. Williams was acquitted of rape. On the larceny count, however, the Court was sure of guilt after Williams himself admitted taking the complainant's Samsung A05 phone, valued at $860, without permission. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Rape convictions require the court to be sure the complainant is truthful and reliable
Late-emerging material omissions can create reasonable doubt
outcome: Acquitted of rape; convicted of larceny
osint_notes: Sexual offence / larceny verdict; complainant anonymised as virtual complainant

social_headline: Car dealer ordered to repay $162,770 over botched Lexus import
social_post: Justice Robin Mohammed has ordered car dealer Sudhir Sagramsingh, trading as S. Sagramsingh Motors, to pay Air Technology Limited $162,770.60 plus interest over a mishandled 2016 Lexus NX F Sport import. Air Technology, through director Marcus Ghany, paid the dealer to source and register the UK-export vehicle for $550,000, but the dealer failed to transfer the funds promptly, leaving the company to pay an outstanding balance directly to the UK supplier and take delivery itself. The Court found the dealer breached the oral agreement, awarding the debt plus $32,621 interest, though costs were reduced by half to about $19,000. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Oral agreements are enforceable but hard to prove on precise terms
Partial success can justify reducing a costs award

social_headline: Woman wrongly arrested in 2019 Carapichaima raid awarded $170,000
social_post: Master Rishma Ramrattan has awarded Amirah Khan $170,300 for her wrongful arrest in the November 2019 police raid at her family's Carapichaima home. Officers in black scaled the wall, claimed to find ammunition and marijuana in an outside box, and charged the family with firearm, ammunition and marijuana possession; the DPP discontinued all charges within weeks. Khan, who suffered a panic attack in custody and was treated at a health centre, received $90,000 for false imprisonment, $80,000 for malicious prosecution, and $300 for medication, plus interest and costs. This is one of three related family claims from the same raid. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Aggravated damages reflect the trauma of an unlawful arrest
Only documented special damages are recoverable

social_headline: Three more Khan family members compensated over 2019 Carapichaima raid
social_post: Master Rishma Ramrattan has awarded Mustapha Khan, Jennifer Khan and Kemilly Garib-Khan $75,000 each for malicious prosecution following the November 2019 police raid at their Carapichaima home. Unlike relatives arrested that night, these three were charged with firearm, ammunition and marijuana possession but not arrested; the DPP discontinued all charges within weeks. The Court declined to award exemplary damages, finding insufficient evidence of oppressive conduct against them specifically, but allowed aggravated general damages, plus interest and costs. Special-damages claims for legal fees failed for lack of documentary proof. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Malicious prosecution is actionable even without arrest
Exemplary damages require clear evidence of oppressive State conduct
outcome: Damages: $75,000 each for malicious prosecution; special damages refused; interest and costs
osint_notes: Third of three related Khan-family claims from the 2019 Carapichaima raid

title: In re an application without notice by the Claimant for Judicial Review under Part 56 of the Civil Proceedings Rules (1998) and the Judicial Review Act Chapter 7:08.

financial_figures.what_it_is: Compensatory damages

social_headline: Court orders Cadet Force to promote passed-over captain to Major
social_post: Justice Margaret Mohammed has ruled that the Trinidad and Tobago Cadet Force unlawfully failed to promote Captain Derek Williams to the rank of Major, quashing the decision as unreasonable, unfair and irrational. Williams, a registered nurse who joined in 2000 and became a Battalion Second-in-Command, was ranked more senior than four of the seven captains promoted in 2023 yet was passed over without reasons. The Court found breaches of his constitutional rights to equality and protection of the law, ordered his promotion with effect from August 2023 with all arrears, and awarded $50,000 compensatory and $50,000 vindicatory damages, registering its disapproval of the Commandant's conduct. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Promotion decisions must follow standing orders and give reasons
Constitutional breaches by public bodies can attract vindicatory damages
outcome: Judicial review granted; promotion to Major ordered with arrears; $100,000 total damages; costs
osint_notes: Judicial review of Cadet Force promotion decision

social_headline: HDC wins San Fernando property fight; 1946 acquisition title upheld
social_post: The Court of Appeal has dismissed an appeal by Joy Creese and Selwyn Burke over ownership of No. 90 Coffee Street, San Fernando. They claimed title through a 2013 Deed of Assent tracing to their late father Oscar Burke, but the Trinidad and Tobago Housing Development Corporation relied on a 1946 compulsory acquisition by its predecessor, the National Housing Authority. Justice Mark Mohammed, with the Chief Justice and Wilson JA agreeing, held the 1946 acquisition vested absolute title in the State, that non-payment of compensation did not create a reversion or trust, and that no resulting trust arose. The appellants' 2013 deed was correctly declared null and void. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Statutory compulsory acquisition vests absolute title in the State
Unpaid compensation is a statutory right, not a condition of vesting
outcome: Appeal dismissed; HDC's title affirmed; appellants' 2013 deed void
osint_notes: HDC (state housing corporation) property dispute; San Fernando; compulsory acquisition

social_headline: Coast Guard captain partly wins case after his vessel ran aground
social_post: Justice Karen Reid has partly upheld a claim by Lieutenant Commander Torinio Tracey, a 22-year Trinidad and Tobago Coast Guard officer, over the fallout from his vessel TTS Port of Spain CG 41 running aground near Herine Bay in February 2022. Tracey argued his commanding officer condoned the alleged offences before he was charged and remanded for court-martial. The Court lifted the stay on the court-martial, rejecting his condonation and protection-of-law arguments, but found the seizure and 13-month detention of his personal firearm breached his constitutional right to enjoyment of property, awarding $20,000 damages and 50% costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Unlawful seizure of property can breach section 4(a) constitutional rights
A court-martial stay will be lifted where condonation is not established
outcome: Court-martial stay lifted; firearm-seizure breach found; $20,000 damages; 50% costs
osint_notes: Coast Guard court-martial / constitutional claim

social_headline: Estranged son named to manage mother's affairs in dementia case
social_post: Justice Avason Quinlan-Williams has appointed Robert Benoit as sole Committee to manage the property and affairs of his mother, Martha Baptiste, who suffers from a major neuro-cognitive disorder likely Alzheimer's or fronto-temporal dementia, per psychiatrist Professor Gerard Hutchinson. Baptiste's two estranged children both sought control; the Court found a joint committee unworkable given their discord, and preferred the son's proposed care arrangements. The judge found the applicant daughter, Teresa Benoit-Noel, not credible on material matters. The Committee must file six-monthly financial and medical reports on the patient. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Committee appointments turn on the patient's best interests, not sibling rivalry
Courts require regular financial and medical accountability from a Committee
outcome: Interested party (son) appointed sole Committee; six-monthly reporting ordered

social_headline: Tobago golf-course land sale voided over forged powers of attorney
social_post: Justice Margaret Mohammed has set aside the fraudulent 2018 sale of Anthony Romain's property at Lot 26, Mt Irvine Estate 'Golf Course', Tobago. Romain said his friend Stephen Gellineau induced him to sign powers of attorney, then used void POAs to sell the land, with two $585,000 cheques paid to the second defendant and to Mass Contracting Company Limited, a firm Gellineau directed, rather than to Romain, and at a reduced price. The Court declared the POAs and the sale void, ordered the deed expunged, and found purchasers Naresh Persad and Sushilla Maharaj liable for fraud, conspiracy to injure and unjust enrichment, with damages to be assessed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Sales under forged or void powers of attorney can be set aside
Purchasers who ignore red flags in a deal can be liable for conspiracy and fraud

social_headline: Tobago man's police-framing and theft claim dismissed for lack of proof
social_post: Justice Ricky Rahim has dismissed a claim by Kevin Mark, who alleged Tobago police framed him for marijuana possession in 2017 and stole $2,500 from his car during his arrest at Buccoo. Although a magistrate acquitted him in 2018, the Court in this civil claim did not believe his account, finding it implausible and noting he failed to call available witnesses, drawing adverse inferences. The judge held Mark did not discharge his burden to prove absence of reasonable and probable cause, nor that police took the money. His claims for false imprisonment, malicious prosecution and conversion all failed, with $14,000 costs against him. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Civil claimants bear the burden of proving malicious prosecution
Failure to call key witnesses invites adverse inferences
outcome: Claim dismissed; costs $14,000 against claimant
osint_notes: Malicious prosecution claim dismissed; narcotics charge background

social_headline: Tobago father-in-law wins possession of apartment from estranged daughter-in-law
social_post: Justice Robin Mohammed has granted Clifton Moses possession of a one-bedroom apartment at No. 8B Pascal Village Street, Lambeau, Tobago, occupied by his daughter-in-law Yvonne Duke Moses. Moses, the registered owner, had given her personal permission to live there in 2013, but after the relationship deteriorated he served a Notice to Quit in 2017. The Court found she was a bare licensee whose licence was lawfully revoked, rejecting her claim to a matrimonial-home interest, and ordered her to vacate within 30 days. Her counterclaim was dismissed, with costs of $14,000 and $7,000. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A bare licence to occupy family property can be revoked by notice
Occupation as a family member does not create a proprietary interest
outcome: Possession granted to claimant; defendant to vacate in 30 days; counterclaim dismissed; costs $21,000
osint_notes: Family property/licence dispute in Tobago

title: Mohammed, Andile, A. v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

social_headline: Officer shot in gun raid loses negligence claim against police service
social_post: Justice Westmin James has dismissed a negligence and employer's-liability claim by Police Constable Andile Ansar Mohammed, shot in the right leg during an October 2020 search-warrant raid for firearms at John Street, Chaguanas, when officers came under gunfire. Mohammed, attached to the Robbery Unit and assisting the Gang Unit, said the TTPS failed to provide a safe system of work, specialist training and protective gear such as ballistic shin guards. The Court held the injury flowed from the inherent dangers of armed policing rather than actionable negligence, and dismissed the claim, but made no order as to costs given his good-faith public service. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Employers are not liable for every inherent risk of dangerous work
Good-faith public-interest litigation can justify no order as to costs
outcome: Claim dismissed; no order as to costs
osint_notes: Police officer injury / OSH Act negligence claim; firearms raid context

social_headline: Court revives time-barred claim over 2014 highway crash that killed a family of three
social_post: Justice Frank Seepersad has allowed a statute-barred claim to proceed over the July 2014 Uriah Butler Highway crash that killed Mahadeo, Nanda and 13-year-old Nyron Ramcharan. Their son and brother Nigel Ramcharan, then 12, sued B.K. Holdings Limited, driver David Diaz and insurer Gulf Insurance Limited, alleging their negligence contributed to the collision in which another vehicle became airborne and landed on the family's car. The defendants sought to strike out the claim as filed outside the four-year limit, but the Court exercised its discretion to disapply the limitation period. Notably, the judge warned against any abdication of judicial decision-making to artificial intelligence or research staff. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts have discretion to disapply limitation periods in personal-injury/fatal claims
Judges retain a duty to decide cases personally, not defer to AI or staff
outcome: Striking-out application dismissed; limitation period disapplied; claim to proceed

social_headline: Taxi driver jailed for Couva kidnapping-robbery of commuter
social_post: Justice Lisa Ramsumair-Hinds has sentenced Navin Ramdeo to three years' imprisonment for the June 2024 kidnapping and armed robbery of Richard Ramkissoon, who was waiting for a taxi to work at Isaac Junction, Couva. Ramdeo picked up two men who, with a firearm, robbed the victim of his phone, headset, bank card and even a bowl of food, before dumping him on a roadway. Ramdeo accepted a Maximum Sentence Indication of three years and pleaded guilty; kidnapping and robbery-with-violence sentences of three years each will run concurrently from 7 July 2026. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A Maximum Sentence Indication caps the sentence once accepted
Guilty pleas earn a one-third discount
outcome: Convicted on guilty plea; 3 years each for kidnapping and robbery with violence, concurrent

social_headline: Home buyers win flooding misrepresentation case but only nominal damages
social_post: Justice Robin Mohammed has found that vendors Anthony and Jocelyn Chung misrepresented the flood history of their Mausica home when selling it to Joseph Berment-McDowald and Pui-Sum Au for $2 million in December 2016. The buyers said they were assured the house had never flooded inside; it later flooded in 2018 and 2022. The Court entered judgment for misrepresentation under the Misrepresentation Act, but awarded only $25,000 nominal damages plus interest, finding the buyers had largely failed to prove their quantified losses. Costs were awarded at 60% of the prescribed scale. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Pre-contract assurances about property can ground misrepresentation liability
Winning on liability means little without proof of quantifiable loss
outcome: Judgment for claimants on misrepresentation; $25,000 nominal damages plus interest; 60% costs

social_headline: Court faults Police Commissioner over secrecy on officers' transfers
social_post: Justice Robin Mohammed has ruled in favour of two serving police officers, Corporal Claudette Fermine and her son Constable Kareem Fermine, who were abruptly transferred out of specialist units in November 2023 without the required notice or reasons. When they sought the reasons under the Freedom of Information Act, the Commissioner of Police refused, calling the document an internal working paper 'of disinterest to the public.' The Court found the refusal contrary to law, based on an irrelevant consideration and a failure to weigh the public-interest override, and ordered the Commissioner to reconsider the FOIA requests within 35 days, with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: FOIA refusals must properly weigh the public-interest override
Public authorities must give reasons and findings when refusing access
outcome: Judicial review granted; Commissioner ordered to reconsider FOIA requests within 35 days; costs
osint_notes: Freedom of Information Act / police transfer dispute

social_headline: Caretaker loses claim to late pharmacist's Mt. Irvine home
social_post: Justice Jacqueline Wilson has dismissed a proprietary-estoppel claim by Cheryl Hepburn, the live-in companion and caretaker of the late pharmacist Annabel Eleanor Dove, over her home at No. 21 Gleneagles Drive, Mt. Irvine, Tobago. Hepburn said Dove repeatedly assured her she could live there for life and that 'no one can put you out.' But the Court found that a 2015 Caretaker Agreement Hepburn signed with Dove's sister and administratrix, Theresa Nwosu, was inconsistent with any life interest, and that her six-year silence undermined the claim. Hepburn's claim and bid to revoke the grant of administration failed; she must deliver possession by 31 July 2026. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Signing a terminable caretaker agreement can defeat a proprietary-estoppel claim
Long delay in asserting a right undermines claims of a promised interest
outcome: Claim dismissed; claimant to deliver possession by 31 July 2026; costs $14,000
osint_notes: Estate/proprietary estoppel over named Mt. Irvine Tobago property; overseas administratrix

social_headline: Tabaquite land fight turns on decades-old tenancy and limitation clock
social_post: Justice Devindra Rampersad has resolved a long-running dispute over a residential lot and about three acres of agricultural land at Church Street, Tabaquite, once held by British Union Oil Company and later acquired by TECU Credit Union. Krishna Harricharan, whose family held a tenancy dating from 1945, argued that with no rent paid since 1994 and no recovery step for over 16 years, the registered owners, the Guialdo and Kennedy family, had lost their title under the Real Property Limitation Act. The defendants counterclaimed for possession, contending time only ran from 2009 after a 2008 notice to quit. The judgment turns on the tenancy structure and effect of that notice. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: When limitation time starts running depends on tenancy structure and notices to quit
Historic tenancy records are decisive in agricultural land disputes
outcome: Judgment delivered on possession/ownership turning on limitation and tenancy analysis

title: In re an application for leave to apply for Judicial Review pursuant to Part 56.3 of the Civil Proceedings Rules, 1998 (as amended) and pursuant to Section 6(1) of the Judicial Review Act Chap. 7:08.

social_headline: Appeal Court partly backs importer in customs back-duty dispute
social_post: The Court of Appeal has partly allowed an appeal by Trinmesh Limited against the Comptroller of Customs and Excise over a post-clearance audit demanding additional duties on imported steel and wire rods. Justice Rahim, with Rajkumar and Wilson JJA, held that penalty provisions inserted by Act No. 2 of 2018 could not operate retrospectively, and that a request for Mill Test Certificates on four 2014-2015 entries was made in excess of jurisdiction and was null and void. However, the Court upheld the Comptroller's power to verify classification and confirmed additional-duty provisions applied to several later wire-rod entries. The appeal was allowed in part. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Customs penalty amendments do not apply retrospectively absent clear wording
The Comptroller may verify classification but only within statutory limits
outcome: Appeal allowed in part; some customs demands upheld, others declared null and void

social_headline: 'Bandit' Facebook post was defamatory but honest, so mosquito officer's claim fails
social_post: Justice Robin Mohammed has dismissed a defamation claim by Vibert Small, a Ministry of Health Insect Vector Control officer, over a 2019 Facebook post by Sharon Meighoo-Gildharry that branded him 'a bandit pretending to spray for mosquitos.' Small was doing legitimate dengue-control work in Westmoorings when someone photographed his ID; the post was shared over 4,900 times and reposted by outlets including LoopTT and the Trinidad Express, and his son was bullied at school. The Court found the post defamatory and harmful but held it was made on an occasion of qualified privilege without malice, so the claim failed. Small must pay $14,000 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Qualified privilege can defeat a defamation claim absent proof of malice
Fact-check before posting: honest but false posts can still cause lasting harm
outcome: Claim dismissed (qualified privilege, no malice); costs $14,000
osint_notes: Social-media defamation; named parties; posts amplified by LoopTT and Trinidad Express

social_headline: Tobago man gets 9 years for shooting and illegal gun possession
social_post: Justice Lisa Ramsumair-Hinds has sentenced Nkosi Smart to nine years' imprisonment after he pleaded guilty mid-trial to wounding Davion Thomas with intent and to possession of a firearm and ammunition, arising from a January 2025 incident at Carnbee, Tobago. The judge noted Parliament's steadily rising maximum penalties for gun crimes, setting a 15-year starting point before adjustments and a one-fifth guilty-plea discount, then crediting time already served. The three sentences of nine years each will run concurrently at hard labour from the date of sentencing. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Rising statutory maxima signal Parliament's intent to deprecate gun crime
A mid-trial guilty plea attracts a reduced (one-fifth) discount
outcome: Convicted on guilty plea; 9 years each (concurrent) for wounding with intent and firearm/ammunition possession

social_headline: Marina worker wins ~$198,000 for five-year malicious firearm prosecution
social_post: Master Rishma Ramrattan has awarded Joseph Paul, a businessman and Captain Mike's Marina employee, about $198,536 in damages for malicious prosecution. Paul was arrested in January 2013 after a boating accident, held in custody for four days in what the Court described as unsanitary and inhumane conditions, and charged with firearm possession without a licence; police also seized USD9,000, later ordered returned. The prosecution dragged on for five years before being discharged in 2018 for want of prosecution. With default judgment already entered, the Court awarded general damages inclusive of an aggravation uplift, plus interest and $23,268 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Prolonged prosecutions and poor detention conditions justify aggravated damages
Exemplary and special damages require specific proof
outcome: Damages assessed: ~$198,536 general (incl aggravation) plus costs $23,268

social_headline: TSTT not liable for crash caused by cable a third party knocked down
social_post: Justice Westmin James has dismissed a negligence claim against Telecommunications Services of Trinidad and Tobago (TSTT) by motorist Anthony Williams, whose car overturned on Erin Road, Buenos Ayres, in September 2020 after hooking a fallen telecommunications cable. TSTT's evidence was that an unidentified vehicle had struck its pole and cables shortly before, and that it responded once notified. The Court found Williams failed to prove TSTT had actual or constructive notice of the hazard before the accident, so no breach of duty was established, and dismissed the claim. Given TSTT's infrastructure was directly involved, however, the judge made no order as to costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Utility owners are not liable without notice of a hazard created by a third party
Courts may decline costs where a claim, though failed, was reasonably brought
outcome: Claim dismissed; no order as to costs
osint_notes: Names TSTT in a road-traffic negligence claim over fallen cables

social_headline: Prisoner granted leave to challenge transfer to military barracks and 'bag over head' rule
social_post: Justice Westmin James has granted Earl Richards leave to pursue judicial review over his July 2025 transfer from the Maximum-Security Prison, Arouca, to a detention facility at Teteron Barracks, Chaguaramas. Richards, held amid a declared State of Emergency and later subject to a Preventive Detention Order, alleges the transfer to a military facility not lawfully designated as a prison was unlawful, that his whereabouts were initially hidden from his attorneys, and that legal visits were conditioned on his lawyers being blindfolded and a grey nylon drawstring bag being placed over his head. He was granted leave to seek mandamus, declarations and vindicatory damages, with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Prison transfers must be lawfully authorised and respect access to counsel
Leave for judicial review requires only an arguable case
outcome: Leave to apply for judicial review granted; claimant to file fixed date claim; costs to applicant

social_headline: Woodbrook bouncer wins $103,000 after officer assaulted and framed her over cover charge
social_post: Justice Margaret Mohammed has awarded nightclub bouncer Andrea Phillips $103,000 after finding an off-duty officer assaulted and then wrongly charged her during a 2017 cover-charge dispute at the Stumbling Night Club on Ariapita Avenue, Woodbrook. Phillips, collecting entry fees, blocked PC Nicholas Holder when he tried to enter without paying; the Court accepted that Holder struck her, splitting her lip, and that police refused to take her report and instead charged her with resisting arrest, obstruction and obscene language. Those charges were dismissed in 2019. The judge awarded $83,000 general damages with aggravation and $20,000 exemplary damages for Holder's oppressive conduct as a State agent. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Exemplary damages punish oppressive conduct by State agents
Police who concoct charges expose the State to aggravated and exemplary awards

Ishmael, Jaraad and Shahnaaz Abdool, owners of a 464.9-square-metre parcel, said their neighbours Jodhan and Baigentee Ramsumair diverted a drain around 2009 and built an apartment, shed and wall encroaching 59.9 square metres onto their land. Carl Morris says he agreed to buy the property from Keith Beckles for $1 million in 2011 and paid $628,000 before financial difficulties stopped him, plus $100,000 in renovations. social_headline: Man jailed nearly 9 years for fatal Curepe bar stabbing over a drink
social_post: Justice Lisa Ramsumair-Hinds has sentenced Kerwine St. Rose, known as 'Packet' and 'Strongy', to 8 years, 10 months and 9 days for the manslaughter of Matthew Mungaldeen, called 'Big Matthew' or 'Bolo', at Bleu's Bar in Curepe in August 2024. Originally charged with murder, St. Rose pleaded guilty to manslaughter on the basis of provocation after being repeatedly refused a share of a bottle of puncheon rum during a birthday lime and told to leave. He stabbed Mungaldeen in the cheek with a pocket knife, causing his death, then surrendered at a police station. The judge accepted his remorse and applied a two-fifths plea discount. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Provocation can reduce murder to manslaughter but still attracts substantial jail time
Genuine remorse and an early plea can earn an enhanced sentencing discount
outcome: Convicted of manslaughter (provocation); sentenced to 8 years 10 months 9 days at hard labour
osint_notes: Manslaughter sentencing; bar stabbing over rum dispute
